We, Monday Niters, are still recovering from the devastating loss to Oakland, two weeks ago out at Arrowhead stadium. We've replayed it all a thousand times and still have not resolved how a team can be up 17 points, at home, and still lose. We have also pondered who the next kicker will be...hopefully, someone who won't kick barefooted. More important, we hope that all of the positions will be evaluated, not just special teams. The offense, and particularly the quarterback position, needs further review. And the defense, especially the cornerbacks, needs some new faces.

And, we are all avoiding Ike Gardner. That's because he, Paul, and Ed all picked Oakland to win. What's more Ike has been teasing us throughout the year that this team would fold, especially as the season moved toward the play-offs. Sad to say...but Ike was right, as we all witnessed against Seattle and Oakland. So really, we don't have much to say right now about the Chiefs. Thus, we will slowly turn our attention to the play-offs. Dallas is out. Miami hangs on. Tampa Bay's defense makes them a contender. And Minnesota and St. Louis are ready to meet and see who's who in the National Football Conference. Tennessee got a big break. And it is all shaping up to be an interesting arrangement.

Bottom line, we are going to skip this week's predictions and wait until next week when the picture is a little clearer and there are fewer teams in the race to Atlanta (January 30 Super Bowl). It is interesting that two African American coaches could pair up for the National Football Conference championship game next week (Dennis Green of Minnesota and Tony Dungy of Tampa Bay). See you next week with our predictions.
Thanks for going along on our odyssey."

*I played football in high school for the love of the game. After the pride I had in my school and my team. After that, I tried my hand at semi-professional ball. We played and traveled under the most difficult conditions i.e. no sponsors, no insurance. If someone got hurt, we would pass the hat. The most I ever made per game was $35. Those days are gone. Players today can and do make megabucks. While football is a business, it is still a game. When the love of the game is diminished, winners become losers. Wake up Chiefs and the NFL.""*

**A LETTER TO SANTA**. . . Dear Santa, I know this is your busy season and I will try to make it short and to the point. As a long-time Chiefs' fan, I know you know that there are several things they need. And, since you seem to be the only one left to ask, I want to share their needs with you. Forget about my personal list. Just do something for this team of ours. First off, find them a quarterback, one that can run, pass, and lead. And while you are at it, get us a running back, one that can catch and run. And gain at least 1,000 yards in a long 16 game season. Oh yes, we have five running backs but we really just need one good one. Speaking of running back, get us someone who can return kicks for more than 15 yards. I see other teams running kicks for back for 50 and 60 yards and quite frankly, Santa, I am quite envious. Speaking of envy, I really get mad when I see how our once heralded defense has fallen. Once, we had Derrick and Neil and a whole lot of respect. Now, teams laugh at us and know that the defense really sucks. It's just a shame the way we screwed up in front of the national television audience last Monday. I will never understand why we threw a last second pass for five yards and we need to throw for a touchdown. Perhaps, the coaches saw something 40 million people didn't. Speaking of coaches, try to give them what they are asking for 100. For the Lord knows, they need some help. While I am not sure what Lamar Hunt and Carl Peterson are going to do about their future here in Kansas City, just whatever you think will be okay. Finally, Santa, you have been so good to us, but it's been a long time since we got any good gifts for our football team. People are calling them ""the Cheaps"" and I don't like it. If you took care of Oakland, I'm sure you can take care of us. Thanks Santa. I'll be home all during the holiday season, for again, the Chiefs won't be going anywhere.

column1: Reminiscing, that's what the Monday Niters did over to Ed West's house, this past Monday night. To help everyone ""look back"", there was an author present, who was writing a book on ""Monday Night Football"". Overall, the reminiscing was actually fun, for it had been a long time since the guys had just sit down and looked back. Paul Booker began to nod. Of all the Monday night televised games, the Kansas City-Buffalo game, here in 1981, was considered ""the best"". That October night, the Chiefs actually beat the Marv Levy-coached, Super Bowl-bound Buffalo Bills, 33-6. Then someone remembered the announcer trio, consisting of Howard Cosell, Dandy Don Meredith and Frank Gifford. Cosell was praised for his supportive relationship with Muhummad Ali. Booker is fully asleep by now. Conversation switched to how African Americans in the corporate workplace, must be extra cautious, especially, when they express their dissatisfaction with something. Their remarks are usually over-interpreted to mean that violence is likely to follow. Then conversation switched again. When Charles Allen and Ed West host Monday Nite Football, they actually do their own cooking. Braggarts (the rest of us need all the help we can get). Then, Greenfield, a realtor, was praised for guiding our group to become NAACP Life members. By now, Booker is snoring quite loudly, sprawled out in Ed West's gadget-filled message chair. And as usual, the argu-ment over how much CALL papers cost in 1940. Bubba Cleaver kindly recounts how and why he became a member of this garrulous group. Ollie Hubbard thinks he is not wicked, as he slips away. Then the conversation again goes awry, in several different directions. Reminiscing, while it is good for the soul, can get out of hand. Then, soft-spoken Carl Clark thanks the group for its participation especially in the Riwers/Harvester's partnership. Too much to keep up with. Especially when there are ten men, all talking at the same time, all on different subjects. That Paul Booker has slept through the entire football game. How could he with all this noise? And the author, who came to get information for his upcoming book, put up his note pad. And, he just gave up and joined in the reminiscing. So much for the book."

column1: Ollie Hubbard is one of the more colorful Monday Niters. Having had a barber for a father and graduating from the old R.T. Coles school has allowed Ollie to rack up thousands of acquaintances and friends around Kansas City.

He reads the obituary religiously and attends as many funerals as he can. And his telephone rings night and day, every day. “Big Hub” flat out knows everybody. That knowledge distinguishes him as a top Lincoln University, alumnus. Ollie used to attend Homecoming festivities every year, for about 30 consecutive years, never missing and always first to arrive in Jefferson City.

As for his involvement in the Monday Nite Footballers, Ollie is most noted for his humorous, colorful comments, when predicting the Chiefs’ football games. Freely borrowing from the “blues”, Ollie warns the Chiefs not to “make their move too soon.” He reminds the Chiefs of that day against the Green Bay Packers in the first Super Bowl.

Even though Otis Taylor caught an over-the-shoulder pass for a touchdown, the Chiefs still lost. Dotan cautiously says that the Chiefs are off to a good start. And that there are still 13 games remaining. Again, the Chiefs should not “make their move too soon.”

**Sports Editor Note:** Either the waters have parted over the Missouri river, a once proud defense has risen from the dead or “the Priest” has walked on sacred turf. Whatever the case, a miracle has taken place. One that you haven't seen in decades. Our cantankerous friend, “Ike,” has picked the Chiefs for the third consecutive week. Had he picked the Chiefs in the season opener, ol’ Ike would be tied with the rest of the Chiefs’ faithful Monday Niters. Of course, ol’ Ike either doesn't believe in miracles or he simply has a problem with our boys at One Arrowhead Drive. Well, miracles do happen and you’re looking at one right now. Way to go Ike. Don't lose your persona."

column1: Ike Gardner and Paul Booker are just plain haters. This anti-Chiefs duo has expanded and now includes the likes of Carl Clark, Ollie Hubbard, and the highly-respected Bubba Cleaver. I call these rebels, ""Ike and the Haters"". While it is true that the Chiefs are 0-3, these haters continue to believe that the Chiefs are just doomed. But the truth is they said the same thing last year, when the Chiefs went 9-0 and eventually onto the playoffs.

But, Ike is well-known for his rugged individualisms. In fact, Lura Anderson hosted a surprise birthday party for Ike. He turned about 85 last Sunday. Every-body here commented on Ike's widely-known cantankerousness. The 40-odd guests traded stories about Ike, ranging from putting ""do not disturb"" signs on his hospital door to driving from St. Louis in a hard-driving snow, scaring Charles and Carl to death. Some referred to Ike as a ""gentle giant"". While I respect the person who said this hughely, we have yet to see either gentle or giant in Ike.

Bottom line, Ike hates everything. And, this haterness is spreading to others. Pretty soon, everybody will be hating our Chiefs and there will be no one to be found, when they make it to the playoffs (only three teams out of 168 have ever started 0-3 and made it to the playoffs)."

*“In spite of the losses, the Chiefs really are a pretty good foot-ball team, especially the players themselves. However, the coaching sucks. Gunther is more like a cheerleader than an NFL coach. Face it y'all, he should be replaced.”*

**SUPERFICIAL SUCCESS FOR CHIEFS** . . . Okay. Okay. We're under the Gun(ther) and we are not going to make the playoffs. But, the Chiefs did beat the Super Bowl Champions, the St. Louis Rams. And if you stretch it just a little, the Chiefs did bury ""Marty Ball"" and assembled a surprising passing offense. But, all that is so superficial. . . doesn't mean a thing. Our sure-footed defense became lame and mistake-filled. Even though the running-back-by-committee system was supposed to be dead, it was still in force last Sunday when they lost to Buffalo. And, while Grbac has amassed large passing statistics, he still will throw an interception at the most inappropriate time. And to make matters worse, he never became the leader the national press tout-ed him to be. And, Jimmie Raye, former quarterback and now offensive coordinator . . . was at best mediocre with his play-calling. All of this means that the Chiefs will be lucky to finish the season at 8-8 and won't be playing anywhere in January. Of course, all of us have our reasons to explain the ""up and down"" vicissitudinary season for our Chiefs. But, like all hard times, we get use to it, or we stop talking about it, or we redi-rect and re-channel our energies and interests. Anybody for chess?

Marylyn and Carl Clark, attended the renown Michigan-Ohio State football game in Columbus, Ohio, this past week-end. Shawn Edwards, host of the Hot 103's ""Generation Rap"" and Fox 4's film critic, was the hon-oree at a birthday party, given by G-Rap crew members. According to a Kauffman Foundation study, drug use among youth in Jackson County is generally lower than the metropolitan area as a whole. COMBAT sponsors 38 drug prevention programs.

column1: Okay, Chiefs' fans everywhere seem to be recovering nicely from last week's reality check (the embarrassing beating from the Denver Broncos). Now, come on, everybody in the country knew that the Chiefs would not go 16-0 (except maybe Charles Allen). And we are not that far removed from the days when the Chiefs would just blow everything. And, of course, we knew the offense would fail one day, and openly demonstrate just how weak our run defense really is. And it all happened, out in Denver, last Sunday. The Broncos beat the Chiefs so badly in the second half, when the Chiefs' highly-touted offense just plain quit. The Chiefs performed just miserably, as they have been known to do. The mistakes they made were unexplainably dumb. Then, too, Monday Niters expected that Denver, Minnesota, and Cincinnati would be formidable opponents because of an otherwise easy schedule. So once again, the early season. They have to crank up the offense, so as to conceal the often-exposed, vulnerably sub-par defense. That means Priest will have to get more touches; and the sometimey receivers will have to catch more and run further after the catch. It's funky but it's fair. It's like that and that's the way it is. Just like Bubba, Ike, and Ed said it was. By the way, the Chiefs will clinch the AFC West by beating up on the roar-less Detroit Lions. What a difference a week can make."

column1: Each year, we change something about our organization...not just to attract new readers but to broaden our horizons. Last year, we added the Reverend Emanuel Cleaver to our ""brainiac-maniac"" football group. Listed under the moniker, Bubba, our readers immediately began to question where did this ""Bubba"" come from. Now, affectionately known as ""Rev"", and ""Mayor"", Cleaver's first nickname was given to him, by his oldest of three sisters, trying to say ""Brother"". It stuck and anybody from his home state, Texas, would certainly know who Bubba is. Besides, having the most recognized name in all of Kansas City, Cleaver is a genuine Chiefs' football fan. Like his fellow Monday Niters, Cleaver actually was an All-Star linebacker and defensive back, in the '60s, way back, in high school. He was destined for a promising college career before injuring his knee. In fact, Cleaver could write a medical journal because he has been operated on 11 times mostly for continuing knee problems. When Cleaver joined up with the Monday Niters, there was quite a bit of talk, about his presence...being a former mayor and a minister and socially interacting with three of his parishioners. When asked, Cleaver crisply states that he became a part of Monday Nite Football, just to have fun and be around a bunch of guys who like football and each other. Further, it gives him a chance to be just ""one of the guys"". Now, even though he acts down-to-earth and all, none of us would ever dare to refer to him as ""just one of the guys"". We, Monday Niters, regard Cleaver with the highest of respect. We really respect his ability to prognosticate, for he is the current leader in correct predictions. So, we just rate his football savvy, right up there with his ability to preach, his political prowess, and his God-given talent to lead wherever he goes. We all envy his unique ability to remember any conversation, even though it might have taken place months or years ago. And we are all proud to have him, as a friend to football and to us, as well."

column1: Charles Allen is, first and foremost, a well-respected dentist and is held in high esteem by both colleagues and clients, alike. Along with a loyal and capable staff, Dr. Allen has built one of the largest and best dental practices in the metropolitan area.

Even though successful, he still works hard at reducing the time it takes to get an appointment with him. And he is quick to point out that he likes to see every patient, whether it is preventative teeth-cleaning or a major denture repair.

But, that’s Charles. Loyal, trusting, professional, unrelenting. That’s exactly the way Charles is about his Chiefs. Unlike Ike, he always supports the Chiefs. In his 34 years as a Monday Niter, he has never once predicted a Chiefs loss, not even in the lean days of the recent past. One of his special qualities is his fondness for Chiefs trivia. He smiles when he remembers the Sunday afternoon when Derrick Thomas, the late Chiefs’ All-Pro linebacker, sacked Seattle’s David Krieg seven times, out at Arrowhead. In fact, he tells his 8-year-old grandson, Robert that Von Holliday and the newly-oiled Chiefs’ defense will lead Kansas City to the Super Bowl. . . If not this year, then certainly in 2004. While some of his fellow Monday Niters will disagree with Charles’ steadfastness, that doesn’t stop Charles from being loyal, trusting, professional, and unrelenting. Just like he is with everything else."

column1: These days, it is quite popular to refer to our African heritage via quotes and quotations. And after the Chiefs sixth loss in a row and a now-lost season, I can try and find an appropriate African proverb to elucidate another Chiefs' disastrous season. Get ready for the players to start saying ""we just gotta correct these mistakes"" or they will say ""we all are family and that includes everybody"". And the coaches will start admitting that their experiments didn't work.

Yes, if it was Lamar Hunt's dream to win a Super Bowl again, he must be rotating in his grave. For the current Chiefs are nowhere near Super Bowl calibre. All we can do is hope for a better tomorrow. Which reminds me of a simple old African proverb... ""What one hopes for is always better than what one has"". Now, ain't that the truth?"

column1: **Sports Editor’s Note:** Well, we knew it wouldn’t last and it didn’t. Ol’ Ike broke his record three straight weeks of picking the Chiefs. Like I said last week, Ike doesn’t believe in miracles. But that’s okay. We all knew that he was a faithless fan. Some day Ike you’ll learn. Just like the faithful Monday Niters already have.

This week, we will dedicate our words to the recently departed fellow Monday Niter, Lewis Walter Livingston. Paul Booker, Ike Gardner, and Ollie Hubbard, all knew “Uncle Walt” from his days as an educator and a co-worker at the Niles Home For Children. It was that association that led Walt to become an active part in the Monday Night Footballers.

Though a quiet man, he held his own, among those louder Monday Niters. Walt had a way of correcting misinformation, especially about all levels of football. It was if he waited for someone to make a mistake. And whammo, he would pounce on them, lightly but forcefully. In his later years, he fell victim to a series of illnesses. However, he kept in contact with Monday Niters, by even entertaining them at his assisted-living quarters, just south of Kansas City. While he wasn’t an extrovert, those of us who got to know him, knew that we had a true friend and someone who could set us straight . . and still remain friends."

*“Chiefs are a playoff team except for four flaws: Cunningham, Grbac, Schottenheimer, and Jimmy Raye.”*

Chiefs Sunday date with the Chargers – Ollie Hubbard, known for his quips of wisdom, says the Chargers will create an ""electric slide"" over the Chiefs, meaning that the Chiefs will experience their third defeat. Ol' Ike Gardner, usually a maverick against the mien, says just the opposite. Surprisingly, he believes the Chiefs will prevail with God's mercy. However so, it is still premature to talk about resignations, playoffs, and losing seasons – just yet. There hasn't been enough time to see who will step up and help the Chiefs become a winning team. But it is clear the Chiefs definitely miss Derrick Thomas. In the meantime, we wish Cunningham would stop saying Grbac is a great team asset – that alone could cost him his job.

Kudos For Northeast Middle Scholars – As a result of homework bootcamp and some tough summer school hours, 15 Northeast Middle seventh-graders are expected to maintain their good grades through their second year of middle school. These students were a part of an intensive effort by COMBAT, ARC, the Kansas City School district, SMART PARTNERS and the Northeast community. Called NIA, the program emphasizes staying in school, getting good grades, and becoming good school citizens. Starting in October, the program will admit sixth graders.

Threescore and Seven For Earl Abel – The Rev. Earl Abel was honored by his congregation for his 70th birthday anniversary. As he was presented with a portrait, he displayed a humility-filled smiled of appreciation. Rev. Abel, Palestine's only pastor for 41 years, praised his congregation for their loyalty and faith. Rev. Abel has been steadfast in his commitment to serve the Lord, developing services for senior citizens and youth.

The Chiefs demonstrated they have championship caliber talent when they defeated St. Louis. *However, they lack four key ingredients necessary to achieve a champion-ship: consistent, intelligent coaching; consistent, special teams with a quality running game; and a championship-caliber quarterback.* Grbac's excellent statistics are directly attributable to three factors: Alex-ander, Gonzales and Morris. Plus, he has completed almost as many passes to the defense as theh offense. His ineptness late in the game continues to plague the Chiefs.""

**NOTHING MATTERS SUNDAY** . . . Sunday's game will mark the NFL, halfway point, especially for our Kansas City Chiefs and their arch rivals, the Oakland Raiders. The fact that Rich Gannon is on the front cover of Sports Illustrated does not matter. Neither does the fact that the Raiders are 7-1, their best start in years. Conversely, the fact that Grbac has risen from a goat to the third-rated AFC passer is of no value. And it certainly won't matter that big-play, receiver Alexander is second in touchdowns and fourth in receiving yards. Absolutely nothing matters really – except blind fate. No one can really say who is going to be ""on"" on Sunday. Conversely, no one can safely say which team will be ""off."" As usual, Ike, Paul and Ollie say the Raiders will win it by a touchdown or better. On the contrary, the rest of us clearly believe in the Chiefs again. Thus, the outcome is or will be totally unpredictable. Of course, there will be resounding cheers and deafening boos. As can be expected, the team that scores first won't win. And the game won't be decided until the last moments. Penalties will likely plague both sides. And most of all, some instant replay will probably determine the outcome/direction of the game. Thus, lay aside your loyalties and knowledge about professional football, because it won't matter either. Just join us in watching every-body's Game of the Week. And it all will be happening Sunday afternoon between the well-balanced Oakland Raiders and our, uprising Kansas City Chiefs. (I said all that to say, the Chiefs will be 6-3 on Sunday night.)

**POTPOURRI AND OTHER STUFF** ... Ask Rev. Emanuel Cleaver what practical joke does he have planned for Ike Gardner. Ike predicted the Chiefs would lose against St. Louis (wrong); There is word that Cicely Tyson may be coming to town this weekend; Alvin Ailey is not only a well-known, dance troupe, it also offers drug prevention services through COMBAT.

column3: What if someone told you the Chiefs would win bigtime, this Sunday. And someone else said the Chiefs would win the next three games. And, still another said that Larry Johnson would just destroy the 49ers, zipping and zipping the rather fragile, San Francis- co defense. Well, that’s what the Monday Nites are telling you. End of sentence. And to demoralize, all except of Ollie, are on board with the Chiefs this week.

Really, of cantankerous Ike and Booker, the haters are anti-Chiefs. But, you notice, this time, they have joined up with the loyalists. Being fair, the haters have been right, about Herman and his Chiefs. After all they are 0-2 and not going to be going to the Super Bowl.

Having said that, this bye week has allowed [Damon] Huard to get more time with the first team and the offensive line has worked harder. That simple means that the offense will score more and Larry Johnson, in particular, will break both scoring and rushing records. It is as simple as that.

That thing that is not so simple. That is how those Monday Niters will switch on you. Just like Ike and that Booker, there will be several more joining the loyalists over the next three games. Moreover, as Herm starts coaching more and profiling less, the Chiefs will take on the hard-nosed character that the coach wants them to be. Unfortunately for Herm, the action speaks louder than words.
Welcome Ike and Booker to those of us who stick it out... through thick and thin.

column1: Does a 14-5 week spell disaster? Naw, just warming up for the perfect week! Of course it won't be this week. Tough picks for week four.
**Green Bay at Philadelphia.** No re-run for the Pack here. Eagles will soar. **Prediction:** Philadelphia
**Detroit at St Louis.** Rams airways will be busy. Lions remain winless. **Prediction:** St. Louis
**Indianapolis at N.Y. Jets.** Sorry Jets, this isn't Buffalo. Too many weapons to defend. **Prediction** Indianapolis
**Miami at Houston.** Texans could spring a surprise. COULD is the operative word! **Pre- diction:** Miami
**Dallas at Tennessee.** Stop me if you've heard this before. Tennessee is playing for a high draft pick. **Prediction:** Miami
**Cleveland at Oakland.** The one game I figured Oakland would win. NOT!! **Prediction:** Cleveland
**New England at Cincinnati.** Bengals refuse to take any prisoners. **Prediction:** Cincinnati
**Seattle at Chicago.** No Alexander, no win, no way!! Defenses will dictate outcome. **Prediction:** Chicago
**New Orleans at Carolina.** The party’s over. Carolina has regrouped. Saints won't come marching in! **Prediction:** Carolina
**Minnesota at Buffalo.** Tough one to figure. Is Minnesota for real? I think so. **Prediction:** Minnesota

column1: We’ve spent the last nine weeks, highlighting each of the Monday Niters. Now, it is time to really talk about our Kansas City Chiefs. I can now see why Hank Stram was inducted into the Hall of Fame. That was the last time, up until now, that our Chiefs were held in high esteem. You would see the late Buck Buchanan or Willie Lanier or Otis Taylor on the streets and look up to them, talk about the upcoming game and actually feel that they represented our community and the team well. That was over thirty years ago. Many of the current Chiefs’ players were not even born, yet. Over those three decades, we have really forgotten what it is like to have a team...that just might win. In fact, long time fans, like Barbara Hill, don’t know how to react, when their team is actually leading the NFL in scoring, hosting the top touchdown produc-er, and is undefeated after ten games into the season. We all are tempted to pinch our-selves and say ‘it isn’t so’. And to top all that, Trent Green actually is doing his part, by not giving the ball to the opposing team. That, alone, makes it scary to even say the “SB” words. We can talk about the Super Bowl of 1970. You know the Chiefs did play Minnesota and the score was 24-7. Otis Taylor was the star and the American Football Conference prevailed over the National Football League. Hey! Stop that! That was a long, long time, ago. This is 2003 and Tennessee and Indianapolis and Denver and New England and maybe even Miami, are in our paths. Oh, well, It was a nice thought....for now."

column1: James Nunnelly, his birth name, is truly a human chameleon. A chameleon because he adapts very well into a multitude of environments. He is a devoted worker in the Palestine Missionary Baptist church; he has distinguished himself as a noted medical administration; he is currently the outstanding czar of COMBAT; and he is one of the stars of the “Generation Rap” radio program. Most importantly he is a devout family man, as his pulchritudinous wife, his two children, Zel and Duna, and his four grandchildren, Cool Rae, and the Nunnelly boys will testify. Though not a charter member (he hates to be reminded of that), he became associated with the Monday Nite Footballers in the early '70s. He has done more singlehandedly to raise our presence in the community than all the rest of us combined. As a Chameleon of course, he has many names. Some know him as “The Commissioner” of Monday Night Football, some know him as Nun, many call him Jim, and as one of the stars of Generation Rap, he is known as “Grand Dad”. I can't print what Sandra Hubbard, who knows the real man, calls him. But what is not well known is what the old folks call him. They have known him since he was wearing diapers and running around bare-footed in Columbia, Mo. They all know him and now you know him as “Doodle Bug”. So the next time you see him in the halls of the Jackson County Courthouse, or the offices of state executives or at the 02, just give him a high five and a “Hi Doodle Bug”."

column1: Well, the big game of the season will be at Mile-High Stadium in Denver, this Sunday afternoon. No, it will not decide a championship; for it is clear that our Chiefs will eventually become the AFC West Division Champions. But, it is THE game of the week and the year. Betting experts in Las Vegas are saying Denver will win this one, in spite of the near-spotless record of the Chiefs. And so does Ike, Bubba, and Ed. Ike has been waiting for someone to agree with him about the Chiefs. And, this game is it. In addition, a garrulous Denver newspaper columnist called the Chiefs ""the biggest fraud in the NFL."" He, like Ike, thinks the Chiefs are highly overrated and have been blessed with a weak schedule. Maybe, just maybe Ike, Bubba, Ed and the Las Vegas bettors are right. With the Chiefs being vulnerable to the running game, the Monday Niteers know that Clinton Portis will tip, rip and dip through our sometimey Defense. But, the underlying assumption, supporting a Denver victory, is that there will be absolutely no controversial kick returns, at Mile-High Stadium. One will never forget what happened at Arrowhead on October 5. Dante Hall returned a kick ninety three yards for a go-ahead, game-deciding touchdown. And there will not be a sea of Red, to assist. Neither will there be great weather. From all points of view, the Chiefs should lose: But, somehow, someway, the Chiefs will rise up again and take win outright the AFC West, this Sunday afternoon. It may even take overtime, but the Chiefs will prevail. (At least, some of us believe it!)"

column1: While the Monday Niters were collecting dollars for the Kiwanis/Harvester partnership, the inevitable conversation rose about the Chiefs' tragic loss to Cincinnati, last Sunday. Some called it a poor excuse for a running game. Some even pointed to Trent Green's inability to throw under pressure. Special teams didn't show up. Everyone was trying to evade the plain truth. Cincinnati just outplayed us and the last, lace-in-the-pass, on third down, just iced it for the now division-leading, Bengals. But there is a higher ground victory, one that we all have clamored for, and should clamor for and should remember, for an awful long time. An African American coach, Marvin Lewis, applied his ""defensive genius"", and actually defeated the nation's leading NFL team. As up-close fans will attest, Lewis had been overlooked many times, for head coaching jobs, prior to the Cincinnati position. So often did he hear about his lack of offensive prowess, as the basic reason for his failure to rise in the NFL coaching ranks. So, let's not feel bad, about Kansas City's loss. Instead, let's be glad that Marvin Lewis can now, say that he beat the best team and the best coach in football. All with style and professional grace, but smiling inside and saying to himself.......""How do you feel about me now?"""

column1: When high-powered, highly-paid coaches sign on to become a head coach in the NFL, there are lots of neat little ""perks"" that find their way into a contract. For example, there are additional dollars added, when a team makes the playoffs or avoid a losing season. Well, for the Chiefs, Vermeil and company will not be in the hunt for the playoffs. But, if they win the next two games, that could mean “bigger bucks"" for the coaches. For while they would not have had a winning season, it would not be a losing season, either.

This is just a lesson in the ""small print"" of these big-time contracts. Then, too, Vermeil has some making up to do both with the fans and specifically, with Larry Johnson. This boy can run that ball, equal to or better than Priest Holmes. And Vermeil, because of some personal issues with Johnson, kept Johnson out of competition, even though he was a high-round, draft choice. And to see Johnson perform like he has lately, Vermeil has to save face and let Larry run around, through, and over Oakland. Look for Larry and the Chiefs to save Dick Vermeil's – (job). More important, a full stadium on Christmas Day will help save any threatened decrease in season ticket sales. On the other hand, the Chiefs should not expect the fans to pay for an increase in ticket prices. For really, this season has been mediocre, at best. Chiefs 40, Raiders 17.

Note: Congressman-elect Cleaver is still homeless in D.C., having failed to locate an apartment on his last trip East. Ike Gardner and Ed West, fellow Monday Niters and St. James members, are assisting Cleaver by providing contacts. Cleaver is planning to be in Washington Tuesday thru Thursday of every week and here in Kansas City Friday through Monday. Cleaver will return to Washington for swearing-in ceremonies on January 4, 2005, At that time, he hopes to surrender his homelessness card."

column1: **Note:** Tony Dungy should be on the cover of *Ebony* magazine. On top of that, he will surely become the NFL's Coach of the Year for 2005. He's been to the playoffs each of his three years with the Colts. After 10 years as a head coach, Dungy still displays that no-nonsense persona and is respected by front offices and players, alike. And further, he ought to be the next recipient of the NAACP Image Award. He may not sing like Luther, but he seldom speaks volumes. Can you hear me, now?
**ALL THE WAY, WITH EDDIE, LARRY AND DANTE**

A strange thing happened over at Ollie Hubbard's place this last Monday night. For the first time in 96 years, Ollie actually served a normal-size meal. Usually, he and his amicable wife, Sandra, prepare enough food for two armies. But, this time, they deviated from their usual feast-level portions. That's a good thing and nobody complained. Ike was back from a short illness and because of that, there was a lot of arguments and hollering back and forth.

For some reason, dissension seems to follow some people. And, of course, with the Chiefs playing Denver, there was our usual disagreement, as to what will happen, Sunday, out at Arrowhead. Ike, Booker, Carl and Ollie think that Denver will just beat the crap out of the Chiefs. However, loyalists like Greenfield, Dr. Allen and I, believe the Chiefs will squeak by. Fans will remember last year when Dante Hall electrified the Arrowhead crowd with one of his exciting run backs, kicking off the favored Broncos. Look for a repeat performance by Dante on Sunday about 5 p.m. Most important, former Bronco Eddie Kennison will have a red-letter day against his former teammates.

And because of his predictably stellar performances, Larry Johnson will again be selected AFC ""Player of the Week"" because of his rampant running. Chiefs by 3 and with that victory, the Chiefs become a playoff contender, not a pretender."

column1: We’re gathering this week over to Carl Clark’s house. Carl has every kind of electronic gadget imaginable. And Mary Lynn, Carl’s wife and business manager, will serve up some of the best food, anywhere in the country. All of us will enjoy the unmatched hospitality. These niceties will help unite us, on one accord. But Heaven knows that we are divided in our predictions. Even after intensive, counseling from his pastor, Ike Gardner has chosen the Raiders over the Chiefs. And so has his career pal, the Apostle Paul Booker. Plain and simple, they are just haters. But, the rest of us are looking for the Chiefs to do well out in the valley and shadow of death (a.k.a. the Oakland Coliseum).

On Sunday night, you should look for a flurry of screen passes from Trent Green to both Larry Johnson and Priest Holmes. Because Oakland will be prepared to stop the yard-producing, quick pitches, the Chiefs will throw the shorter, run-like passes. And there will be a new slant to the defense, as well. Look for Derrick Johnson to blitz a lot more, jumping on Oakland’s sorry quarterback from the start. Those of us faithful will be praying for the Chiefs to enlarge their territory, further adding to their seldom-held, AFC Division lead. The Chiefs have been known to stray from the path of winning. Yea, though the Chiefs will be on the West Coast, let’s pray that they will fear no evil. For most of us are with them.
Except for that Ike and Booker."

column1: They are on the job; they are at the grocery store; they hang around the barber shops; and they are even among your best friends. Bring up our rising Chiefs and they rise up, like vultures. They are called ""haters"" a.k.a. nonbelievers. They just won't accept the fact that the Chiefs are ""in the playoff hunt"". Look above and you will see that we have several ""haters"". In spite of mounting evidence to the contrary, Cantankerous Ike and that Booker still think the Chiefs are not ""for real"".

Face it, they are just ""haters"" and they would hate the sun on a cold day. But, unfortunately, they reflect the caution that we all have to apply, when talking about our Chiefs. The Chiefs tend to lose some games they should win and win the ones that they should lose. I am never sure if they are the ""worst of the best"" or the ""best of the worst"". But, come Sunday afternoon, we will find out for sure. Fighting San Diego for a playoff spot, the Chiefs will have to beat Dallas, in order to ""just hold on"". And for those of you who didn't know about Larry Johnson, we haven't heard much from you. In fact, some of us have even forgotten Priest Holmes in the process.
Look for Larry and Kennison to ""represent"" and take the Chiefs one step closer to the playoffs. Chiefs by 9!"

The “Apostle” Paul Booker is the most liked guy among the Monday Niters. He is low-key, dedicated and always avoids useless confrontations. Quietly going about his life. In fact, after leaving the Air Force, many years ago, he quietly enrolled at Lincoln university. Not having any financial help, Booker had to clean up the state office buildings, at night, just to be able to go to school in the day. Nobody knew his financial status. As he began to make friends, there at Lincoln, he never imagined that those friends would become lifelong……like Stewart Watson, Ollie Hubbard, Walt Livingston, and Charles Allen. And, quietly, he carried those friendships forward, as he became an educator in the Kansas City School District and as an evening staff worker at the Niles Home for Children. If Booker has an outspoken interest, it would be the plight of troubled youth. As home-school coordinator under Ike Gardner, it was not uncommon for him to tirelessly search for services for the young people, assigned to him. In fact, a young man approached Booker one night, at a social gathering and thanked Booker for keeping him out of prison. Now, that is Booker…at his best, helping youngsters who could go either way. Moreover, Booker’s interest in football springs from his untiring interest in troubled youth. He knows that many of the Chiefs have come from broken homes and similar and troubled beginnings. Booker is quiet, he is well-known around town. In fact, a recent Mayor appointed Booker and another Monday Niter, to inspect metropolitan area night clubs on Friday evenings. On a serious note, Booker is well-liked, respected, quiet guy, everywhere he goes."

The season has proven my original evaluation of the Chiefs. Outstanding talent, poorly coached. It is time for Carl Peterson to clean house.""

TET-A-TETE – Strange word. And we’ll show how it fits around the current events for our Kansas City Chiefs. Now, that most of you have turned you attention to Christmas Eve and some even toward Kwanzaa, you must not forget that the Chiefs have one more game to play. Some gave up on the Chiefs after they lost unexplainably to San Diego. Some don’t even know we’re playing in the Georgia Dome, down in Atlanta on Christmas Eve Sunday. But, all of us know that there has to be some major changes made in order for our Chiefs to thrive and compete. I’m never sure whether the Chiefs are the ‘best of the worst’ or the ‘worst of the best.’ Either way, we won’t be seeing the Chiefs anymore after Sunday. That is, until next year. And that is precisely where TETE-A-TETE comes in. This word/phrase means a private, head-to-head conversation between two people. Nobody else and no interference. And that conversation has to happen between Carl Peterson, president and general manager, and Gunther Cunningham, head coach of the Chiefs. In this private conversation, the two will decide next year’s goals or next year’s coach. If Gunther wins, the talk will be how to improve on an 8-8 season. If the Chiefs lose, the conversation will take on a more chilled atmosphere. Showing Gunther the exits from Arrowhead Stadium. So, the game at Atlanta is more than a Christmas Eve football game. It will decide who wants to be the 2001 head coach. We believe Gunther will be back and the Chiefs will have stayed off another losing season. Merry Christmas, Gunther and to all of you.

column1: Ed West left the little country town of Osawatomie, Kas., in 1961, to teach here at Lincoln High school. Although somewhat quiet and shy, he had been an All-Star running back at Pittsburg State and had even been invited to try out for an NFL team. After teaching for a few years, Ed accepted a management position at the now-defunct Western Electric Plant in 1966. Trained in Navy electronics, he was a perfect fit for all the intricacies of this rapidly budding industry. Even today, those unique skills have made him very popular, especially among the youngsters. Now in semi-retirement and having returned to teaching as a substitute, he is frequently called on to either build a computer or help someone unravel the complexities of owning or using a personal computer. His actual ""hands-on"" football experiences, coupled with his remarkable computer wizardry, gives Ed an exalted, special place among his fellow Monday Niters. When Ed West speaks, everybody listens. He is a walking embodiment of what he does and feels and his peers both respect and admire him. In addition, his posh, suburban home is immersed in and full of DVD's, High Definition tv's, and a host of other computer-generated gadgets. When asked how many games the Chiefs would win, Ed quickly shouts out ""at least 12."" In fact, he thinks the Chiefs may even win more, even though it is too early to talk Super Bowl talk. This guy may be from the country, but he sure is smart when it comes to football and computers and the Chiefs."

column1: There are about three things that everyone knows about Carl Clark. One, he is an avid golfer. Two, he loves and admires his family, especially the grandkids. And three, he and co-Monday Niters are always tinkering with technology, especially their computers. However, only a few know how dedicated he is to the Kansas City Chiefs Football club.

It seems he's been a Chiefs' fan forever, maybe even back to his birth at old General Hospital No. 2. Carl was one of the first persons to buy a Chiefs' season ticket, way back in 1964 when the cost was $54 (now the cost is nearly $1,000).
Rain, shine, cold, hot, or cludy, he is out at Arrowhead, usually with Marylynn, his wife of many years.

As for the most memorable moment in Chiefs' history, he remembers the day Pat Fischer, Washington cornerback, literally rode Otis Taylor into the Chiefs' end zone for a touchdown. And he talks about Chiefs' trivia, each Monday night with his football cronies.

While he is noted for his football history, his friends know him best by his ""Carlisms"", things that only Carl Clark says. Once he was asked what the Chiefs would have to do to win the game. Carl aptly replied, ""score more points""."

CHIEFS WILL PREVAIL OVER SEATTLE . . . Elvis Grbac, Kansas City's quarterback, has been blessed with some gifted receivers – Sylvester Morris, Derrick Alexander, Tony Gonzalez and even Kevin Lockett. And the good thing about last Sunday's victory over the Denver Broncos was these illustrious giants were actually utilized and Elvis actually threw to each one of them. Cease talking about running the ball and start using these receivers. The whole key to the Denver game was our willingness to pass in the last five minutes, rather than run the ball against an impossible front. Obviously, Ray Charles and Stevie Wonder can see that Elvis' disabilities are fewer when the Chiefs employ a pass/run game plan, rather than a single-minded running game. Right when all hope was leaving, the Chiefs went airborne. And it worked. Look for a tough defense and a novel passing game to welcome a national television audience to Arrowhead.

COMBAT OUTCOMES . . . Agencies funded by COMBAT are making an impact on families and children, affected by substance abuse. These are the findings offered by such agencies as Alvin Ailey, Heart of America Family Services, Drug Court, Kansas City Police department, 49-63 Neighborhood Association, and some 60 others. Joyce Lyn Tucker Burg and Velda Cook have the primary responsibility of gathering and presenting information about the performance outcomes. If you desire more information about COMBAT or desire a speaker, contact Velda or Joycelyn at (816) 881-3114.

HARDLINE AT THE OLYMPICS . . . There is no excuse – use any kind of drugs and you are out of the Olympics. That's what the ruling committee has told every athlete, every nation, and the world. This drug thing has cast a gloom over the constant smile, usually worn by Marion Jones, reportedly the fastest woman in the world. Her husband, not a participant in the Games, tested positive for some kind of drug use. Unfortunate, but even taking cough medicine is not an acceptable excuse. No drugs, period.

column1: **Sports Editor's Note:** Now that we know that Ike has fallen behind in the race for the Monday Niters championship, maybe we need to let Ike know how foolish it is to constantly go against our Chiefs. Of course, ol' Ike will be ol' Ike and that's okay. There aren't too many of us that want him to win anyway. And **THE CALL** is included! Well Ike, now that you're two games behind, how does it feel? I'm sure, rotten. Gut-wrenching sickness. Light-headed. Oh, do you get to sit in your ""kingly chair"" now that you've lost your battle? Yea, right. The King has been dethroned! All power to the victors! See Ike if you had listened to me and the rest of the Monday Niters you wouldn't be in this mess. But no, you allowed your ego to get in the way and now look you're foolishly sitting in last place. Oh, I thought you said the Chiefs wouldn't be this good. Not on your life, Ike. You're wrong, the rest of us are right and we're all gloating over your last-place standing. See Ike, if only you had listened. . .

**Lloyd Greenfield** . . . quiet, unassuming, quite a thinker. Graduated from Lincoln High school, right after being transferred from the now-defunct R.T. Coles. Spent 33 years as a Kansas City, Mo., firefighter. Acted in 20 plays, dating back to high school drama club. Owns a collection of about 60 model cars, reflecting his whole lifetime. Now, a fully-licensed real estate broker. Avid Chiefs' fan, but has voted against the Chiefs, not more than a half dozen times. And it is in being a Chiefs' fan that vaulted him into the pathway of the Monday Nite Footballers. Lloyd was a natural but opposite fit for the vociferous and highly-opinionated Football club. Lloyd joined the group shortly after he traveled all the way to Canton, Ohio, to see the late great Buck Buchanan inducted into the National Football League's Hall of Fame. Even though a different personality, Lloyd holds his own at group meetings. But, most of all, Lloyd may be the most fair-minded individual I have ever met. . . always thinking of others. In fact, he was his idea that we dedicated last week's column to our late friend, Uncle Walt Livingston. One can immediately see why he is one of the most sought-after realtors in the community. That because he is always. . . quiet, unassuming and quite a thinker."

column1: It is different, with the Chiefs, these days. Last year, at this time, the Chiefs were fighting just to make the ever-elusive playoffs. Not so, this year. Already made the playoffs. Already won on the AFC West Division. So, what’s next? Well, Sunday, the Chiefs will move on to Minnesota and pursue homefield advantage. While the Chiefs struggle a large-opposing running back from openly devouring us, the Chiefs are sporting a large-scale, league-leading offense. One that features an All-Pro, running back, a late-rising quarterback, and a herd of unstoppable receivers. And while everybody knows the almost-unblemished offense, every one still thinks the run defense will eventually run us down. But, weak defense or not, tomorrow’s game rest solely on how well the Chiefs are able to contain the Culpepper-Moss connection. This reliable duo will test the secondary, this time with short and long passes. Some will be bullet-accurate and others will be bobble-wobbly. Either way, look for Warfield, Wesley, and Woods to have their hands full. Moreover, the same defensive line that allowed Clinton Portis, through the cracks, full now have to rush and deck Culpepper. Just like covering Moss, sacking Culpepper will be a formidable task. Ike thinks Minnesota will win, and here he comes with that Chiefs bashing stuff. It was resoundingly Chiefs loss, and here he comes with that Chiefs bashing stuff. Don’t worry, though, the Chiefs won’t be watching Sunday, to see if they are closer to homefield advantage, throughout the playoffs. Happy Holidays!"

column1: As one can discern, the Monday Niters are divided about what team will win on Sunday. Enough said about that. Ike and Booker are the season's best predictors. Enough said about that, too. But rather than write an organized, thoughtful column, let's just look at some Super Bowl trivia and potpourri (just miscellaneous stuff). This will be particularly helpful to those of you who are casual football fans and look to our column for helpful hints. Here goes:
*   Janet Jackson has been replaced by Paul McCartney as the halftime keynote.
*   It's been 24 years, since the Eagles landed at the Super Bowl. Dick Vermeil was the coach, then.
*   Jacksonville is the smallest and newest city to host a Super Bowl. The site is Alltell Stadium.
*   New England will be in the Super Bowl for the third time in four years. Tom Brady, Patriot quarterback, was named MVP in the two games won by the Patriots.
*   Most of the chatter has been about whether Terrell Owens will play. Chances are he will suit up and star. The Pro Bowler caught 14 touchdown passes in 14 games.
*   Tony Gonzalez, Chiefs' All-Pro tight end, says New England will win. Also, Renee Gardner, Ike's daughter, believes in the Patriots, as well.

*   It was Super Bowl 22, in 1988 when Doug Williams, first black quarterback to start a Super Bowl, just showed out in Washington's victory over John Elway and the Denver Broncos. The score was 42-10, with Doug throwing for 4 touchdowns.
*   New England safety, Rodney Harrison is considered the ""dirtiest"" player in the NFL. He has been fined over $350,000 for his vicious hits on receivers.

*   Lloyd and Geneva Greenfield will host the Monday Niters and their guests at their Birchwood subdivision home for the annual Super Bowl party. Lloyd is a real estate advisor and Geneva is a renown storyteller."

The Chiefs are returning home to play the Atlanta Falcons, this Sunday. Tired, weary, confused, and broke of spirit, they will try to restore the widespread fan faith of a year ago. Instead of being atop everything, the Chiefs are 1-4, allowing the less faithful an opportunity to pontificate. And there's been a lot of fan talk about why the Chiefs decided not to exercise their trading rights, given their dismal record. Have the Chiefs conceded the season and are now positioning themselves for first round drafting in 2005? Balderdash!

This team has what it takes to win. And you can expect them to get the offense and defense uncorked this week-end. The Chiefs must win for ticket prices have now fallen below zero. And merchants who overstocked their shelves with Chiefs' accessories, are worried that the extra large supply of jerseys, hoodies, baseball caps, ashtrays, and a lot other stuff will remain unsold.
Look for either a rushing, passing, and sack record to fall Sunday. The Chiefs will outslick Vick."

column1: 1. Colts quarterback Jim Harbaugh may have taken the NFL by surprise this season, but the Chiefs only have to look back two years to know what kind of damage the resourceful nine-year veteran can create. Back on November 21, 1993, the Chiefs took an early 14-0 advantage at home on the Harbaugh-led Chicago Bears, but let the game slip away. Despite passing for just 98 yards, Harbaugh tortured the Chiefs all day with his scrambling ability which netted the Bears 56 yards and four rushing first downs.

2. The Indianapolis Colts are coming off their first playoff victory since 1971, a 35-20 upset over the defending AFC Champion Chargers. The Colts churned out 178 yards on the ground despite losing the services of Marshall Faulk early in the game. Rookie Zack Crockett burst on the scene with 147 yards on 13 tries; he had one carry for zero yards during the regular season.

3. Tamarick Vanover's 1,897 all-purpose yards this season was the second-most ever by a Chief (Abner Haynes, 2,100 in 1960). Vanover averaged 126.5 total yards per game and 17.1 yards every time he touched the ball.
4. Derrick Thomas and Neil Smith have combined for 145.5 sacks in the 1990's, far more than any duo in the League. Smith leads all NFL defenders with 53 sacks the past four seasons.
5. If you or anyone that you know is seeking treatment for addiction please do not hesitate to contact Jim Nunnally at 881-3814 or the COMBAT lines at 84C-OMBT.
6. This Sunday's game will be the Chiefs 42nd straight sellout. For the second straight year, the Chiefs led the NFL in home attendance, this time with a average of 77,523 fans per game."

All during the 1995 Chiefs football season I have been chided, verbally harassed, and accused of being a traitor by church members, family, friends and former co-workers because I had the vision to see the Chiefs as they really are.
At best, the Chiefs are a mediocre team who lucked up on a 13-3 record, which clearly belies their true performance.
When the facts are scrutinized objectively the following is disclosed:
1. Though 13-3, the Chiefs played only four teams that have a .500 or better record;
2. The Chiefs have struggled to get by some of the weakest teams in the League e.g., the New York Giants and the Houston Oilers;
3. In terms of offense, the Chiefs ranked 18th (out of 30) in the NFL;
4. Some of the Chiefs' victories were not only against scrubby teams, but against teams whose key players were out because of injury e.g., San Diego, Denver and Oakland; and
5. Bono does a good job only of being a pal to Joe Montana ( for whatever that's worth).
For all of you who were disappointed, last Sunday's defeat was really a blessing. At least the score was close.

If Pittsburgh would have come into town this Sunday, Nick Lowery and Jan Stenerud in their prime would not have made a difference. The Chiefs rode the shoulders of the Almighty all season long, but like Moses, they did not make it to the promised land (nor did they deserve to)."

What goes wrong with the Chiefs? Each year they start out winning, start to slip in mid-season and go on to sink to new lows? Is it our garage sale quarterback? Is it coaching? Is the defense overloaded and expected to carry more than its share? Is the pass coverage both suspect and sub-optimal? Are the unpredictable Chiefs enter their 10th game, the answer to all of these questions are not that simple. Truthfully, the Kansas City Chiefs are still a work in progress...rebuilding the prundite might call it. At any rate, the Chiefs are not an average team that has many faults and they show up. Just like in the last two weeks against Tampa Bay and the Indianapolis Colts. Even Stevie Wonder can see the obvious, i.e., the kickoff returners fumble, the secondary (Dishman and Warfield) get beat a lot, and Elvis will throw the ball to the opponent as if they were Chiefs players. Put this together with a spectacular defense and you have what the Chiefs are a very average 5-4 team who are hell-bent on courtring catastrophe in the fourth quarter.

Nonetheless, there is a good side to all of this. Somehow the Chiefs manage to emerge and erase some of these blatant shortcomings before the hometown crowd. It is as if they take a magic pill and just do it in friendly Arrowhead Stadium. Why is this so? We don’t know and can’t even imagine a decent explanation. But we had better try because Sunday, the first place, well-coached Seattle Seahawks come to town Sunday. No doubt about it, if we survive and beat the Seahawks, it will be the equivalent of making the mid-season playoffs. To achieve this miracle, the locals have to do three things...One, Derrick Thomas must sack and tackle like never before. Two, our ever-changing running backs must tank for a long run and Grbac and Alexander or Horn must connect for two big plays. On top of that, McGlockton, Hicks, Edwards, Hasty and Woods must play at superlative levels, getting to the quarterback right from the start.

If the Chiefs do these things and the play-calling is adequate, then the 78,000 Arrowhead fans can help. In short, Kansas City can no longer live by even normal means. Usual Chiefs' supporters like Ed West and Carl Clark have changed their minds and believe Seattle will win handily. They join ol' Ike Gardner, Ollie Hubbard, and Paul Booker who collectively think the Chiefs will lose their first home game Sunday and leave the Chiefs at 5-5. On the other hand, Dr. Charles Allen and Lloyd Greenfield know that the Chiefs will find a way to win at home, as unexplainable as it might seem. Look for the Chiefs to win their sixth game and resurrect thoughts of having a decent, play-off likely season. I’m with Charles and Lloyd.

1. Houston is no defensive slough this season, permitting an AFC-low 298.2 total yards per game. Kansas City has been triumphant in both playoff contests against Houston.

2. The last time the Chiefs opened a season with a 9-1 record and won six straight games was 1969, the year the club went 11-3 and won Super Bowl IV. The Chiefs have now posted a winning record in six consecutive seasons.
3. Neil Smith and Derrick Thomas are tied for fifth in the AFC, with 7 sacks a piece. Dale Carter is tied for third in the conference with four interceptions.
4. The Houston Oilers dropped a tough 32-25 decision to Cincinnati last week after winning back-to-back games over Tampa Bay (19-7) and Cleveland ( 37-10).
5. Houston's top receivers are former Chief Todd McNair, Haywood Jeffries and rookie Rodney Thomas. Thomas is also the club's leading rusher with 549 yards and two scores on 136 carries.
6. Anyone seeking treatment can call Jim Nunnelly at 881-3814 or the COMBAT lines at 842-COMBAT.

7. Kimble Anders now has 183 career receptions, tying him with J.J. Birden for the ninth most in Chiefs history. Steve Bono has four rushing touchdowns this season, tying Steve Fuller ('80) for the most by a quarterback in a season.
One can always trust to time. Insert a wedge of time and nearly everything straightens itself out.

Don't make your move too soon

1. Kansas City has allowed just 303.4 total yards per game, the fourth fewest in the league, and only 16.0 points per game, the second-fewest.

2. This will be the Chiefs biggest pass defense challenge of the year having faced just one of the NFL's top 10 passing offenses this year. One way to slow to the Miami aerial attack is to get to the quarterback, which the Chiefs have done 39 times this year, tying for the fourth-most sacks in the league.
3. Miami does an exceptional job protecting the passer, allowing just 23 sacks, tying for the seventh-fewest in the NFL.

4. Don Shula is in his 33rd season as an NFL head coach and 26th with the Dolphins. Miami has won or shared first place in the AFC East in 15 of his first 25 years in Miami, and earned 15 playoff berths in that span.

5. The Chiefs 11-2 record is the best in the NFL and good enough for an AFC West title and a home playoff game. The Chiefs can clinch a first-round bye with a win at Miami and can clinch home-field advantage throughout the playoffs with a win at Miami and Pittsburgh loss or tie at Oakland.

column5: This week, defense is for real
Two losses, 14 more chances to win
Still, I want to know 'what's going on'

column1: Seven teams have lost their first two games. Unfortunately, one of those teams is our Kansas City Chiefs. What's more is we are still expecting the Chiefs to be 2-0, like they were this time last year. Speaking of last year, we were all worried up about Priest Holmes' hip. You will remember he was not supposed to play at all and he just got well and became the NFL's top touchdown producer.

Well, now it's his knee and ankle. Priest was hurt on a play that bent him backwards and thus, he was held out the remainder of the Carolina game. While Priest's health is something to talk about, we are still wondering why he was not in the game on the fourth down play, against the Panthers. A guy who is the franchise player . . . who is the best scorer in the entire league. . . has a burst of speed like no other Chief in history (except Mike Garrett). . . and Vermeil chose to go to a second stringer. Hmmmmmm. Five days later, and there still is no reasonable explanation why Priest was not in the game. Maybe there was another injury or just plain bad coaching.

Speaking of injuries, the wide receiver corps is affected again. Kennison and Morton are probable for this week. That means they will play. Who is their right mind would not play Kennison, the only deep threat the Chiefs have? Look for (Kennison) to lead the Chiefs to their first victory, this Sunday out at Arrowhead."

# Carolina Panthers at Kansas City Chiefs
A win will heal a broken heart
Priest runs, catches, scores, beyond belief.
Shame on you, you fair-weather Chiefs’ fans.

You are joining up with the haters (Ike and Booker). You are acting like the Chiefs are 0-5, rather than 0-1. Hey, this is just the first game and while there are things to be fixed, 16 other teams lost, last week-end, as well. So, let’s not talk as if there are no more opportunities to win.

In fact, watching Carolina play on Monday night reminded us all that this was the team that was in last year’s Super Bowl. They certainly didn’t look like they were the 2003 NFC champs. Further, the Chiefs will again get yet another opportunity to power up its powerful offense and improve its lackluster defense. And just think, there will be 14 games after that.
Bottom line, we will have plenty of time to see if the Chiefs are for real or if they are just pretenders. The jury is still out on Gunther and even Al Sanders and yes, even Dick Vermeil.
So, let’s just wait a while before we write-off our beloved Chiefs. We’ve got a tough schedule ahead and we will have to be at our level best. But, fans, it is only the second game. Let’s be patient.

**Sports Editor’s Note:** Paul Booker and Ike Gardner were the only two winners in last week’s prediction of the Denver game. Way to go Paul and Ike! Shame on the rest of you Monday Niters for even imagining that the Chiefs will ever win in Denver. P-l-e-a-s-e. They couldn’t win there even if Schottenheimer was still here."

I hate to see that evening sun go down...on the Chiefs...that is. Sunday will show how far removed the Chiefs are from becoming a contender. St. Louis is too fast, too deep, too balanced, and too diversified. The Chiefs will lose bigtime. (Ollie says he'll lose 100 pounds if the Chiefs win!)""

TOO MANY CHIEFS AND NOT ENOUGH YARDS -- If William Shakespeare were alive today, he would say that old adage would summarize the Chiefs and their ineffective, five-man running back system. On first hearing, one may think that if we keep fresh backs in the game, it will wear down the defense and one of the quintet will break one or two for one or two touchdowns. The ridiculousness of this system was in full force last week against Oakland, when these fresh backs only gained a mere 60 yards on the ground. Richardson stumbled while Anders ambled while Moreau moped while Cloud continued to further cloudy the situation. The time has come for the Chiefs to decide that one running back is going to carry the ball and that's it. Coach Cunningham announced Wednesday that Kimble Anders will be the Chiefs starting running back on Sunday. That decision will surely result in more yardage, more scoring, and even better passing game. No other NFL team employs such a medieval, terribly outdated system, leaving opponents completely unmoved by this pigskin, group therapy. If the Chiefs decide to change, they can anticipate a win on Sunday. If they continue with this proven nonsense, they will slip into another year without playoff participation and their fourth loss. St. Louis is highly favored but a Chiefs' victory is not out of the question.

IMANI HOUSE, A LEADER IN EFFECTIVE DRUG TREATMENT – A few months ago, Imani House of the Swope Parkway Health center, was one of the 37 COMBAT agencies participating in an outcomes fair. Highlighting culturally relevant treatment systems, Imani House reports that four out of five clients are drug-free at the time of discharge. About the same number are employed or have received relevant employment training. Imani provides a panoply of services, including out-patient, in-patient and HIV components.

Everybody in town is talking about the new Chiefs, the same team that Ollie Hubbard and ol' Ike Gardner thought would be 1-5 about now. And to their disdain, the Kansas City Chiefs are 4-2 and in a first-place share with San Diego and Seattle. While the Chiefs are winning, they are still a team with lots of developing to do. And for Pete's sake, avoid any talk about championships and Super Bowls.

As Ed West and Walt Livingston keep pointing out, the defense is the main reason for these four wins. A strong defensive line, coupled with a resurrected Derrick Thomas and a big-play maker Donnie Edwards, make the Chiefs a daunting force at pressuring opposing quarterbacks. And, of course, the secondary, just waits for the interceptions. And there are a few bright spots on the offense side of the ball. There's center Tim Grunhard and guard Will Shields who together open large, gaping holes and pass blocks with accuracy, skill, and without holding penalties.

Receivers Alexander, Horn, Gonzalez and Lockett are beginning to rise to the third-down occasions and each would be better if there was some consistency in Elvis Grbac's performance. If Grbac doesn't throw an interception, or call needless timeouts, the Chiefs should beat San Diego Sunday at Arrowhead stadium. Look for a big play from ""seldom seen"" Andre Rison and overdue Tamarick Vanover. The two Derricks - Alexander and Thomas - will play awesome and running back Shehee will again prove worthy of Jimmy Raye's confidence. In short, the Chiefs will still be in first place on Monday morning. Even Ike Gardner believes that.

Super Bowl winning, Elway-less, Denver Broncos will play their AFC West rivals, the San Diego Chargers, in the American Bowl on Saturday night on FOX. Following on Monday night on ABC, the Dallas Cowboys will re-welcome the Cleveland Browns into the NFL. Give all the credit to Cleveland’s African American mayor, Mike White, for relentlessly restoring pro football to Cleveland. Note that the team retained its ageless name, the Browns.

Jackson County Prosecutor Bob Beaird wants 50 inner-city youth involved in the planning and development of drug prevention programs. Because young people have had no previous role in these efforts, Beaird plans to seek their advice on new programs, especially those affecting youth. If you are between the ages of 11 and 19, and have had drug problems in your family, you are invited to call Jim Nunnelly or Angela Castle at 881-3113. The youth will be chosen at a ceremony at the Eastern Jackson County Boys and Girls Club in Independence, Mo., on August 14 at 11 a.m.

Former Central High School great, Warren (Armstrong) Jabali, will appear this weekend as part of the annual M.U.S.T basketball tournament. A standout at Wichita State and the ABA, Jabali will also appear on the Generation Rap program at 8 o'clock tomorrow morning on KPRS-FM. While at Central, he won the coveted DiRenna award for the outstanding high school basketball player in the metropolitan area. Carl Boyd is host of the show.

If you have ever witnessed someone who does things effortlessly and did not know how to explain such grace and skill, chances are he or she possesses ""prowess."" That’s the “Word from Grand Dad” on Generation Rap, also Saturday on KPRS-FM. Listen in for more details.
**OUGHT TO BE BANNER YEAR FOR CHIEFS’ DERRICK ALEXANDER**

The 1999 season ought to be a great one for big-play, wide receiver, Derrick Alexander. After being injured early in the season, Alexander went on to catch 54 passes for almost a thousand yards last year. His six-two, 210-pound frame, and his deceptive speed allowed him to team up with then-Chiefs' quarterback, Rich Gannon. However, this year with a healthy Grbac and with the addition of ""rifle-armed"" Warren Moon, Alexander should be among the top receivers in the American Football conference. By the way, Grbac and Alexander were teammates at the University of Michigan. Gannon is now the starting quarterback for the Oakland Raiders."

ENTITIES: Pittsburgh Steelers, Kansas City Chiefs, Jim Nunnelly, Charles Allen, Carl Clark, Lloyd Greenfield, Ed West, Minnesota Vikings, Derrick Thomas, Ike Gardner, Jeff George, Paul Booker, Ollie Hubbard, Kordell Stewart, Randy Moss, Seattle, Denver

Charles Allen, Carl Clark, Lloyd Greenfield, Ed West, and I told you that the Chiefs would beat the Minnesota Vikings last Sunday night at Arrowhead. It wasn't pretty and as usual, the Chiefs scared us, especially after Minnesota came back after being held 21-0. Just as we predicted, there were lots of mis- takes with special unmentionables going to the special teams. Like we said, the Chiefs had never put together a total victory and Sunday night was no excep- tion. The special teams allowed two touchdowns to be run back on them, one by the fast-off-foot receiver, Randy Moss. The defense played well, with special kudos for the resurrected Derrick Thomas. The offense played okay, and Grbac did not hurt us too much. In fact it could be said Grbac was respectable, especially in the two-minute drive in the final seconds that led to Stoy- anovich's last second, game winning field goal. Now that we've won and are now in a two-way tie for first place with Seattle, what must we do to win Sat- urday against failing Pittsburgh.

Right away, Ike Gardner says we must put first teamers on the special teams. If you notice, other teams treat the special teams as an essential component of the team. The Chiefs seem to crowd the special team with rookies and also rans, giving this vital unit second class and unimportant status. In addition, the Chiefs have to jump on Pittsburgh's quarterback quickly and harshly, just as they did Minnesota's brittle quarterback Jeff George. Had it not been for getting the jump on George, the outcome of the game would have been radi- cally different. Then, we must continue to mix up our offense. Passing on first and second down was refreshing and finding Gonzalez more often is critical. If the Chiefs do this, they can be ready for the ""showdown in Seattle"" the day after Christmas. But let's not go too far too fast and get back to Saturday morning with the Steelers.

The Steelers are struggling badly. They've replaced ""Slash"" Kordell Stewart and put him back at wide receiver. The Iron Curtain defense is now aluminum and they've got real problems with special teams. Even Ike Gardner thinks the Chiefs will win this Saturday, along with the regular Chiefs supporters. Only Paul Booker remains stubborn, in spite of the Chiefs three straight victories. He thinks ""Slash"" will be instrumental. However, Ed West thinks the ""Slash"" will get slashed, and allow the Chiefs to tighten their hold on first place. In case Denver should beat Seattle, then the Chiefs would possess first place all alone. That would be great, because if the Chiefs win all of their remaining games, then they would win the division outright. All things considered, the Chiefs will be 9-5 on Sunday morning.

**ABOUT THE GIANTS:** Several Monday Niters have registered their prediction in favor of New York this Sunday. The Giants are coming off the most relating win of the season over the previously undefeated Denver Broncos. And the Giants may be for real, winning three out of their last four games. Quarterback Ken Graham, running back Tiki Barber, and receiver Christ Calloway make up this tough-minded offense. Pro Bowl defensive end Straham and linebacker Gene Armstead lead the defense. Sometimes, the Giant defense will be out to stop the GARB Connection (Gannon, Alexander, Rison and Bam).

**ABOUT THE CHIEFS:** Martyball has been changed to Smartyball. The offense has churned out nearly 350 yards and 30.5 points per game over the last four weeks. Last week against Dallas, the Chiefs assembled a running game, a solid defense, and decent playcalling to eke past the Cowboys 20-17. Bam Morris, recently acquired running back, smashed through the Dallas line for 137 yards, the first time since Donnell Bennett did it on opening day. The defense literally shut down Emmitt Smith and Troy Aikman, all of this coupled with the GARB Connection. Look for the Chiefs to win the next two to finish 8-8.

ENTITIES: Hank Stram, Kansas City Chiefs, Jim ""Grand Dad"" Nunnélly, American Football League, National Football League, Chicago Bears, San Francisco 49ers, Washington Redskins, Lamar Hunt, Len Dawson, Grambling, Southern, Texas Southern, Branch Rickey, Jackie Robinson, Super Bowl
Hank Stram was the first head coach of the Kansas City Chiefs.

Only those of us over 50 will remember the days when Kansas City was part of the old American Football League. This was the early '60s, when the predominant National Football League touted franchises . . . like the Chicago Bears, San Francisco 49ers, and the Washington Redskins. And Hank Stram and others had the task of competing with and enlarging the territory of the fragile but willing League. And enlarge they did. And Hank Stram, under the guidance of Lamar Hunt, brought Kansas City up from nowhere into the big leagues.

Before merging, the AFL actually had to outbid and outwit the more formidable NFL. More important, they also had to outplay them, as well. And that's where Hank Stram came in. With smaller, lesser-known players, Hank had to be an innovator. With a small, frail quarterback like Len Dawson, Stram implemented a ""moving pocket"". That allowed the bigger, faster offensive linemen to outmaneuver the slower, meaner defensive linemen. And that would lead to a more fan-friendly game.

While Hank was a master at coaching, he knew how to motivate the newly-found, small-college athletes. Especially the young athletes from Grambling, Southern, and Texas Southern. And it is here where he will be most remembered by folks of color. Never was there any kind of racial uprising or unfair play on any of Stram's teams. He just didn't tolerate it and he made everybody feel included. More important, he treated them fairly at the pocketbook level, as well. Many came poor and left well-paid, especially in their later years. In conclusion, Hank Stram was more than innovation to this-new league. He was more than a coach, who coined phrases like ""matriculating down the field"". Since Stram was in charge, we have not been near a Super Bowl. But he was right up there with Branch Rickey, who cleared the path for Jackie Robinson to play Major League baseball. With the same level of commitment, Stram cleared the ""economic path"" for these young unheard athletes to make a lot of money. Simple as that. He did enlarge our territory.

column1: Every now and then, I get to talk one-on-one with each of the Monday Niters. By far, one of the most intelligent and low-key guy is our host for the week, Lloyd Greenfield. Steeped in the field of real estate and a staunch patron of the theater, Lloyd seldom talks about himself or his career or his feelings. With all the other loud-talkers in the group, Lloyd is seldom heard. But respected, he is. If you walk into his newly-renovated, south Kansas City home, you will find a hospitable ambience, highlighted by a large collection of model motorcycles. It's almost as if Lloyd has lived one continual, teenage life, espousing all the things he has always championed. And he has always devoutly supported the Chiefs. In spite of those haters in the group, Ol' Lloyd still hangs on to the hope that one day, the Chiefs will return to the Super Bowl. And you know something? This current crew of Chiefs is about to take us all there. Needless to say, the Chiefs are advancing and ready to take on anybody, at any time. And this Sunday, Derrick Johnson will show LaDainian Thompson what the NFL is all about. And you can count on Kennison to show up and show out. And look for Priest and Johnson to break a record or two. While others may not listen to Lloyd, I do. He says Chiefs by 6. And I say, ""right on, Lloyd""."

column1: Rev. Emanuel Cleaver will preach this Sunday and every Sunday at the St. James United Methodist church, in spite of having a new gig in Washington. Then, the next night, he will host the Monday Niters, who will start their 35th season as a group of predictors. Moreover, Ed West, Ike Gardner, and Carl Clark are members of his church, leaving Rev with some additional influence over the Monday Niters. And influence he has... already Ike Gardner is actually voting for the Chiefs. And so is Carl Clark. But, it seems that Ed ""Unpredictable"" West is the renegade, this year. He and Ollie Hubbard think the folks at Arrowhead will hang their heads in shame, losing to Mr. Edwards and the New York Jets.

As for Dr. Charles Allen and myself, we are predictable. . . we are steadfast in our support for the Chiefs. In fact, we know that Johnson and Johnson will add offensive and defensive punch to our wayward Chiefs. We must pray for the backsliding secondary. And stay in prayer for our defensive line, as the Lord knows our every weakness. Overall, the Chiefs will look a lot like David, taking on the stronger and mightier Goliath (the Jets). But, that's why we have Rev in our midst. . . to increase our spiritual faith and apply it everyday. Let there be victory! Amen. Amen.
(Chuck James is a former Board of Trustees member with the Metropolitan Community colleges)
*   N.Y. Jets over Kansas City -- I’m not a Kansas City fan. Jets have an improved defense. Kansas City has yet to prove that their defense has been revamped.
