column1: **Editor's Note: Congratulations goes to Ed West, long-time Monday Niter, for being the winner of the 2006 ""Predictions Award"". Way to go Ed, from THE CALL.**

Well, you know we have some Monday Niters who got some bad news over the holidays. It wasn't about the Chiefs and their playoff hopes. It wasn't about how Denver squeaked by the Bengals in a thriller. It was about their favorite hang-out, the infamous ""6902"". A shooting, resulting in a murder, was recorded. Then, right after that, the place burned up. What a week for the well-attended night club. Booker cried. Carl is just not himself at all, and Charles is clearly at a loss for words. Several neighbors have commented that the club is usually trouble-free and attracted both young and old. And it is significant because what happened there was more newsworthy than what is happening with our last-chance Chiefs.

Here's the scene. If the Chiefs are to win a spot in the playoffs. First, they must beat Jacksonville on Sunday, out at Arrowhead. Some think they will become usually (I say usually) the Chiefs win at home. But, in addition to a must-win for the Chiefs; both San Francisco, Tennessee and Pittsburgh must win. Then, the Chiefs would backdoor into the last playoff spot. But, I have a question for you. Why is it we always have to struggle our way into the playoffs, especially here in December? Why couldn't we just have beaten weak Cleveland and crippled Miami? Then, we would be just like the Jets...already in. But, that Herman Edwards and that no-named offensive coordinator just blew it all...with their senseless, play-ground play-calling. Bottom line, prepare yourself for a Chiefs' loss Sunday or for Denver and Cincinnati to win. Any one of those scenarios will spell the end of the sea-son for Kansas City. And just think, Herm has already started talking about 2007 and how he is going to stick with Trickey Trent Green and just like always, the Chiefs will soon be talking smack and lament that they were two or three plays away from post-season competition. Crap.
Anybody know when the ""6902"" is gonna re-open?"

column1: Ike is a maverick. . .an individualist. . .a tough character. But when he hosts, he makes sure each of us gets their favorite dish. And though he never articulates it, he really likes most people, especially those who strive to do their best. That's because he has always stood out, in everything he has done. And he still exhibits this ""rugged individualism"" today, even though he is 72. Thus, this ""maverick thing"" is deeply-rooted in his youth. Let's explore.

Even as an eight-year-old, he actually rode the train from Kansas City to Detroit, all alone and having to change trains in Chicago. And, most of us met Ike, when he dared to take the chairmanship of the infamous Desegregation Task Force, way back in the early seventies. . .when Kansas City received national attention for its faulty efforts at desegregating its schools. And he is still mad at the University of Missouri for not admitting him, even farther back in the fifties. And when you catch him reminiscing, he even drifts back to his Air Force days when segregation was at its worst in Texas.

Still looking back, Ike speaks positively about his undergraduate days at the University of Michigan and Howard universities. Proudly, he talked about how his Alpha ""frat-brothers"" saved him from a good ol' spanking, while on a weekender in East Lansing, Michigan. Now, all of this is Ike for he literally lived in times gone by. Times when things were better; parents were stricter; and everybody lived up to a standard. He flat-out revels in yesterday. And it is here that he has problems with the Chiefs. Even though the **CALL** readers get mad at Ike, he still is a loyal Chiefs' fan. Let me explain.

You see, Ike was literally one of the first season-ticket holders, way back when they played the games at Municipal Stadium at 22nd and Brooklyn. Never missing a game, ol' Ike has seen some real heroes in a Chiefs' uniform. He's seen Ernie Ladd, Mike Garrett, and Otis Taylor in their finest hours. And it angers and disappoints him when he witnesses the Chiefs lapsing slowly into a constant mediocrity. In fact, he thinks the Chiefs will win only three more games this year. To make matters worse, he thinks lowly Oakland will beat the Chiefs on Sunday. That's just the ""rugged individualism"" that lives inside of him. But, he's good at what he does. In fact, he is the only Monday Nite to pick the point spread exactly on two occasions in the same year. And arguably, he may well be the all-time best predictor. And I guess that's why he goes to extreme lengths, when hosting us... giving us everything we like, including that precious walnut-laced chocolate cake. Even in his kindest moments, he still is a ""maverick"".
Thus, so many people totally misunderstand him. But he knows that. And, quite frankly, he doesn't give a damn, either. End of story."

# Kansas City Chiefs vs. St. Louis Rams

column3: Lloyd Greenfield was our host last Monday night. And his home is like museum, with all kinds of collections... motorcycles and cars everywhere. While I don't know a lot about motorcycles, I can attest only to the 130 model cars that I personally witnessed. There were old Studebakers, a Nash, a Corvair and even a black 1941 Cadillac.

column2: KC by 6 Hang on Snoopy, hang on!
column3: Throughout the evening, someone would call out the name of a classic car and ol' Lloyd would proudly pick it out from his flock. But, that's the kind of guy Lloyd is... not too talkative, by no means a loud-mouth. In the mix but definitely letting his actions speak for him.

In fact, it was Lloyd who urged us to become Lifetime Members of the NAACP. And further, many of listen to Lloyd to keep us informed about all the Broadway plays. Just three weeks ago, he made us purchase tickets to see the play *Jitney*. And each time we go to his house, we find that he has “re-modeled” his museum again... a new room or a built-in big-screen or some addition to house his collections. Quietly, but forcefully, his house reflects its owner. Thus, when it comes to the Chiefs, Lloyd loves his some Chiefs.

column2: KC by 10/ Chief's hands vs Holt's Rams
column3: In the group, we have the Loyalists (those who support the Chiefs) and there are the Haters (those that berate the Chiefs). I can't ever remember Lloyd ever voting against the Chiefs. And when others are talking loud and drawing a crowd, Lloyd simply states his predictions. But the Chiefs are not as loyal to Lloyd as Lloyd is to the Chiefs. Somehow or someway, the Chiefs always man-age to come out all right but Lloyd wants a championship team. He wants a collection of Chiefs’ stuff. He wants to boast loudly about his home-town team. If the Chiefs were to keep winning, Lloyd might even shout from atop City Hall. If... If... If.

That is the word... If. Face it y'all, the Chiefs are “iffey”. And after a shaky start, the Chiefs have grown to a 4-3 record. Now they must face the “The Greatest Show On Turf”, the St. Louis Rams. Ty Law and Patrick Surtain will ask for a disability check after trying to cover Bruce and Holt on Sunday. And, like Lloyd, we Loyalists must stick together. In fact, I believe that Tamba Hall and the Johnson boys will set some records in St. Louis. Just like Lloyd quietly said.

column2: KC by 10/ Tony G. and Huard are connected
column3: But that's Lloyd for you... quiet but forceful... living in his own, museum-filled world... waiting for the Chiefs to advance to championship status...

column1: Oh yes, we talked about the Chiefs over at Ike Gardner's house. And we frequently made good references to the chocolate-walnut cake, prepared by Ike's Ann Lillian. And we got the good news that Carl Clark was okay, after a brief visit to the emergency room Sunday. And of course, we highly praised the Chiefs for their comeback victory over the hapless Arizona Cardinals. However, the always-loyal Lloyd Greenfield stated upfront that we ought to dedicate this column to Buck O'Neil. Thus, you have our joint thinking about Buck.

All of us knew Buck, personally. We can see him now, driving around in his late-model Cadillac, wearing that broke-down brim and the extra-thick glasses. Always smiling, he always managed to uplift whomever he met. And for the record, Buck would forget your name but never the circumstances around which he met you. Buck might have enjoyed being in touch with folks, more than anyone I've ever met. And to enhance the encounter Buck would go into oft-repeated act of singing, talking about important folks he'd met, or make some historical reference to his connection to baseball. Night or day. Old or young. Buck acted like he liked and knew everybody.

Regardless of your age, Of Buck could charm you. And if you tarried long enough, you could also see the sounds of serenity, just emanating from his personality. Every hard to reach and teach teenager warmed up when Buck came near. They'd never seen anybody remain exactly the same, regardless of the day of the week or time of day. He seemed forever glad to see you and all of Buck confirmed that.
And regardless of how your day was going, Buck could improve it, right on the spot.

Ain't it funny we don't see a lot of people like Buck anymore? Someone who just enjoyed life to the fullest, complaint-free. And just make sure that he would be remembered, he'd use the smallest things to make big impressions. I can see him now... singing the ""Greatest Thing,"" or he'd be talking about kissing Hillary Clinton, and reminiscing about the many times he hit for the ""cycle."" We have a job opening in our community now. Is there anyone out there who can replace Of Buck O'Neil?
Probably not. They just don't make 'em like that any more. Case closed."

column1: Gonna try this one more time!
**Can Herm get his defense to play like**

Ollie and Sandra Hubbard just renovated their south Kansas City home. Living there for over 30 years, their house is usually full of visitors...from both near and far. Thus, when the Monday Nite convention there last Monday, the new digs and a roommate greeted us at the front door. It was like a whole new house, even though there were just a few adjustments made to the family room. But, the endless football conversation was not new at all. It was the same ole crap...what do we do with our Chiefs? Some say it is the defense. Some say ol' Herm has messed up a good offense. Even others say that Larry Johnson cannot do it all. And most of us, still wonder why we can't lock down a play-off spot, on our own.

Of course, when the Chiefs lost to lowly Cleveland, the haters came out in full force. Even ol' Booker, who had joined up with the loyalists, has reverted to his old ways and thinks that McNair and the Baltimore Ravens will embarrass the Chiefs, even out at Arrowhead Sunday. Now, we know that Ike feels but doubtful and Ed West has moved to the “haters” side, as well. So what are we loyalists to do? Shall we follow the facts that the Chiefs are just “iffey” and just live with that? Do we abandon the Chiefs and just cease our hopeless hoping? Should we just “bite the bullet” and trust that the other 7-5 teams will lose, giving us yet another dimming-chance to the coveted play-offs.

The answer may lie in the scriptures. The Chiefs are like the prodigal son for they have taken the Kansas City fans through so many changes, so many vicissitudes (ups and downs). In-fact, it is no longer safe to say that you are a Chiefs fan. Take the prodigal son, one day they'll come back...a little raggedy but willing to start all over and become the team they are capable of being. Like the father, who waits for news of the crew why. We must just wait from them, when they decide to turn from their wicked ways.

Dr. Allen, Lloyd Loyal, and I think they are just inches away from “showing up and showing out” again. Yes, we have had to go through some real drama every year, of watching them play good one Sunday only to look like clowns the next. However, in the end, the Chiefs will be looking as re-newed as the Hubbard's newly-renoved house and we'll forgive and forget about all we had to go through.

And then we'll welcome the Chiefs to the playoffs, even home-field advantage...and Larry John-son will be crowned the MVP...and the Chiefs will beat not only Baltimore but Oakland, San Diego and Jacksonville. And the television announcers will be saying, “What about those Chiefs?”
Wait! Am I dreaming or what? Let's go back to the Hubbards and start this conversation all over."

column1: The Colts will have to enhance their defense if they are to beat Chicago in the Super Bowl! Wait a minute! Isn't this column supposed to be about the Kansas City Chiefs. Yes, that is so. But, the Chiefs are dead ... certainly out of the playoffs (almost).... Larry Johnson will not make MVP or capture the rushing title....and the rest of the team will be home in January, watching the playoffs on TV like the rest of us. For all practical purposes, the Chiefs are ""history"" and now we can hope that two teams, led by African American coaches, will end up in the February Super Bowl.

Especially, we Monday Niters are through with them...after that disappointing display of junior high football, against San Diego last Sunday. Dropped passes, missed tackles, scant blocking, and un-heard-of play-calling. It even caused Charles Fort, a former Kansas Citizen (now living in San Diego), to literally cry...That's because he is so embarrassed that he can no longer face his California friends. Well, Charles, we loyalists can't face our friends here in Kansas City, either. We have to admit that the ""haters"" were right. Even ol' Ike says that we should have kept Marty here. Wow! Boom! Pow! Right in Herm Edwards' face. And he's the first African American coach, here in Kansas City. Doesn't that count for something.

Not if he ain't winning. Now, back to Tony Dungy (Colts) and Lovie Smith (Bears) playing in the Super Bowl. Isn't that such a nice thought? Seeing history made. Do you think it can happen? Well, maybe.... Happy Holidays in the meantime."

column4: Chiefs have no chance at all!

column1: Let's get behind our Chiefs, now. Yes, the Colts are highly favored. And, yes the Colts have one of the best passing trios in the NFL (Manning, Wayne and Harrison). Yes, the Colts have hungered they will win a Super Bowl. Yes, everyone is expecting a slaughterhouse tomorrow. Consequently, that Ike and Reverend/Congressman Cleaver are going with the national odds makers and saying that the Colts will advance and the Chiefs will come back home, empty-handed.

Not so fast, here. Isn't it true that the Chiefs won last Sunday and Tennessee, Denver, and Cincinnati lost? And isn't it true that Larry Johnson ran a close second in the final NFL rushing contest? And finally, isn't it true that seldom-celebrated, highly-underrated Eddie Kennison comes through somehow, some way? Bottom line: the Chiefs are due to be a winner tomorrow. In short, the Chiefs have some strengths too. And those strengths have saved us from Playoff distinction, just like they did against Jacksonville.
So forget the past and lean not on own understanding. Enough said.

column1: ## BLACK ON BLACK CRIME IN CHICAGO

I hate it when we have to talk about black coaches playing opposite black coaches. It is as if there is some kind of Cinderella brotherhood that will magically turn the game into a last-minute tie or even a win for both teams.

It’s like the NFL should change its rules . . . when black coaches play each other. Remember, all the hoopla, surrounding the last Super Bowl when Lovie Smith and the Chicago Bears played Tony Dungy and the Indianapolis Colts. Well, this week, Herm “The Firm” Edwards will play against Lovie Smith. Both black and both with stellar reputations.

But, Lovie has the better team. He’s got a quarterback. Herm don’t. He’s got a defense. Herm don’t. Lovie can go several deep. Herm can’t. Whatever the match, Lovie will kick the devil out of Herm and his Chiefs. It will be “black on black crime”. And at the end of the game, Herm will meet Lovie at the center of the field. They will whisper something in each other’s ears. And security will lead them in opposite directions.
And the scoreboard will still read Chicago 16, Kansas City Chiefs 17."

# Kansas City Chiefs vs. Green Bay Packers

column5: Too much Brett, not enough Bowe!

column1: **Too Far, Too Fast, With Too Little!**
The Chiefs' fans have gone wild. They have won four games and they act like Kansas City is going to the Super Bowl.

Hey, wait a minute! And don't take me too far, too fast, with too little. It is barely at the half-way point in the season and these next three games will tell determine if the Chiefs are ""for real"" or just pretenders.

Starting with Green Bay on Sunday, the Chiefs will play Denver and then Indianapolis. Everyone in the Monday Nite Footballers know the Chiefs won't beat the Colts. But, opinion varies when it comes to Green Bay. The Haters, led by Ike and Ike Booker think that Brett Favre is too much for the Chiefs to handle, even at home.

And here's some news. . . Congressman Bubba Cleaver has joined up with The Haters, after witnessing how Favre wiped out Denver last Monday night. But, the Thinkers of the Group believe the Chiefs have a chance. Carl, Dr. Allen, Lloyd, and Ed are going with the Chiefs. Now, the ""Thinkers"" study the game and have concluded that the Chiefs can be real contenders, with a few breaks. They earnestly believe the Chiefs have developed a solid defense and a surprising passing offense, all leading to unexpected victories.

This emerging pass offense includes the new ""Otis Taylor"". . Dwayne Bowe. He is a star about to shine and we all have zeroed in on his big-time playmaking ability. But, I'm with the Thinkers. . . the Chiefs can and will beat Green Bay.
In the meantime, I repeat. . . ""Don't take me too far, too fast, with too little""."

By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

Each week for the last 40 years, Monday Nite Footballers have met and discussed football. Mainly about the Chiefs because it was in the late ‘60s, when the Chiefs were in their last and only heyday. In fact, the Chiefs have not made it to the Super Bowl since 1969.

That memorable victory over Minnesota put Kansas City on the map. Otis Taylor, Glouster Richardson, Tank Holmes, Buck Buchanan and Bobby Bell became local and world-wide celebrities. And each of us vicariously enjoyed the hype. Funny thing though. If you are under 50, you probably don't even remember those days and for you all, the Chiefs have stunk, as long as you have known about them.

Add to the frustration, the Chiefs were the backbone of the AFC West. Ironically, each of their division partners have been to the Super Bowl. . . since the Chiefs have. Oakland, San Diego and Denver, all have been there and won. And yet, the Chiefs, then the most innovative team in football, hardly can earn any kind of victory. . . anywhere. And it is sad for us old guys, who once went to Arrowhead, in the rain, in the snow, in the cold. Today, only Ike and Dr. Allen still go with any regularity. Moreover, our one-channel conversations have drifted to “Search For Tomor-

Yes, New England will go undefeated all the way. And yes, it will be between Dallas and Green Bay in the Super Bowl against the Patriots. And yes, there will be no black coaches vying for the coveted ring, this year. So, all we have to talk about is the draft, come April. And you know, Ike and Carl are demanding that the Chiefs draft an offensive linemen. With Shields and Roaf gone, the front line must receive priority. And after that, draft some cornerbacks. Both Surtain and Law are Medicare-eligible and need to move up to assistant coaches in some middle school. After that, we don't care who the Chiefs draft.
Bottom line, we hope the Chiefs get back to the Super Bowl, before we die. After all, we don't have that much longer to even remember. Go Chiefs in 2008."

column1: Ike is President of the Haters. He is just that way. . . old, cantankerous, forgetful and just plain ornery.

When Priest Holmes got hurt, Ike asserted that Priest was ""washed up"". Earlier this year, Priest reported to football camp in River Falls. Ike then asserted that it was all a joke. Priest, surprisingly to some, made the team. Ike then said Priest will never play. Now, that Larry Johnson is hurt, Priest is resurrected to start on Sunday. He will rise on the third week and save the Chiefs' first-place standing. I'm not sure what Ike is going to say when Priest takes the Chiefs to the Playoff-Promised Land.

Look for him on the other end of the many screen passes and at the end of the game, he'll be carried off the field by his peers. We will all sing ""When the Chiefs go marching in"" or maybe we'll hum ""Mine Eyes Have Seen the Glory of the Coming of the Lord"". But Ike and his grandson, sit right behind the Chiefs' bench, will probably say that Priest just got lucky. And just to show you how ornery he really is, he predicted the Chiefs will win (which is a rarity). While the crowd and commentators praise 'ol Priest, Ike will probably say that Priest was lucky. That's Ike . . . just old, cantankerous, forgetful, and just plain ornery. Won't even give a Priest a break."

# Kansas City Chiefs vs. San Diego Chargers
Stick a fork in the Chiefs; they are done!
Chiefs are done for the rest of the year!
In the words of James Brown. . . please, please, please!
The Firm: Smith, Hali, Allen, and Bowe!
Chiefs will be struck by the lightning bolt!
Dr. Charles Allen, a well-respected dentist in our city, is most noted for his painless procedures. His professional techniques are remarkable, complemented by an unusually well-trained office staff.

But, Dr. Allen said something this week, about a coaching change. And amid all the hoopla, I failed to get more detail. Was he asserting that there is going to be a coaching change at Arrowhead? How does he know? If his credibility in football circles is the same as his professional credibility, we all had better listen. Since he is not near, let's specu-late. Ok, the Chiefs lost to lowly Oakland last Sun-day. That was the last straw for many. And where has this Louisville Slugger running back been? You mean to say that we had this kind of backup and we resurrected Poor Priest! And he actually gained 150 yards with our Cinderella-laced offensive line! Wow! Who could the venerable Dr. Allen be talk-ing about?

For now, we have to contend with a dilettante football team, called the Kansas City Chiefs. In the meantime, keep watching Mizzou, New England, Dallas, and the Colts to nourish our pain-free, need to be on a winning team."

column1: # The Chiefs, St. James, And Ten Dead Flies
Ike, Carl, and Ed all attend St. James United
Methodist Church. In fact, when it comes to activi-
Now what does that fact have to do with a
bunch of old men talking about football every
Monday night. Unlike most other Monday nights,
the conversation, chatter and commentary have
slowly drifted away from The Chiefs. That's
because the locals keep losing and it is senseless
to keep boarding a sinking ship. Thus, these chat-
ters are scrambling to find something good to talk
A frustrated minister, Ike out-of-the clear-blue
asked if New England would go undefeated in
2007? Unlike Carl, Ike, and Ed quickly said that the
Patriots would certainly go unbeaten. Every one
else, including Greenfield, Booker and Dr. Allen,
felt that New England would lose at least one
game. Ideally, they all would be chatting about the
Chiefs if it weren't for this tag-team we call the
Chiefs. But, like so many other fans, these Monday
Niters have switched their conversational loyal-
ties. This time to arguably the best team ever in
the history of the National Football League. And
that Sunday, the no-loss Patriots will take on the
Pittsburgh Steelers, a perennial contender. It will
be ""the"" game-to-watch. Brady will be Brady and
somehow, just like against Baltimore, the Patriots
will emerge at 13-0. For the record, all of these
guys, except Dr. Allen and me, think that Broncos
will annihilate the Chiefs on Sunday in Denver.
And then Sunday evening just after ""60 Minutes,""
they will all go back to talking about St. James or
something else that is positive. That's because the
Chiefs ain't worth ten dead flies. Go Patriots, go!"

ENTITIES: Ike Gardner, Paul Booker, Ed West, Carl Clark, Lloyd Greenfield, Congressman (Bubba) Cleaver, Mike Solari, James Saxon, Charlie Joiner, Herm Edwards, Carl Peterson, Dick Vermeil, Dr. Allen, San Diego Chargers, Indianapolis Colts, New England Patriots
Even Ike (Gardner) and that (Paul) Booker did not believe the Chiefs would lose 12 games.
Those ""two"" plus Ed West and Carl Clark thought the Chiefs would break even at 8-8.

Then, Lloyd Greenfield and even Congressman (Bubba) Cleaver joined the Haters, and began to predict their irreversible demise, especially after losing to lowly Oakland. And just think, they finally listened to the Monday Niters and got rid of (Mike) Solari, the offensive, offensive coordinator.
And to remove all vestiges of a winning, (Dick) Vermeil era, they erased (James) Saxon and (Charlie) Joiner as assistants.

Now, ol' Herm (Edwards) and (Carl) Peterson will have their own draft and their own coaches. In plain English, they slickly blamed the disastrous 4-12 season on the assistant coaches, especially those that worked for the ""town crier,"" Vermeil.

So, that means ol' Herm and Emperor Carl will be in total charge of everything, alone and uninfluenced. So, we, as fans, can now hold them fully responsible for all that happens or doesn't happen in 2008. Reluctantly, let's give this ineffective duo another chance.

Now, we all start out as fools and become wise through experience. If that African proverb is true, both Carl and Herm ought to share the King Solomon Wisdom award next year. Maybe, they can make Dr. Allen's 11-5 prediction more likely.

All jokes aside, 2007 is all over for Kansas City and now we must all throw our support to the San Diego Chargers and the Indianapolis Colts. That's because the New England Patriots are about to go all-the-way.
See ya on Super Bowl Friday."

# 'Monday Night Footballers' Hold Annual New Year's Party

With fun, festivity and food, the ""Monday Nite Footballers"" 1981 annual New Year's Eve party went off ""with a big bang."" Over 250 persons jammed the Barcelona and Mediter-ranean meeting rooms at the Glenwood Manor, 91st and Metcalf.
Once there, the guests were treated to the finest food, the best drink and the best music possible. There was enough of everything for everybody and once started, the party lasted till around 4 a.m.
There was no space on the dance floor.

The dinner, featuring turkey and brisket, salads and blackeyed peas, was the high point of the even-ing. Two large bars, operated by members of the group, served ""spirits"" to move the festivities all night right into New Year's Day.
With horns, whistles and traditional hats, the group wished everyone in Kansas City a prosperous New Year and hoped that those present will again join them in 1982.
Group members are Charles Allen, D.D.S.; C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Ollie Hubbard, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner, Jr., James T. Nunnelly, Jerry Price, Ed West and Thomas Brown."

TITLE: Monday Nite Footballers Commentary / The Road To The Super Bowl

ENTITIES: Super Bowl XX, Jim Nunnelly, San Francisco, New York Giants, L.A. Raiders, Chicago Bears, Dallas, Miami, New England, Cleveland, L.A. Rams, C. Ashley, Charles Allen, Tom Brown, Paul Booker, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jerry Price, Ed West
Editor's Note: San Francisco was eliminated, losing to the New York Giants on Sunday, Dec. 30.

Before each Superbowl playoff, the Monday Nite Footballers have a vote to predict something about the Super Bowl Game. In keeping with that tradition, this year the Monday Niters met on Saturday to predict the final two teams that would meet in Super Bowl XX, all before the playoffs actually begin.
The analysis of the predictions show quite a bit of variation among the Monday Niters. After watching football for sixteen weeks, you would think there would be some general agreement.
Before we look at how each individual predicted, let's look at the overall picture on Super Bowl XX, according to the Monday Niters:
1. Founding members Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner Jr., and Ollie Hubbard all differed on the “final two.”
2. In total, the group felt the final two would be LA Raiders and San Francisco (already eliminated).
3. The Chicago Bears, despite their runaway regular season, was the groups second NFC choice, behind San Francisco. The Bears were also in the playoffs last year.
4. The LA Raiders (Oakland) was selected as the AFC's top choice receiving six of the 10 votes. This is the fourth straight playoff year for the Raiders.
5. Dallas was mentioned by two members, despite the fact Dallas missed the playoffs last year.
6. Miami received four of the 10 votes for the AFC representation to the ""Main Event"". This is the fifth consecutive playoff year for the Dolphins.
7. No member voted for the New England Patriots, the New York Jets, Cleveland Browns of the New York Giants.
# The Road To The Super Bowl
Now let's look at the Super Bowl XX as predicted by each members.

What other team used a 300-plus pound offensive lineman as a back in the National Football Conference Championship game in 1984. (Two weeks ago the Bears used William Perry as a running back and the refrigerator man made a touchdown.)

Guy McIntyre, a San Francisco 49ers offensive guard was used as a blocking back against the Bears in the 1984 National Football Conference Championship game. Ironically, the game was against the Bears, who two weeks ago used 300-pound Perry to run a touchdown.

1. Gary Anderson, newly acquired San Diego running back scored 49 touchdowns while playing the USFL franchise Tampa Bay. Anderson is a native of Columbia, Mo. and was an all-stater from Hickman High School.

2. George Brett is not a defensive first baseman; he is definitely a hitter. His goal is to get the Golden Glove Award. Frank White, Royal's second baseman, has received a total of five Golden Glove Awards.

3. Texas has an unusual high school rule. Players must pass each course in order to play interscholastic sports. It is called the ""no pass, no play"" rule. It's reportedly the only state to enforce such a rule.

4. After the Royals's World Series victory Sunday night, President Reagan talked with George Brett, Bret Saberhagen, Dick Howser and Ewing Kaufman, Majority owner. Interesting that Frank White, George Orta, or Darryl Motley did not chat with the President over the telephone.
Members: Charles A. Allen (DDS); C. Artis Ashley; Paul Booker, Tom Brown; Carl Clark; Ike Gardner; Ollie Hubbard; Jim Nunnelly; Jerry Price; and Ed West."

# FINAL LEAGUE STANDINGS AND OVER-ALL RECORDS

1. After the Chiefs loss on Sunday to Pittsburgh, the group was split as to whether John Mackovic would retain his job as Head Coach after this season. Five felt he would be fired after this season and five felt he would have at least one more year. However the group unanimously felt a coaching change should occur after this years lackluster 7-3 season.
2. The group again recognized the winning ways of the Chicago Bears, now 10-0 in the NFL. Steve Fuller, former Chiefs quarterback, ran two touchdowns against Minnesota last Sunday.

3. The group remembered the entertainment businesses owned by members of the Kansas City Chiefs Super Bowl team of the late sixties and early seventies. To name a few, there was the “End Zone”, the “Flankers”, the “Instant Replay”. Can you name the fourth?

4. The group remembered former quarterback, Dennis Marlin, “The Magician” Briscoe, who after being waived by Buffalo, went on to become a starting wide receiver for the Miami Dolphins, then a frequent playoff and Super bowl participant.

5. Ike Gardner was joined by his fellow Monday Niters suggesting that the Monday Niters review those black quarterbacks of the National Football League. At some future date our column may include such a commentary.
Please let us hear your views.

TITLE: Monday Nite Footballers Commentary: Sam Cofield: Pushing, Without A Cry
ENTITIES: Jim Nunnelly, Sam Cofield, InterScholastic League, Kansas City, Mo., NCAA Division I, Frances Cofield, Lincoln (Academy) High School, Metro Selection, Sumner high school
Not the cry, but it’s the flight of the wild duck that leads the flock to fly and follow. Let’s see how that old Chinese proverb applies to Sam Cofield.

First, his flight. For three years now, he’s been the Interscholastic League Coordinator for the Kansas City, Mo., school district. And with declining school enrollments and tougher academic standards, he faces the most critical times of inner city high school athletics.

He reads daily where some district or some state has followed the trend and set tougher grade stand ards for the high school athlete. Proposition 48 al- ready says that no fresh- man can enter a NCAA Division I school unless he or she has made a 2.0 grade point average and scored 700 on a nationally recognized test. Wow, he says without a cry. He’s real careful not to push too hard when he talks about this. No excitement, just calm.

He’s also trying to pre- pare his league and school district to be a leader – not a follower – in accepting this well-entrenched trend. Everyone, everywhere is pushing for tougher aca- demic standards for ath- letes.
But, it’s not only the ath- letes he has to convince of this trend, there’s the nine schools, the fans, the community, the school board. And, in particular, there’s the coaches.

The coaches are already implementing programs to meet this 2.0 GPA standard before August, 1986. That’s good. And Sam is proud and cautious about this. He knows that it is only a step in the right direction. It’s also difficult to do where the coaches are the lowest paid of all the surrounding districts.

So, at this point, he’s not willing to push for a “no pass, no play” rule, where the athlete must pass all courses before being allowed to participate. In- stead, he wants to proceed gradually toward a tougher grade point - say 2.3 - and allow a student to fail maybe one course. Not quite like Texas, but Cofield understands that a thou- sand mile journey starts with just that one step.
Those who know Cofield say he’s quiet and calm but effective. He boasts that the coaches are al- ready implementing pro- grams to meet this trend.

He wants the community, particular groups like Mon- day Nite Footballers, to “watch” the progress. And, help, by supporting tutoring programs – to assure that the athletes do well in the classroom. He wants the Interscholastic League to be a good example.

Speaking of examples, let’s now take a look at Sam Cofield, himself. Be- sides being a preacher’s son and married to Frances Cofield, Sam is a “quiet go getter”—up and at work by 6:45 a.m. and doesn’t stop until 10 p.m. Always at a sports event, some- where near 121 nights a year.

Beginning as a practice teacher at Lincoln (Acade- my) book in 1966, he has steadily progressed up through the system. The next year he became a coach. Later, the Athletic Director and then, in 1983, he took on the issues-laden position he now holds – Interscholastic League Coordinator. So he knows “his word” from the bot- tom up – that word, ex- ample.

Now, the most important force that guides Cofield is that he was a star athlete who did well in the class- room. In 1961, he was an All-Metro Selection at Sumner high school in Kan- sas. Most important he was on the honor roll. And at Lincoln university, he was on the basketball team, until a back injury crippled his career. But, he was a honor student, there, too - talk about an example.

And Cofield knows what the athletes, the coaches, the teachers, are all faced with – when a nation de- cides it wants its athletes to be good students as well. He’s been each of them, at one time or another. Further, he knows it’s real- ly possible to get tougher. He know it’s just a short time when it’ll be imple- mented – everywhere, and just in the big schools. And, he’s telling everybody that – quietly, with graceful articulation, and without a cry.
Not the cry, but it’s the flight of the wild duck that leads the flock to fly and follow. Good luck, Sam."

# Monday Nite Footballers Treat 'Sweethearts' To Candlelight Dinner

Last Saturday evening, the Monday Nite Footballers entertained their sweethearts at the Alameda Rooftop restaurant on the Country Club Plaza. Seated at the two center-most tables, the group easily became the main attraction in the 280-seat facility.

Before the delightful dinner, the ladies were treated to cocktails in the Rooftop Lounge, overlooking the well-lighted Country Club Plaza. The lounge and restaurant have been a favorite spot for visitors and Kansas Ciians for the past fourteen years.
Michael Turner, manager of the restaurant, stated, ""It was a delight serving such an illustrious group.""

The group tries' to treat the sweethearts to a ""night out"" in return for their many support activities. The sweethearts have been instrumental in supporting the group with parties, community events, fund raising, and weekly hosting activities.
It goes without saying that the ladies and men were dressed for the occasion. The event usually is held around Valentine's Day."

# Horace Peterson: Questions And Answers For A Black Sports History
There's a man in the old fire station and he's got a lot of old stuff up there. Let's go hear what he has to say. Who's the man, what's he got and what's he talking about?

Well, the man is Horace Peterson and the old fire station is the Black Archives of Mid America Building located at 2033 Vine. Some of the old stuff is information and exhibits about black sports; and he has a whole lot to say about it. Let's look at all four topics — up close — starting with the man.

Horace M. Peterson, 1964 Central high school grad-uate, accompanied Martin Luther King to Selma, Ala. in 1965 and marched in Washington in 1963. He's a jogger, television show producer, and 22-year member of Freedom, Inc. He was also a relay runner of Central's State Cham-pionship track team in 1964. Peterson is married to Barbara Peterson and the father of four children. That's Horace-in facts.

But that's not all there is to Peterson. It's what he says and what he does that puts the ""excitedly serious"" smile on his face. For 13 years, he's been the executive director of the Black Archives.

Now, about the old fire station building. Built in 1911 during the old Pen derghast period, the two-story structure was built by ""WPA"" hands. It's fitting that it would now house Peterson and his adminis trative assistant, Deborah Jones, and tons of black ""memories."" There is even more up close. Now, let's see what's there.

On those two floors and in the garage are lots of information, pictures and exhibits, particularly about black sports. There's O.J. Simpson's first pro jersey, number 32 for Buffalo, donated by Woody Smallwood, a former member of the Negro New York Yankees and now a repre sentative for Miller Brew ing Company. Now, how about that!

Then, there's information on the old Kansas City Buf faloes, a semi-pro football team of the late fifties. Baseball fans will like all the information about the Kansas City Monarchs and the Kansas City Clowns. These items were donated by Arrington ""Bub Ble"" Klice.

And now Horace turns the excitement up. He's talking seriously now — and proud. There's an exhibit featuring a black big game hunter in town. And, Horace has his collection right there in the old fire station.
And there's more, too much to mention here. Now, Horace quickens gracefully; and like a seasoned pro and begins to talk about the whole subject — fewer facts, more com mentary.
What does the man at the old fire station say about all these exhibits and pictures? He speaks slowly now — for emphasis.

It all starts to ""come alive"". He has a focus on black baseball — the Monarchs, the Clowns, Satchel Paige, Woody Smallwood, Jackie Robinson, the 1924 Black World Series, Frank White. It's here that his ""militant approach to his tory"" flourishes. ""We've been jammed,"" he states. Baseball meant a lot to blacks here in Kansas City and Horace is a little mad about it all.

He wants to know why they didn't name the Royal's baseball stadium after Satchel Paige? Why doesn't ""homegrown"" Frank White have a life-time contract with the Royals? Why is there a ""special"" section in Base ball's Hall of Fame for the old Negro Leagues? Why don't people know that major league baseball for blacks was a natural out-growth of the old Negro Leagues?

Maybe the reasons aren't so obvious, Horace. And, maybe, that's why Black Archives is important — to tell us. And maybe that's why Monday Nite Foot ballers are writing about you and your work here in this column. And maybe one day the many questions will have answers.

And those answers will be housed over at the ""old fire station at 2033 Vine."" And those answers will be another step of progress in the racial struggle. And people will say — ""Old Horace Peterson did his part.""

TITLE: Monday Nite Footballers Commentary: Debi Thomas: Falling or Rising?...
ENTITIES: Debi Thomas, Katerina Witt, Calgary Winter Olympics, Stanford

Editors Note: The comments in this column were rendered by the sports-minded wives of the Monday Night Footballers. While each wanted to remain anonymous, the reason for including them in this particular column is obvious.

Did Debi Thomas fall or did she rise? That is a big question and it always, history will judge as. Before we answer that ques- tion, let’s look back to that Saturday night, when a Su- per-Bowl like audience watched Debi in the Calgary Winter Olympics. The tension. The disappoint- ment. The dignity.

Everybody knew that Debi Thomas was tense, some even say that she just plain “froze up.” Especially since she was the leading competitor and was to perform last. Many believe this was the cause of her eventual defeat. The athletic anticipation turned into neurotic waiting and finally emotional exhaustion. After all, the eyes of the entire world awaited her arrival, particularly after the cautious perfor- mance of her ever-present rival, Katerina Witt.

That night at Calgary, history was made with an ebony “soul on ice.” Debi took to the paradise-like pavement, knowing that she had to beat not only Witt, but a Japanese young- ster and a homegrown Ca- nadian. All had performed above and beyond expec- tations.

Now, quickly to the disappointment. Within a minute from her beginning, an unsteady turn flawed her hopefully-perfect, long program. And after the second flaw, we all knew that her hopes for the top medal had fallen and faded. And as they showed the Thursday night before, the restrictively strict judges would reduce her score from gold to bronze. The disappointment was there. And we all felt it.

Yes, the much-publicized ice goddess had fallen. And the “Star Spangled Ban- ner” would not play for Debi. And at last, the re- alization of having lost be- gan to slowly set into her face. Debi, her gender, her country, third world na- tives, and a whole race of hopeful blacks shared the disappointing fact that the possibility of a black Olympic figure skating champion would have to be delayed to another point. Another time. Dignified and astonished, she searched for a way out. She searched for the right words to describe the long awaited, fought-for dream that had slowly slipped away. Not in Calgary. Not in 1988. Not in the Olympics.
A way out. Oh, no. She didn’t really need a way out. Debi Thomas has won not only a bronze medal, but gained the hearts and respect of all the world ath- letes and spectators alike.

Regaining her stately, slightly-girlish smile, one neither explained nor com- plained. And unlike so many athletes, she was prepared for the day when her athletic prowess, phys- ical grace, and the hand clapping crowd would not be a part of her everyday world. In her head, she hung up the figure skating shoes and reached for the long, white, physician only coat. Though we didn’t ac- tually see this transition
could see her accepting the victorious defeat and mentally preparing her- self for the next challenge. Not an athletic challenge, but an academic one.

After the now-famous, whisper-like, “I’m sorry,” Debi mentally slipped from the ice back to studying medicine at Stanford. She neither explained nor com- plained. And unlike so many athletes, she was prepared for the day when her athletic prowess, phys- ical grace, and the hand clapping crowd would not be a part of her everyday world. In her head, she hung up the figure skating shoes and reached for the long, white, physician only coat. Though we didn’t ac- tually see this transition occur, we knew that she was about to become an- other star, this time in the classroom.
We salute you Debi for your many achievements in your short twenty year lifespan. Those on and off the ice. Most of all, we congratulate you for rising, every time you fell."

ENTITIES: Al Campanis, Jim Nunnelly, Jerry Price, Jesse Jackson, Joseph Lowery, Ewing Kauffman, Ike Gardner, Earl Butz, Charles Allen DDS, C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Walt Livingston, Jerry Price, Ed West, Mount Calvary, Watergate
What does Al Campanis have in common with Mount Calvary and Watergate?
Before we comment on that, let's look real closely at what he said to cause all the talk about major league baseball and its lack of ""front office color.""

Well, Campanis said that blacks do not possess ""the necessities"" to become field managers or front office material. For those of us with ancient memories, we know that there have been three black managers in baseball. So, what's the fuss?

Simply put, it is just inappropriate for anybody in any kind of position to say anything about the inadequacies or the inferiorities of blacks. We are still wondering what propels people ""in high places"" to speak so candidly about the always-sensitive subject of race. What inspiration could provoke the front office vice president of the most celebrated and most progressive team in baseball to make such an untimely, and insensitive statement?

Jerry Price is kind and usually tries to find some ""decent"" explanation. He attributes Campanis' comments to his age. After all, the former Dodger executive, is threescore and ten (70). However so, it is still hard to understand why someone would make such a statement, when others have been fired for doing the same. Any man of any age should know better.

Another aspect of this unfortunate mess was when Campanis added a little more insight to his topic. He not only spoke of the lack of ""necessities,"" he made matters worse by informing the audience that blacks couldn't float either—as if being able to swim is a requirement to be considered for the front office in baseball. And for all that, Ike Gardner says Campanis should have been fired, as it was with Earl Butz.

But from Japan to Jersey City, it has got everyone talking again—about affirmative action or the lack of it. Jesse Jackson is talking to the baseball commissioner. And Joseph Lowery, in town for the SCLC board meeting, talked to Royals' owner, Ewing Kauffman. And, it's working, too. And, pretty soon, all twenty six teams will flaunt the fact that they now have a black assistant field manager and director of community relations. And some of us will forget that all of this came from a 19th century, terribly-biased comment from an old man, nearing retirement. Something good from something bad.

And, that's what the name Al Campanis will have in common with Mount Calvary and Watergate. Something good came out of those events, too. Members of the Monday Niters are Charles Allen DDS; C. Ashley; Paul Booker; Tom Brown; Carl Clark; Isaac Gardner, Jr.; Ollie Hubbard; Walt Livingston; Jim Nunnelly; Jerry Price; and Ed West."

Rosemary Lowe, Delma Johnson, Icelean Clark, Anna Mae Towner, Lizzie Lewis, Florine Jones, Maureen O' Hara, neighborhood activists, were key participants in the planning and development of the nationally known **PROJECT NEIGHBORHOOD**. **Kansas City CALL Player Of The Week**

**Aaron Johnson**, senior tailback for Lincoln Academy, rushed for over 100 yards in leading Lincoln over Southwest. He ran for a 6-yard touchdown. He is the son of Don Johnson Sr. and Carol Johnson. Coach Jerry Price said of the 6'1"" 185 pound tailback, “He’s a nucleus of our ballclub. He can go both ways.”
1. **James Scott**, running back, Southeast, ran 53 yards for TD against Westport.
2. **Lew Coleman**, running back, Southeast, scored a touchdown against Westport.
3. **Jerome Tolliver**, defensive tackle, Lincoln Academy, 6'2"" 230 pounds had 13 tackles against Southwest. A good student, he is definitely a good college and pro prospect.

Because of the severe underfinancing of Interscholastic League, the Monday Nite Football group challenges all graduates and teachers to attend the Lincoln Academy Homecoming game against O’Hara on Sept. 28.

1. Monday Nite footballers feel that the lack of a pre-box instant replay cost Houston the game against Washington on Sunday. The Moon to Hill pass was definitely a touchdown. The referee ruled the pass out of bounds.
2. The group also was concerned that the Chiefs were not featured on ABC's Monday Nite football program, following the Thursday night victory over LA Raiders, 36-20.
3. While watching the Cleveland game on Monday night, the group remembered Leroy Kelly, former Cleveland running back of the 70’s."

Kansas City Chiefs vs. Seattle Seahawks.

column4: The phantom Chiefs will show up
Winning in spite of Jay Taylor

column1: 1. The Chiefs have won seven straight games against the Seahawks, and in that run, Kansas City is an amaz- ing +22 in give-take ratio ( three gives and 25 takes). This season, the Chiefs sit atop the NFL in turnover ratio (+12), takeaways (31) and fumble recoveries ( 21), an old Seattle favorite.
2. Tom Flores has an 0-5 record against the Chiefs as the Seahawks head coach, and is 9-13 overall.
3. R. Robb Thomas played with the Chiefs from 1989- 91, collecting 92 catches for 1,148 yards and seven TDs.

4. Seattle Seahawks snapped a six-game losing skid with a 22-21 home win over Tampa Bay. Of the Sea- hawks last six losses, only one ( San Diego, 35-15) was by more than a touchdown. Seattle got off to a fast start, winning three of its first four. The workhorse is RB Chris Warren, who leads the AFC with 1,043 yards and a lofty 4.8 yards per attempt.

5. The Chiefs pounded out a season-high 172 rushing yards, getting strong efforts from Allen ( 77 yards) and rookie RB Greg Hill ( 74 yards). Montana com- pleted 21 of 31 passes for 270 yards and two TDs.

2. The group remembered Charlie Bryant's Barbecue located on the Southwest corner of 18th and Euclid; Payne Coal Co. at 19th and Highland; Crawdad Stand at 18th and Paseo. This discussion is always led by Charles Allen, D.D.S., Lloyd Greenfield, Carl Clark, and Ollie Hubbard.
3. In the early forties during wartime, four Monday nighters sold The Kansas City CALL newspapers. The early entrepreneurs were Ollie Hubbard, Ike Gardner, Lloyd Greenfield and Carl Clark.

4. Born in San Diego, Cal., Marcus Allen played quarterback at Lincoln high school there. Junior Seau, Chris Mims and Leslie O'Neal lead the Chargers' defense. Marcus Allen leads the league with 14 touchdowns, sharing the honor with Jerry Rice and Andrea Rison. San Diego has lost three of their last five games.

5. The Full Employment Council, under the leadership of Clyde McQueen, has agreed to provide job skills training for those referred from the C.O.M.B.A.T. (Community Backed Anti-Drug Tax) Drug Court. The New C.O.M.B.A.T. drug Court is a cooperative effort between the Prosecutor's Office and the Circuit Court of Jackson County."

1. The Chiefs have posted three straight wins over the Seahawks at Arrowhead Stadium and have lost to Seattle one in the last 12 games played in Kansas City. The Seahawks lone victory during that span came in 1990, when LB Derrick Thomas set an NFL record with seven sacks, only to see then Seattle QB Dave Krieg toss a last second pass in a 17-16 Seahawks victory.

2. The Chiefs are 4-2 after six games for the fourth time in the last five seasons. The Chiefs will play just their third home game of the season and their first in nearly a month ( 16-0 loss to the L.A. Rams on 9/25).
3. Seattle has impressive victories over Washington ( 28-7); L.A. Raiders ( 38-9) and Pittsburgh ( 30-13) with losses to San Diego ( 24-10); Indianapolis ( 17-15) and Denver ( 18-9).
4. Chris Warren, vastly under-rated Seattle RB has been a 1,000-yard rusher in each of the past two seasons. He already has 504 yards and five TDs on 124 carries ( 4.1 avg.).
5. Chiefs WR Willie Davis has now caught at least one pass in 38 straight games, the fourth longest streak in team history.
6. The Van Horn Falcons are 6-0 and lead the Interscholastic League. Jerry Price, head coach, was formerly a Monday Nite Footballer.
7. A total of 32 agencies will share $900,000 prevention dollars starting this November. Among those agencies are the Ad Hoc Group Against Crime, Blue Hills Neighborhood and Neighborhood Alliance.

# Kansas City Chiefs vs. Miami Dolphins
Monday, Dec. 12, Joe Robbie Stadium, 8 p.m.

column4: Peterson had a talk with Marty

column1: 1. The Miami Dolphins lead the AFC East with an 8-5 record, but, like the Chiefs, have lost three of their last five. Miami possesses the NFL's most productive offense, generating 384.5 yards per game. Their passing attack, led by strong-armed QB Dan Marino, is the AFC's top-ranked passer (88.0 rating).

2. LB Derrick Thomas was born in Miami, where he was a standout prep linebacker and tight end at South High school. DT Darren Mickell was also born in Miami, just a few blocks from Joe Robbie Stadium. He was a first team all-state lineman at Miami Senior High school and won the Nick Kotys award as Dade County's top prep player in '88.
3. Marty Schottenheimer was a fourth-round draft choice of Don Shula, then head coach of the Baltimore Colts. Schottenheimer signed with the Buffalo Bills of the AFL.
4. Kansas City has score just 10 points in the first quarter of the last 10 outings, while Miami has managed a lowly 12 points in the opening period of all 13 contest.

5. The Chiefs are 10-7 against the Dolphins during the regular season and 0-2 in post season play. Kansas City has been victorious in three of the last four regular season meetings, but lost the last encounter, a 30-10 setback last year in Miami.

**Off Season Thoughts From Monday Nite Footballers**
* Next week, the Mon-day Niters will try their hand at predicting the out-comes of the NCAA cham-pionship. The group will convene at Carl Clark's house and watch the show-down in Charlotte, N.C.

* Ike Gardner, well-known critic, has yet to recognize the good fortune of the Missouri Tigers' bas-ketball team. Should the Tigers remain successful throughout this weekend, they could reach the final four for the first time.

The after-school program will feature math and reading skills as well as substance abuse resistance skills.

Editor’s Note: So far this season, the Monday Nite Footballers have predicted 7 of 8 games, for a percentage of 88%.
# KANSAS CITY CALL PLAYER OF THE WEEK

David Cobblns, Paseo running back, scored five touchdowns and gained over 200 yards in leading Paseo to a 56-0 victory over Northeast. A massive back, Cobblns sports a 5'10"", 263 pound frame. He is considered to be a fine college prospect.
Phil Taylor, Lincoln Academy quarterback ran two touchdowns and passed for another in leading Lincoln to 28-6 victory over Westport.

1. Representatives from the Monday Nite Footballers attended the NAACP welcoming reception for the Superintendent of Schools, Dr. Claude Perkins. The reception was held at Palestine Baptist church on Saturday, Sept. 21.

2. Each member and their guests will attend the Lincoln Academy Homecoming game against O'Hara on Saturday evening. The group has issued a challenge to all graduates and teachers of Lincoln to attend for the next few months. The group has undertaken an effort to increase awareness of Interscholastic sports.

1. The group praised Eric Dickerson, LA Rams running back for his performance against Seattle Seahawks Monday evening. He ran for 149 yards on 31 carries. Dickerson broke the NFL single season rushing record last season.

2. The group complimented Coach Jerry Price, and his Lincoln Academy Blue Tigers for its fine start at 3-0. Particularly noteworthy is that Coach Price started the season with just over 20 players. Some players were lost because they failed to meet the 2.5 grade point required at Lincoln Academy (Ike Gardner.)
3. At least one member felt the Chiefs would regain a winning status and beat Seattle this Sunday at Arrowhead. Game time is 12:00 noon. (Ed West).

4. The group also felt that poor play selection was the main reason for the Chiefs loss to Miami last Sunday. The most memorable poor call was the fake field goal in the first quarter, resulting in a loss of three points. The fact is that the Chiefs have the best field goal kicker in pro football, Nick Lowery. What a shame! (group).
5. In a high scoring affair last Sunday, Cincinnati and San Diego scored 85 points, with San Diego gaining the edge 44-41. No defense, it was all offense. (Carl Clark).
6. One member does not like the ABC Monday Nite football trio of Frank Gifford, O.J. Simpson and Joe Namath (Charles Allen). Lets watch the ratings.
7. One member recognized the continual Big Eight college football supremacy by citing Nebraska's win over top rank Illinois (Ollie Hubbard).
MEMBERS of the Monday Nite Footballers are:
Charles Allen (DDS), C. Artis Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner, Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnely, Jerry Price and Ed West."

# **Instant Replay: Second Look, Second Chance, Or Second Opinion**

While only a few people know that violent crime went down 15 percent in Kansas City, everyone knows that the cry for instant replay in the National Football League is up this year. And, with the Super Bowl coming up next Sunday, there'll even be more talk about how non-officiating affects the outcome of any game. Let's look at some calls, then the officials, the decisionmakers and finally our opinion.

First, a look at the calls. On any Sunday, 60 million fans can see that a player stepped out of bound before crossing the goal line. Call touchdown. And, if you're at Arrowhead, the giant replay scoreboard can detect interference or a flagrant personal foul. No call or penalty. And, even though San Diego changes are in the Super Bowl, few believe they would have gotten there without some help. The officials.

What about these seven guys in control of a multi-billion dollar industry. They don't work full time. They load up every week from all over and run this program called the NFL. They're insurance agents, doctors, former high school coaches and millionaire executives. Some step up from the college ranks and still fewer, come from the Canadian and now-defunct world football league. And, for this part-time gig, they rake in around $2,000 per game. But, lately, there magnificent seven have invited the use of millions of fans, with their mistakes in judgement. And that sets off another discussion about instant replay.

Now, the owners, not the coaches, nor the players, have clearly stated, ""No instant replay."" The decisionmakers say it takes too long to decide, causing the game to go too long. And, of course, the longer the game, the less pleased the networks and football widows will be. And, of course, they say that instant replay isn't instant, sometimes delaying the game up to ten minutes or even longer. So, what do we say about the instant replay with Super Bowl Sunday, just around the corner?

Well, one way Ike Gardner wants instant replay to return as a permanent component of the game. This on again, off again talk is unnecessary and insulting to the fans. And, with technological advancements, both inside the networks, and inside the stadium, there is really no excuse to debating the ""pros and cons"" of instant replay. The time has come for the owners to listen to the fans and work out something to address the length of the game, at the same time, ensure playing calling accuracy.
The fans deserve a second look and second opinion and the officials need a second chance. As for more on the reduction in violent crime, call the Jackson County Prosecutor's Office."

column1: 1. Most Monday Niters think San Francisco and Pittsburgh will square off in the Super Bowl. However, Dr. Charles Allen thinks the Chiefs will represent the AFC and Tom Brown believes in the Cleveland Browns.

2. The sixth grade class at Central Middle school is writing an anti-drug prevention plan to assist younger children at McCoy Elementary School. Under the direction of COMBAT officials, the class is directed by Wendy McNitt. Sixth grade children are gaining work experience and each of the 26 are assigned work tasks.

3. Art Shell was named the ninth head coach in Raiders history on Oct. 3, 1989. The 47-year-old Shell has been the Raiders offensive line coach for seven years. He first joined the staff in '83 after 15 seasons as one of the finest offensive tackles in NFL history. He came to the Raiders in '68 as a third-round choice out of Maryland State, and went on to play in 207 league games and 24 play-off contests.

4. A win by the Chiefs this Saturday puts the team into the playoffs for the fifth straight year, which would be the longest current streak in the NFL. A victory would also give Kansas City its sixth straight winning season and the ninth in a row for Marty Shottenheimer.

5. RB Kimble Anders is 12th in the conference with 61 catches, the most by a Chiefs player since 1990 (WR Stephne Paige, 65). For the first time in club history, the Chiefs have five receivers with 35 or more catches ( Anders, WR Willie Davis, WR J.J. Birden, RB Marcus Allen and WR Lake Dawson).

6. Since Marty Shottenheimer's arrival in Kansas City in 1989, the series with the Raiders has been one-sided in the Chiefs' favor. Including a 1991 playoff win, Kansas City has been victorious in 10 of the 12 games played in the Shottenheimer era. Kansas City won a Week 10 game at Arrowhead, 13-3.

7. The Raiders own a slight 35-31-2 advantage over the Chiefs in the regular season series that got its start in 1960, the initial AFL season. The Chiefs have been victorious in eight of the last nine regular season games, sweeping the Raiders in 1990, 1991 and 1993. Kansas City has posted wins in three of its last four journeys to L.A. The Chiefs have won two of the three play-off meetings between the two clubs, winning the 1969 AFL Championship game ( 17-7) and a 1991 Wild Card game ( 10-6), while losing a 1968 Divisional play-off game ( 41-6). This is the second time in four years the two teams have finished the regular season with a game in L.A."

**4.** The Cleveland Browns have posted two straight wins over Houston (14-7) and Tampa Bay (22-6) after dropping their season opener to New England (17-14). WR Andre Rison, has only five grabs for the year. WR Andre Rison, has only five grabs for the past five seasons with the Atlanta Falcons.

**5.** Marcus Allen has 99 career rushing TDs, and needs one more for 100 in his career, a milestone reached by just three other players in NFL history: Walter Payton (110); Jim Brown (106); and John Riggins (104).

**6.** Webster Slaughter, a second-round pick of the Browns in the '86 draft, spent six productive seasons in Cleveland ('86 - '91), catching 305 passes (sixth in team history) for 4,834 yards (also sixth) and 27 TDs. He owns the Browns' record for most receiving yards in a season with 1,236 in '89.

**7.** Neil Smith has compiled 21 tackles, 2 1/2 sacks, three forced fum- bles, a fumble recovery and a touchdown in four games vs. Cleveland . L.B. Derrick Thomas had a career-high 13 tackles as a rookie vs. Cleve- land on 11/19/89.

UNDERAGE DRINKING will be the topic of a three-day conference at the Adam's Mark hotel on Sunday, Monday, and Tuesday. Those of you who want to attend on scholarship should call Jim Nunnelly at COMBAT at 881-3814. Nationally acclaimed speakers will share why drinking and smoking early can lead to more serious drugs and criminal behavior. This is a ""must"" for parents of adolescents.

THE CAROLINA PANTHERS, like the Chiefs, opened the season with a loss to the Redskins. Then, the Panthers beat the Atlanta Falcons and the San Diego Chargers. Previously injured quarterback, Kerry Collins, will be ready to give the youthful Chiefs another test. You will remember that Carolina, an expansion team, actually surprised everybody last year when they made the play-offs.

THE CHIEFS' YOUTHFUL DEFENSE is doing all right. Led by the linebackers Donnie Edwards, Fellom Daniels, and Anthony Davis, the Chiefs did real good against Buffalo at Arrowhead last Sunday. Matter of fact, Anthony Davis is the leading team tackler, having a good day against the Bills. And the untested and untried secondary has earned its stripes as well. Jerome Woods and Reggie Tongue are impressing the fans each week. And everybody is talking about the aggressive play of 145-pound ""Mighty Mouse"" McMillian, who intercepted the pass that beat Buffalo. And when you add in the consistent play of veterans James Hasty and Dale Carter, we have the best combined secondary in the AFC. Arguments are invited.

PRAIRIE VIEW AM&N, a traditional black college outside Houston, has lost another game. Now it's 63 in a row. But, don't be too hard on the Texas school; it has given many academic scholarships to struggling Kansas City high school graduates. Thanks to Leon Dixon and others."

column1: 1. The Chiefs are off to their first 4-0 starting franchise history and have now won 12 straight AFC West games, the third longest division win streak since 1970. The 1972-73 Dolphins and 1993-1994 Cowboys both had 14 game win streaks.

2. Chiefs Linebacker Derrick Thomas is tied for second in the NFL with six sacks. Cornerback Dale Carter and Safety Mark Collins both have two interceptions. The team's leading tacklers are Safety Brian Washington (30), Collins (26), Carter (24), Linebacker Tracey Simien (24) and Linebacker Anthony Davis (20).

3. One of the crime prevention objectives of COMBAT is to jail dangerous drug criminals. In 1995 over 1,700 drug trafficking cases were presented for prosecution as a result of investigative work by the Kansas City Police department's Narcotics and Vice Division.

4. Running back Marcus Allen continues to engrave his name among the game's immortals. Two weeks ago in Seattle, he bypassed Jim Brown (126) for second place on the NFL's all-time touchdown list; Allen now has 128. Only San Francisco 49er Jerry Rice has more with 157.

5. Failing in school is one of the seven early warning signs of drugs use and criminal behavior. COMBAT funds 35 prevention programs across Jackson County to address the seven warning signs. One such program designed to assist children is the W.E.B. Dubois Learning center. Call 523-3339 for more information.

6. San Diego Charger Safety Kevin Ross was a seventh-round draft choice of the Kansas City Chiefs in 1984 and played 151 games, 144 starts, in 10 years with the organization. While Kevin Ross wore Chiefs red, he made 826 tackles and 30 interceptions.

7. The Kansas City Chiefs sit alone atop the AFC West with their 17-14 victory over the Denver Broncos, (3-1). The San Diego Chargers, (3-1), posted an impressive 40-34 victory over the Chiefs' long-time rival Oakland Raiders, (1-3). That win put the Chargers just one game back of the Chiefs in the division standings.

Dr. Charles Allen is a prominent dentist here in Kansas City. Back in the fifties, he and his dad operated a chicken place at 18th and Vine. Later, he and his dad reopened the Birdland, a renowned jazz club from the old days in the jazz district. Thus, he is self reliant, self assured and definitely a self starter. Consequently, each week, no matter what the circumstances are, he picks the Chiefs to win. Even though the Chiefs' running game is inconsistent, Grbac will throw his predictable interceptions, Allen still goes with the Kansas City Chiefs. That's because he knows life is full of vicissitudes (predictable ups and downs), and if you stick it out, remain positive, and stay prayerful, things will work themselves out.

Just as Allen, Greenfield, Clark and West believed, the Chiefs are now back in the play-off race, standing at 7-5. They've watched the Chiefs' defense rise to the occasion time after time. They've seen Bam go back and forth and Derrick Thomas rise and fall like the holy Roman Empire. And on top of all that, they cringed at the start of every fourth quarter, knowing the Chiefs go erratic in the last stanza of each game. But still the Chiefs are in the play-off race and each of them think the Chiefs will find a way, again, to win this Sunday against Minnesota.

But, there's another side, the side that is represented usually by Ike Gardner and Paul Booker. Both were respected and very capable educators in the school district. In fact, many praise Ike and Booker for their effectiveness as educators, especially in the handling of children with special needs. However, when it comes to football, they assert the Chiefs are not beating play-off contenders, just beating up on lowly San Diego and falling needlessly to Chicago. They see the Chiefs as ""too iffy"" and think that the Chiefs will return to a 7-7 record, before a national, prime time television audience. For sure, these two venerable educators have reason to believe the way they do. That's because the Chiefs have consistently, game after game, let us down. And it's never a total victory when they do win. What's the point?

Again, the Chiefs will have their backs against the wall. Here we are, well into December, and we are trying to ""get there."" And to make it into the playoffs, we still have Pittsburgh, first place Seattle, and revengeful Oakland, waiting after Sunday. It's going to be hard and no one can tell for sure how it will at all turn out. Like Gladys Knight, somehow the Chiefs will make it through. So I'm with Charles, Carl, Greenfield, and West...the Chiefs will be 8-5 on Monday morning.

column1: **ABOUT THE CHIEFS:** Fresh from a respectable loss to the undefeated Denver Broncos, the Chiefs have to face the fact that 1998 will not be a winning season: 8-8 is the best the Chiefs will do, assuming we beat Dallas, the Giants and Oakland. Because of the consistent play of Rich Gannon, Bam Morris and Derrick Alexander, the Chiefs have scored 30 or more points in the last three games. Too little, too late, but refreshing. On the defensive side, linebacker Donnie Edwards, safety Reggie Tongue, and linebacker Anthony Davis led the team in tackles. All this has helped save a disastrous season. Most fans were set up to believe that the Chiefs were better than any team in the NFL. They were, but they collapsed somewhere and the objective now is to beat the Dallas Cowboys. And they will by 10 points.

**ABOUT THE DALLAS COWBOYS:** Dallas leads the NFC East by two games. Their 8-5 record includes two straight losses to New Orleans and the Minnesota Vikings. Michael Irvin is still the favorite target of veteran quarterback, Troy Aikman. And the evocative Emmitt Smith still is and will always be Dallas' most prolific runner. Currently, he has rushed for 1,113 yards and 11 touchdowns. Not bad for a strong running back on a struggling team. Defensively, tackle Leon Lett and end Greg Ellis will tamper with the renewed Chiefs offense, and last but not least, is Deon Sanders, the punt returner and cornerback extraordinary. He will be ""the show"" out at Arrowhead. Now dedicated to Christianity, Deon still is the fastest, most gifted and talented player of the Dallas Cowboys. He is one player who can do everything he says. That, alone, is worth going out to Arrowhead."

# Tampa Bay Buccaneers At Arrowhead Saturday

Following a victory last week over the lowly Tennessee Titans, the Chiefs will appear before the home crowd again on Saturday night, this time against Tony Dingy's Tampa Bay Buccaneers. Longtime NFL fans will remember the “dog days” when Tampa Bay started off in 1976...they went winless their first season and had lost consecutive games before winning just one. But that was the old Bucs.

Steve Young, star quarterback for San Francisco, has not always played for the 49ers. What two teams did he play for before becoming backup to Joe Montana in 1987? **ANSWER**: Young was a product of the United States Football League and played for the Los Angeles Express. Then, he played a stint with the Chiefs' opponent tomorrow, the Tampa Bay Buccaneers.

Listen tomorrow to Carl Boyd's Generation Rap on Hot 103 for the “Word From Grand Dad.” Though another word was promised on the show last week, this week's word will be inimitable. See how we use it in a tribute to Carl Boyd.
# Farewell To Carl Boyd At Friendship, August 27

Ruth Hill, vice president for The Art of Positive Teaching, announced a farewell event for Carl Boyd. Boyd and his wife “Wonderful Wanda” are moving to Phoenix at the end of the month. Thus, on Friday evening, Aug. 27, starting at 6 o'clock, friends and associates are encouraged to bid farewell to them at the Friendship Baptist church, near 35th and Van Brunt, just south of the Veterans Hospital. Boyd began teaching at J.S. Chick school back in 1980 and launched The Art of Positive Teaching consulting firm in 1986. He is well known throughout the country as a lecturer and educator. In 1986, he began the Generation Rap program and has nurtured it to a natural part of Kansas City's morning culture. Any other questions, call 363-5270.

We would not expect you to know the name, Shana Wheeler. She's not even in high school and the media doesn't usually chase after middle schoolers. Nonetheless, she's an up and coming basketball star from Ottawa, Kas. Already 5'11"", the eighth grader led the Kansas Belles to a national AAU tournament title in Springfield, Mo., recently. Seventy-two teams entered the under 12 meet, and Shana rose as one of the tournament's top scorers. Not only was she the sole African American on the team, she was the third tallest, yielding to two teammates just over six feet. She won praise from area coaches, after seeing her hold her own against older, more experienced high school players. Shana is the the niece of Monday Nite Footballer, Ed West.

Monday Niters will release their annual predictions for the Kansas City Chiefs in two weeks...without Sammie Sosa and Mark McGwire, both the Chicago Cubs and the St. Louis Cardinals would be hurting. Each team will exceed previous attendance records despite each posting very mediocre seasons...Bill Clark, president of the local Urban League, missed the annual national conference in Houston. He is recovering from an illness...COMBAT Drug Awareness Month activities continue with a picnic, honoring those who have maintained sobriety. It's all at Loose Park, starting at 2 o'clock on Sunday. All are welcome."
