It is apparent that the Chiefs still have problems with their offense. Gunther Cunningham says it's their offensive line. They've even tried to blame poor Bam Morris who was their only hope from last year's dismal display of professional football. The newcomers, Shehee and Cloud, are yet untented and can not even be counted on just yet. But, everybody in town knows the problem is in the quarterback, and particularly with Elvis Grbac. When Grbac threw the first interception in the Jacksonville game last Thursday, there was a collec tive sigh of disbelief. That's because no one can believe that we still believe in Grbac's ability to lead this offense. Even Warren Moon may not be the answer, for he is right up there with the ""Montang Medicare quarterbacks” -- too old, too slow, and resting on past performances. Why we continue to be a retirement center for “used up” quarterbacks is beyond reason. Any way, tonight the Chiefs will try to ignite their “midwest coast offense” by allowing Grbac to start the show. Look for another sigh of disbelief.
# **Greg Hill, The Real Deal Is In Detroit**

You have to remember Greg Hill. He’s the one we drafted and then sat him on the bench. He had speed and had set all kinds of rushing records in college. The Chiefs said he stumbled and was too fast for his feet. Any way, Hill was expected to fill the unfillable shoes of Marcus Allen and he just didn't do it. Now, after a short sting with the St. Louis Rams, he is now expected to replace the irre placeable Barry Sanders at Detroit. Let's all hope he will get a better chance with the Lions and with the right coaching and the right motivation. Hill could have the 1,000-yard season he never had here in Kansas City. Look for Hill's revenge when he comes to Arrowhead on September 26, dressed in ""motor city silver and blue.""
The venerable president of the Urban League, Bill Clark, is recovering well and is expected to return to duty on a limited basis soon. Clark has been ill for several weeks and is recovering at home.

Swinging"" Sammie Sosa is ahead in the homerun duel with Mark McGwire of St. Louis. The Cubs' outfielder has become known for his patented ""skip"" when he realizes that he has hit another ball out of the park. As of Wednesday morning, Sosa has hit 55 and is on pace to set another homerun record set by McGwire last year at 70. Keep on doing it, Sosa....Evander Holyfield and Lennox Lewis have set the rematch date for November 13. In their last contest, they fought to a controversial draw, quite unusual in heavyweight championship fights. It may be noted that Holyfield failed to wear his robe with the Bible verse in his last outing. The New Orleans Saints are hurting. Their number one draft choice, Rickey Williams, is still recovering. Three other potential starters are out and questionable for the season opener. Sep tember 12. Deion Sanders' toe is getting better and he is now expected to play opening day.

Next week, we will celebrate 30 years of predictions, football talk, and as a fan club organization. In fact, we may well be the oldest, organized fan club for the Kansas City Chiefs. More important, the same 10 guys have met each Monday night, watched football on television, and never have departed as friends. We have watched our kids grow up and become parents themselves. We have watched each other go from young Turks to mellowed middleagers and what's more, we've all become more health and family minded. As educa tors and community leaders, we assist with children and youth at all times. We are gravely concerned about the issues in public education, and will be watching the charter school development closely. As important, we want to do everything to insulate our children from the ravages of drugs, and will pro mote and support any organization that protects and develops our children. Thus, next week we'll reveal our predictions and opinions just like we have for the last three decades. Thanks for listening to us."

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions,"Ed West helps people select and install their personal computers. He has a knack for that kind of stuff. But he also is a decent prognosticator, for he, along with Lloyd Greenfield, Carl Clark, and the venerable Charles Allen picked the Chiefs to beat Oakland. ENTITIES: Jim ""Grand Dad"" Nunnelly, Ed West, Kansas City Chiefs, Denver Broncos, Charles Allen, Paul Booker, Carl Clark, Ike Gardner, Lloyd Greenfield, Chris Dishman, Mike Cloud, Elvis Grbac, Terrell Davis, John Elway, Ed McCaffrey, Shannon Sharpe, Derrick Alexander, Tony Gonzalez

Ed West helps people select and install their personal computers. He has a knack for that kind of stuff. But he also is a decent prognosticator, for he, along with Lloyd Greenfield, Carl Clark, and the venerable Charles Allen picked the Chiefs to beat Oakland. We who correctly picked Kansas City had doubts but thought the Raiders also had weaknesses and if our defense played well, and our running backs would run, then we had a chance. Both of those wishes came true. Chris Dishman earned Defensive Player of the Week honors for his fumble recovery and interception touchdowns. And out of the clear blue sky came a quick hitting running back named Mike Cloud who scampered for about 60 yards in eleven carries. Though these two weren’t the only heroes from last Sunday’s game, they represented what we’ve always said: When the defense scores and if we get a first-class running back, then the Chiefs will win consistently.

Thus, consistency should become the Chiefs number one objective. A team cannot win by defense alone. We still have not matured offensively. There’s our garage sale quarterback Elvis Grbac. Though he played okay against Oakland; we all cringed again when he threw his predictable interception. And we have to throw the ball far enough to reach the first down marker on third down. And for Pete’s sake, we have to find all the receivers, not just the primary one. Lastly, when will we get somebody to return kick-offs? Consequently, if we address these inconsistency issues, then we can talk about beating Denver in Denver.

As usual, Ike Gardner and Paul Booker thinks Denver will beat the Chiefs. both are retired, fussy, and cantankerous. And, they have a right to think the Chiefs will again be inconsistent and lose, especially up in the mountains of Colorado. But Denver is not the same Denver as they were a year ago when they wrapped up their second consecutive Super Bowl victory. Terrell Davis is out and of course Big Bad John Elway has retired and the Broncos have been indecisive about who is to replace him. Even though, they appeared crippled, the Broncos still have a lanky receiver named Ed McCaffrey. Not a Shannon Sharpe but a dangerously deceptive receiver who consistently gets open, no matter what the coverage. So, in addition to being consistent, we have to watch and cover McCaffrey. Even though Dishman played well this past week, he is not the one to cover McCaffrey. What’s the point?

Like always, the Chiefs will fumble and stumble along and they will make enough mistakes to make us all join Ol’ Ike in his cynicism. But they will also find a way to beat archrival Denver. It may be Shehee this week. Or it might be Derrick Alexander who will hold on to the ball. And maybe Tony Gonzalez may break one. And these unknown maybes are what makes Charles Allen, Carl Clark, Lloyd Greenfield, and Ed West think that the Chiefs will be 7-5 on Monday morning. I’m with the “maybe” guys.

Well, the critics are right....the Chiefs are not going to make the play-offs and face the reality of another losing season. But, the diehard fans are just as right...the Chiefs will not be the losingest team in the National Football League. Truthfully, the Chiefs are either the best of the worst or the worst of the best. Either way, they are 5-5 and it is no longer news that Sunday's game with the 5-5, Oakland Raiders may be in jeopardy as well. We say that for a number of reasons.

First, Rich Gannon will be trying to show the Chiefs that he really should be in Grbac's shoes, running our garage sale offense. Even Gunther Cunningham is rethinking his decision to go with ""concrete feet"" Elvis. Everyone knows Gannon put the fire in our dull, midwest coast offense. But that's not all. Secondly, Oakland is a longtime rivalry. In the older days, it didn't matter what Kansas City did, as long as they beat Oakland. Simply, the goodness or the badness of the season rested entirely on how we played against the ""silver and back."" Certainly, that feeling will come into play Sunday for sure. But there's another reason this game will be vitally important to both teams. Each is fighting for respect. Indianapolis, Miami, New England, Tennessee, Jacksonville, and even Buffalo are expected to be playing beyond January 2. Oakland nor the Chiefs are currently in that number, save for some unapparent miracle. The next six games are not shoe-ins for either team. Thus, each team faces the stark possibility of losing every single one of those games, replacing San Diego as the last place team in the AFC West.

Obviously that team would be tragic. And Ike Gardner, Paul Booker and Ollie Hubbard would enjoy having told us so. While Charles Allen, Carl Clark and Lloyd Greenfield would strongly disagree, they nonetheless know the Chiefs have one more opportunity before they give up entirely. Along with Ed West, these loyal, dedicated, diehard fans have been ridiculed week after week, believing in a team that has not proven itself to be effective or viable. So, the Chiefs must beat Oakland Sunday for these brave believers and the many of you out there who continue to wear red, no matter what. As for me, I am still a believer and you should look for the Chiefs to be 6-5 and well on their way, to re-earning and resurrecting their respect.

column1: **ABOUT THE CHARGERS:** San Diego is 4-6, just like the Chiefs. In fact, the loser of this game will have sole possession of the AFC West cellar. Under new coach, June Jones, the Chargers have beaten Baltimore and Philadelphia and lost to Denver and Seattle. Craig Whelihan is a so-so quarterback and he'll throw to two so-so receivers, Jones and Still. But, the real threat on the offense is big, bruising Natrone Means. If he plays, he will again test the Kansas City's weakening defense. Also, the Chargers will again showcase All-Pro linebacker Junior Seau. He'll be awesome, and so will Norman Hand, a big, big tackle.

**ABOUT THE CHIEFS:** This year's edition of the Chiefs is the worst under Schottenheimer and Peterson. In fact, Ed West says the Chiefs are the ""best of the incompetents."" Who's to blame for this demise? Is it Marty? Or Carl? Or Jimmie Raye, the offensive coordinator? Or is it free agency? For me, it's all about expectations and pre-season hype. We all knew Elvis was shaky at quarterback. And, we urged management to obtain a first-class running back. And everybody knew that many times our special teams received breaks that could have easily gone the other way. So relying heavily on last year's 13-3 record, Derrick Thomas, who is suspended for this Sunday's game in San Diego, and Jason Whitlock started us thinking about a 16-0 season. And once *Sports Illustrated* and *ESPN* started talking about going to the Super Bowl, it was natural for us to start talking about it, too. It was there that our disappointments originate. Ike, Harry, and Booker have been telling us the Chiefs are not ""all that."" In fact, Ole' Ike predicted 8-8 way back in August before the season began. While we are all sad, we should be thankful that we learned the truth soon enough to do something about it. Chiefs by three."

# BARRY NOT COMING TO ARROWHEAD ON SEPTEMBER 26

While most of the Kansas City area sports fans are just going bananas over George Brett’s induction into the Hall of Fame, we football fans everywhere are wondering who will take the place of the “irreplaceable” Barry Sanders. Sanders, who has not talked to the Detroit Lions since the end of the 1998 season, called it “quits” Wednesday, sending us all into shock. Sanders, who has relatives in Kansas City, was fourth leading rusher in the NFL last year but, no matter where he ranked in rushing yards, he was considered the best running back since Jim Brown. Lighting quick, Sanders was always All-Pro and respected by all coaches and defensive players throughout the league. Unassuming and short in stature, his legs and magical moves did his talking. When he ran, everybody watched, even those who had just a casual interest in the game. But for now, it appears that local fans won’t be blessed by Sanders’ magic in late September. The only one that is happy with the retirement news is the weak defensive line of the Kansas City Chiefs.
# OTIS FOR THE HALL OF FAME

If you go into Maxine’s Fine Foods restaurant, you’ll see Buck O’Neil or you might run into Reverend Hartsfield, or you might even see the seldom-seen Jason Whitlock. But, you’ll always see Otis Taylor, arguably the greatest wide receiver to ever put on a pro football uniform. Now, Taylor spends most of his time, either at Maxine’s or helping his son develop into a respectable athlete. Even so, Taylor still has a hankering to put closure to his NFL career, marked by the Chiefs only Super Bowl victory and countless touchdowns catches, especially in the old Municipal stadium. Ollie Gates, Monday Nite Footballers, Maxine herself, and all the restaurant regulars wonder why Taylor can’t get to Canton (home of the NFL Hall of Fame). Retired now for over 20 years, Taylor’s fate rests with a faraway, unknown selection committee. If you want to help, call or go by Maxine’s.
# FOOTBALL SEASON UNDER WAY, FIRST PRE-SEASON GAME AUGUST 15

Yes, this week-end, our 7-9 Kansas City Chiefs board the plane and head to River Falls, Wis., to start training camp. The first pre-season game here at Arrowhead will be against the Tennessee Titans on August 15, with the season opener scheduled for Chicago on September 12. The next week, September 19, the locals will open their home schedule with the Super Bowl winning, Denver Broncos.
# ‘ALTER EGO’ THE WORD ON GENERATION RAP

On Carl Boyd’s Generation Rap, the “Word from Grand Dad” will be alter ego. If you don’t know what or who this is, you are missing one of life’s greatest gifts. Find out more about an alter ego on the Generation Rap on Saturday, at 8 a.m., on KPRS, the Hot 103 JAMZ.
# FINALE FOR FREEDOM SCHOOL AT GEM TONIGHT

The dedicated and capable staff of the Freedom School will say goodbye to its summer program at a final event tonight at the Gem Theater. Each year, the six-week summer school is held at the Mount Pleasant Education facility. Carolyn Booker and her staff are to be congratulated for adding another effective, educational center for our children. It’s a “must-see” for anyone wanting to see progressive education in action. Ms. Booker, herself is a graduate of Philander Smith college in Little Rock, Ark., a Methodist-sponsored school. Kudos for all involved."

Lloyd Greenfield is a retired firefighter and current real estate professional, and he thinks the Chiefs are just playing good football about now. Ed West and Carl Clark praise the Chiefs' game-winning defense. Well, this week will be the supreme test for Kansas City as they board the plane for Indianapolis to take on the Colts. Fresh from a convincing win, the Colts will have Peyton Manning running an offense that features both running and passing. On top of that, the Colts sport the same record as the Chiefs (5-2). Both teams have good defen- sive personnel and it will be tough for either team to mount and sustain any, long, offensive drives. Derrick Thomas will again lead the defense, along with McGlockton and company. Jerome Woods, James Hasty and Eric Warfield will be ready to see if they really are the best secondary in the American Confer- ence. Thus, Greenfield, West and Clark are all right in their assessment about Kansas City. But let's look in closer at Sunday's 12 noon game.

Everyone in town will be glued to their big screens to see if the Chiefs can squeeze another road win. The Nap Town crowd will loud and definitely a fac- tor in the game's outcome. And while we don't like to say it, the Chiefs do tend to fold on the road, like at Chicago and in the first game at San Diego. You will note that except for Baltimore, all the Chiefs' wins are at home...where 78,000 screaming and booing fans make Derrick Thomas raise his arms in the air, sig- naling for the crowd to ""get busy.""

Even though there won't be a hometown friendly crowd, the Chiefs will get major help from Stoyanovich who will earn his pay by kicking decisive field goals. And, of course, big-play man, Derrick Alexander, will again rise to the occasion and lead the team in receiving yards. No real help cometh from the running backs save for a decent showing from Rashaan Shehee. And as always, this all depends on unpredictable Elvis Grbac, Kansas City's garage sale quar- terback. If he doesn't hurt us with interceptions and unnecessary time-outs, the offense will have a chance to stay on the field. Admittedly, no matter how good they have been, the defense cannot do it alone. Derrick Parker over at Seven Oaks Barber Shop says that Elvis must perform decently, just like he did out at Arrowhead against San Diego. Bottom line.

If the ""iffy"" Chiefs can remain mistake-free, they will keep their shared hold on first place in the AFC West. If not, they will fall victims to the fast emerging, well coached Seattle Seahawks, also 5-2 and first place co-occupants. Do Charles Allen believes in these Chiefs. I do, too, and look for Kansas City to win, even though it will be very, very, very close. Here's what we think:

Defense will make a boy out of Manning.

# Kansas City Chiefs Vs. Seattle Seahawks

That's the question the Chiefs are asking their season-long critics. Since falling needlessly to Chicago way back in mid-September, the Chiefs have been busily correcting the problems and now find themselves alone, atop the AFC West. Their destiny for post-season playoffs lies solely in their hands. Since beginning this topsy-turvy roller coaster ride, here's what these amazing Chiefs have accomplished.

The critics said, ""Throw more to the tight end."" Tony Gonzalez has caught six touchdown passes during this recent winning streak. ""Concrete feet"" Grbac has to stop throwing game-losing interceptions. He stopped. They said further, ""The Chiefs have to open up their offense."" They've been throwing more on first down and even Derrick Alexander ran an 82 reverse to demolish Pittsburgh. It seems the Chiefs have solved every major problem put before them, including threatening to bench Vanover and Derrick Thomas for poor playing habits. So, the question is...now what?

Well, the last criticism is getting the kickoffs into their opponents' end zone. Those who go to the games see how the opposing teams get good field position because Stoyanovich just can't kick the ball very far. So, this week, ol' problem-solving Gunther went out and got a kicker who can supposedly accomplish this task. Again and again, the Chiefs have steadily addressed all these concerns. And because of that, the ""weak-in-faith"" have gradually returned to talking more favorably about these strong-willed Chiefs. And assuming the newly-acquired kicker can perform, the Chiefs are in good shape to complete the rags-to-riches odyssey. But the question still remains, now what?

Of course, accurate prognosticators like Charles Allen, Carl Clark, Lloyd Greenfield, Ed West, and Walt Livingston all say the Chiefs are ready to beat Seattle. Even Paul Booker and Ollie Hubbard, predictable critics of the Chiefs, have changed their minds and are now convinced the Chiefs deserve to be AFC West champions. But, ancient and obstinate Gardner still thinks the Chiefs will fold. Finding nothing to criticize, he says there will be a Y2K glitch and Seattle will topple Kansas City. Of course, Ike has lived a long time and I'm sure he has reason to know that the unexpected can be expected. While that's true, this is not the time or the place to apply that adage. When someone asks ""now what,"" we should all answer in unison...The AFC Conference Championship.

Along with the rest of the world, the Monday Niters believe in Chicago all the way. The Bears have come to be a real team; Coach Mike Ditka has allowed a great deal of fun, while assembling one of the greatest teams in NFL history. They've done videos and made hit records. One avid reader of this column stated, ""The Bears have put the fun back in football."" We couldn't agree more.
But, they are a good football team-with or without all hype. Below are the predictions and comments by each of the available members.

TITLE: Kansas City Chiefs At Seattle Seahawks Preview and Predictions

ENTITIES: Kansas City Chiefs, Seattle Seahawks, Jim Nunnelly, Elvis Grbac, Jimmy Raye, Sylvester Morris, Tony Gonzales, Derrick Alexander, Kevin Lockett, Troy Drayton, Warren Moon, St. Louis, Marshall Faulk, Oakland Raiders, San Francisco 49ers, Lloyd Greenfield, Charles Allen, Paul Booker, Carl Clark, Ike Gardner, Ollie Hubbard, Ed West, Shawn Edwards, Generation Rap, Tennessee Titans, Washington Redskins, Martyball, KCMO TV 5, KMBC TV 9

While the season is not quite at the halfway point, it is still refreshing to know that our Chiefs are capable of blending the pass and run, sufficient to beat the reign-i ng Super Bowl champions. As an organization, the Chiefs must not over promise and we, as fans, must be care- ful not to over expect . . . at least at this juncture. How- ever, we can be encouraged that Marty ball has fin- ally been eulogized . . . hopefully for good.""

UP, UP, AND AWAY. – That's the Chiefs' new password. Whatever Jimmy Raye told Elvis Grbac, it is working. He is hitting all of those talented receivers that we've been draft- ing and leaving to rot on the field. These receivers are making big plays because they are big receivers (Sylvester Morris, Tony Gonzales, Derrick Alexander, Kevin Lockett). Even tight end Troy Drayton wants some props, too, especially after his game-icing touchdown catch from Medicare quarterback Warren Moon. In fact, we beat St. Louis at their own game, putting the ball in the air (up, up, and away). But the real difference was the defense and that will also be the key to victory in Seattle on Sunday. Against St. Louis, the Chiefs tackled everything and everybody. They contained Marshall Faulk, arguably the best run- ning back anywhere, to just a few yards, many times stopping him cold. So my hat goes off to the defense, who must continue to play well in order to remain a playoff-contender. Sun- day, it will be Seattle and after that, the Chiefs will be hosted by division leader, Oakland, and San Francisco. As Lloyd Greenfield has observed, the Seahawks' game ought to be a Halloween treat. But the Raiders and the 49ers will require another perfect game, just like the St. Louis outburst. Finally, the Chiefs still have some work to do and we, as fans, should continue our ""cautious optimism."" And just take it one game and one team at a time. The Chiefs will be 5-3 on Sunday.

MOTHERS RAISING SONS – That will be the topic discussed on the Generation Rap program, Saturday morning at 8 a.m., on 103.3 FM. Shawn Edwards, the venerable host of the hour-long, teen-oriented program, will co-lead the discussion, along with the ""g-rap crew."" The High School Game of the Week will also be featured, and of course, the ""Word from Grand Dad.""
COMMENTS, QUESTIONS, ADVICE, OR SUGGESTIONS – Grand Dad’s e-mail address is nunnjim@gv.co.jackson.mo.us.

**A CASE FOR OHIO STATE.** Yes, it's true. Not Duke, not Michigan State, not University of Connecticut. Maybe the Buckeyes from Columbus, Ohio, where the state university and 48,000 students make up this Big Ten entry. So why would sports pundits like Carl Clark, Ollie Hubbard, and Walt Livingston pick Ohio State over the University of Connecticut in the first semifinal game tomorrow? It's because the Buckeyes are on a roll. They've completely turned their fate around, going from 8-22 in the 1998 season. And for those of you who are trivia buffs, it has been 31 years since these same Buckeyes have visited the big dance. Now, they enter the Final Four with a 28-7 record and a pair of the finest three-pointer guards anywhere in college basketball...Michael Redd and James ""Scoonnie"" Penn. That alone makes them a contender to get into the finals against Duke on Monday night.

**MALIK YOBA VISITS ALTERNATIVE SCHOOLS.** The star of ""New York Undercover"" was scheduled to visit DeLaSalle and Hickman Mills Alternative Schools Thursday. Malik is here on behalf of the March Against Violence program, sponsored under the auspices of KMBC-TV9. Also, Malik spoke at a community program last night at the Gem theatre.

**HEALTH FAIR PLANNING UNDERWAY.** Under chief sponsorship of the Jackson County Legislature, the second Saturday in June has been designated as the date for the annual Health Fair, directed by the Rodgers Health Center. If you are interested in being a part, call Coordinator Nina Smith at 889-4643. Community agencies and volunteers are welcome. More later."

*""Major disappointment last week. However, the Chiefs will regain the momentum. The Chiefs will win two of three on this West coast run.""*

ELVIS AND THE FOUR ACES . . . Did you ever think we would ever be praising and depending on our passing game? And Gunther, who used to be a Marty disciple, is sounding more like Dan Corryel and Don Shula, the way he distributes ""props"" to old Elvis and the Four Aces (Derrick Alexander, Tony Gonzales, Kevin Lockett and Sylvester Morris). But come Sunday, this quintet will again be expected to exceed and excel. Their play looms large in how the Chiefs will fare out in cable car country, and maybe hopes for a season-ending playoff spot. To be in the playoffs, the Chiefs will need upset assistance from other teams. If the defense plays like it played against Gannon and ""the Resurrected Raiders,"" the Chiefs will scoot closer to the bottom. But at any rate, the Chiefs should sneak out of San Francisco, with their hearts and another much needed victory. To tell you the truth, the Chiefs would have to try to lose, given their remaining, six-game schedule. This time, you can gamble . . . because, after all, the Chiefs have Four Aces.

POTPOURRI AND OTHER STUFF. . . Kelly Bowland, deputy director for the COM-BAT Commission, has accepted a position with the ever-expanding Full Employment Council. Kelly will receive her master's from Baker university. Several agencies, like Swope Parkway Health center and the Samuel U. Rodgers Health center, will be recommended to receive grants from the Susan B. Komen Foundation to do breast cancer programs. Sandra Inez Hubbard, wife of Ollie Hubbard, was the first African American born in the Macon, Mo., County hospital, 65 years ago this week.

**SUPER BOWL SUNDAY: GAME VERSUS THE COMMERCIAL** . . . Move over Jesse Jackson, soaring gas bills, Texas fugitives and even John Ashcroft. This Sunday is the biggest holiday of the year, Super Bowl Sunday. Everybody will put aside their everyday differences and concentrate solely on the two best football teams in the entire world – the New York Giants and the Baltimore Ravens. From Friday afternoon until early Monday afternoon, all the talk will be about “the game.” We, Monday Niters, along with our significant others, will gather at Lloyd and Geneva Greenfield’s in their attractive southeast Kansas City home. Genuine Jean Anderson will cater the affair and show off her Louisiana/Arkansas culinary prowess and make everybody happy. But what else?

**POTPOURRI AND OTHER STUFF** . . . Hate to say we told you so. But Elvis is going to the Pro Bowl. And we told you also that Gunther Cunningham would be fired. Now hear us again when we tell you to attend St. James United Methodist’s 8 a.m. services for a special presentation on Super Bowl Sunday.

The Chiefs are woefully heartless and without any enthusiasm. Sunday will be another exercise in mediocre play and play calling. And, as if that's not enough, the ""Flutie factor"" cannot be under-estimated. Look for the locals to dip below .500""

**MANIC-DEPRESSIVE AND VICISSITUDINARY** . . . Some days up and some days down. Some Sundays, the Chiefs play like Super Bowl contenders and some Sundays, they are embarrassing to the max. That kind of play leaves us wondering -- are the Chiefs the best of the worst or are they the worst of the best. The truth is that they are both. To make matters worse, the Chiefs usually play up, or down, according to their opponent. When they played the world champion St. Louis Rams, they play well. Then, where we as fans start ""feeling it,"" doggone if they don't lose to lowly San Francisco. And, with six games remaining, we must be careful for the Chiefs could very well go 3-3 during the weakest part of the schedule. Point being that the Chiefs' 5-5 season is right on track. The record speaks volumes about how unpredictable and unreliable the Chiefs really are. Those fancy words in the headline, manic-depressive and vicissitudi-nary, are just ways of explaining the Chiefs duality. With that in mind, Paul Booker and Ed West believe that the ""down"" team will show up against Buffalo at Arrowhead. But, when stubborn and cantankerous Ike Gardner thinks the Chiefs will win, I want to believe him. After an erratic and topsy-turvy performance, the Chiefs will win by a squeaker.

**POTPOURRI AND OTHER STUFF** . . . Scott O. Wright, senior federal judge, surprised everybody by saying that more drug treatment should be available. Tracey Blaylock, the venerable deputy director of MOVE UP, also teaches night classes on sub-stance abuse issues at Penn Valley Community college. Look for postage rates to increase to 34 cents after the holidays. Need holiday help in preparing meals, call Gen-uine Jean Anderson at (816) 931-2819 or Marcie Brown at (816) 432-6989. The Carter Trophy, an award for the high school Player of the Year, will be presented by Shawn Edwards Saturday morning on the Generation Rap show on KPRS 103.3.

“I like the Chiefs, been a season ticket holder since the old days at Municipal Stadium. The slow roll the Chiefs are enjoying is not enough to beat the Broncos.” -- **Carl Clark**

Ray Crockett knows a lot about cornerbacking. That's why he has earned a spot in the Chiefs' secondary. He came here, showed up well in the pre-season, and now is a quiet force in the Chiefs' respectable defense. He's got cool . . . mainly because he's one of the few current Chiefs that has ever been to the Super Bowl. But, this week, he is more than all that. It's because he knows about the Denver Broncos. After all, up until this year, he was their starting corner. And, our ""sometimey"" Chiefs will face Denver in Denver's new Mile High Stadium.

Okay, so what's the big deal about Ray Crockett? For one, it will be a reunion with all of his old friends from Denver. More important, he will be covering the likes of Rod Smith, as he has many times in practice. But, this time, it will be for real. With Denver coming off a loss against Grbac-led Baltimore and the Chiefs beating up on Marty's limping Redskins, this game is key for both clubs. Is Denver again beatable? Are the Chiefs finally jelling, after months of Vermeil promises? Is it true that San Diego is emerging as an AFC West force?

Well, all three of these questions will be answered by Sunday evening. The Chiefs' offense looked decent last week. Priest Holmes showed some promise and the receivers showed signs of excellence at times. More important, Trent had a little time to throw and he did well. But, Denver is not Washington, in any shape or form. They never seem to run out of running backs. More important, they have receivers like Mike Sours Southern's Rod Smith that just love to go deep and leap. Thus, the Chiefs will be tested. We, Monday Niters, are split on which team will win. Of course, Ike, Booker, Ed West, and even Carl Clark believe Denver will light up the new stadium and the rest of us . . . Charles Allen, Lloyd, Ollie and me . . . think the Chiefs will win big.
Let's call Ray Crockett. He has a better idea than anybody.

column1: Alicia Keys has a song and a video out, called “You Don't Know My Name”. And that's exactly what some people think about this nameless Super Bowl game, coming up Sunday afternoon. Without a doubt, this game promises to be among the most boring and least-interesting, in the entire 38-year history of the event. More people will be watching for the new commercials and Janet Jackson, rather than the game, itself. And of course, we, Kansas City fans, have a special, non-interest in this game, as well.

First, our beloved Chiefs faltered at the altar. Started out winning nine consecutive games and then, they played like the Chiefs we all know...the best mediocre team in the National Football League. Enough potshots. Actually, we all suffered a letdown, when the Indianapolis Colts kicked or behinds, out at Arrowhead, in early January. Just like they did before. And the Colts victory drained us of our remaining enthusiasm and energy. Yea, the Colts did it to us.

Speaking of Indianapolis, there is another reason why this game will be boring. Even after the Chiefs failed us again, we were hoping that just maybe an African American coach (Dungy of the Colts), would be matched up with an African American quarterback (McNabb of the Eagles). As a tribute to Martin Luther King's 75th birthday anniversary. And when both teams lost in their respective championship game, a low-ebb interest, became even less interesting. Bottom line, this game will have no meaning whatsoever, especially to us African Americans in Kansas City. So, what are we to do, then?

Well, first, take a look at what your Monday Niters have predicted. You will find that almost all the guys, are ""feeling it"" for New England. That's because of their long, consecutive, homefield-winning streak; and, after all, the Patriots did beat out every other, AFC rival. But, last year, Tampa Bay surprised everyone and convincingly beat Oakland in Super Bowl XXXVII. And something tells me that Carolina will surprise everyone and beat New England, in spite of the forecasts by the Las Vegas oddsmakers and the arrogant-but-accurate Monday Nite Footballers. Maybe, it won't happen, but...... it will make an otherwise dull game, a lot more interesting.
The Monday Niters will be gathering to watch the Super Bowl at the luxurious sub-urban home of Ed and Celeste West."

column1: Okay, now that it has been established that the Chiefs can win, it is now okay for you of lesser faith to relax and allow the Chiefs to evolve from their rocky, 1-4 start. It should be easier to think more positively about our chances against the Colts, this Sunday.

Clearly, Indianapolis is no pushover. This is the same team that knocked us out of the playoffs, last year. The Payton-Harrison, passing tandem is as good as it gets. However, the Colts are noticeably weak when it comes to pass defense....not horrible but just weak. It is in the passing game that the Chiefs will excel.

Sunday, you can look for Trent Green to show up as a true Pro-Bowler. He will fill the airways for over 300 yards to Morton, Gonzalez, and Kennison. Even Priest will get a few passes to complement a decent running day. In short, look for the Chiefs to explore and exploit the Colts' secondary.
The Chiefs will win by 10 or more."

column1: Two notes on the Super Bowl. One, Janet Jackson will be replaced by former Beatles' great, Paul McCartney. Two, the Chiefs will be in Kansas City, watching the February event, like everybody else. Now, we can all understand why Carl Clark did not renew his season tickets, this year. He alertly foresaw the disaster that has now come to pass. It is a shame when a passer throws for nearly four hundred yards and his team loses.

It is awful that for the last three games, we all have witnessed a team that starts out great, fades in the middle and peters out in the end. But, such is life. This past Monday night was the worst display of football, since the Chiefs went 2-12 under Paul Wiggin. New England just ate the Chiefs alive and just totally embarrassed our locals. Now, Marty and the Chargers return on Sunday. San Diego is co-leading the AFC West, with a respectable 7-3 record. Do you remember when we wanted to throw Marty out?

My, how times change! Chiefs will ruin Marty's homecoming by at least one touchdown. Priest will show LaDanian who the top rusher really is. But, it won't matter one bit. The Chiefs will still be home in February, watching the Super Bowl on tv, just like everyone else.

column1: **Special Note:** Cleaver will remain as Senior Pastor at St. James, while serving as Congressman from Missouri's 5th District. He will leave on Tuesday mornings and return to Kansas City each Thursday evening. He will officially be sworn-in Janu-ary 4, 2005 in Washington, D.C. Rev. Cleaver is currently looking for a small apart-ment in Washington.

Well, the Chiefs have lost again and now stand 3-5. Vermeill doesn't have the answer. Gunther and Al apparently don't either because the Chiefs continue to start strong, fade in the middle, and just peter out at the end. That kind of play has cost them a lot of fans and maybe a position in the playoffs. And on this Sunday, they travel to the Sugar Bowl and try to beat up on struggling New Orleans Saints. And, for the galley of Chiefs critics, there is no one part to blame it all on. The offense is sound. The defense is better than last year. And, the special teams are okay. Then, what is it? It must be the coaching, that important factor that keeps you going when ability and stamina wanes. It's that umphh that helps you through the last moments of any crisis. And, just maybe, the coaching is not doing its part to help the Chiefs win. But, Sunday, the Chiefs will pull it off some kind of way. Bottom line, it is so hard to see an improved team lose again and again. But not this Sunday. Look for Dalton and LJ to keep the Saints from marching in."

column1: All three Chiefs' wins have been at Arrowhead. But, they won't be home, this Sunday. They will be down in Florida, against the Tampa Bay Buccaneers. And after demonstrating a strong offense and a developing defense, the Chiefs will now be viewed differently. We will expect the offense to score like they did against Atlanta. We will expect the defense to play like it did against Indianapolis in the first half. And we just might have restored a little faith in the specialty team. You can expect a letdown against the Bucs.

Of course, the Chiefs will win...but it won't be pretty. There'll be some rough spots but the brightest part will be watching Eric Warfield just show off. TV commentators will be talking about his tackling and his interceptions. And, at the end, the Chiefs will be at 4-4. Let the new season begin!

column1: Let's see how we can make this work. The Chiefs have won one consecutive game.

Priest Holmes is out for the season. The Chiefs have beaten Oakland and the defense didn't fold. Tony Gonzalez is still among the highest rated tight ends. Larry Johnson played Sunday, like he did when he was at Penn State. All of the above are true.

Now, here's the kicker. Chiefs will probably raise their ticket prices, in spite of a very mediocre season. While we had no discussion on the other statements, the ticket-price thing just rubs us the wrong way. At 4-8, we are not going to the play-offs. Even if we win the remaining four games, we would have lost five more games than last year. The stadium, against San Diego, was half-empty, proving that the fans know that the season is finished. All of this adds up to a certain reduction in season ticket prices.

Man, am I dreaming. Wake up and that is pure balderdash (crap). On a positive note, the Chiefs will begin their redemption process, by beating Tennessee by 48 points. At least, that's what 6-year-old Isaiah Gardner says. He's the smart grandson of Ike Gardner, who also voted for the Chiefs to beat the Titans."

This word means the expected changes and variations. The weather and the stock market are examples of vicissitudes. Now, our beloved Chiefs have adopted that same philosophy. They start out strong, scoring many times on the first possession; then they fade a little in the middle; and just plain peter out in the end. So many times lately, we Monday Niters have witnessed that. And because of the Chiefs’ likely outcomes, we spent last Monday night, talking about movies, rather than the Chiefs. While some of us are in denial, we are all disillusioned optimists. Conse-quently, our left-over verve from last year is gone and all we can talk about now is classic, big-screen talking like ""Imitation of Life"" and ""One Eyed Jacks"" and ""The Godfather"" and on and on. For 35 non-stop years, we have ceaselessly talked about the Chiefs and Monday Night Football. And after the New Orleans debacle, we might as well change our interests to movies. For there is not much to talk about these days, when it comes to our Chiefs.

But, wait! Didn’t the Chiefs beat Baltimore, earlier this year, on a Monday night? Yes. And didn't we once beat Buffalo, a Super Bowl contender, here at Arrowhead a decade ago? Well, doggone it. The Chiefs, after many vicissitudes, might just top-ple the high-flying New England Patriots. Maybe. Or else we'll change our name."

column1: Remember Franco Harris helped win four Super Bowls for Pittsburg, under Coach Chuck Noll. . . Paul Booker will spend his first Super Bowl con-fined. He is recovering from a major hip-replacement surgery and is now on a cane. . . Each member of Congress is allowed one guest to the State of the Union speech. Emanuel Cleaver gave that one ticket to his father, Tuesday night. . . The commercials are costly but impressive. Corporations will spend millions for the debut opportunity that only the Super Bowl brings. . . Look for Seattle to surprise folks and beat the Steelers. . . Much of the ""hype"" centers around Jerome Bettis, who is from Detroit and is nearing retirement. . . Pittsburg had to beat the three top-seeded teams on the road to qualify for the Super Bowl. . . I predict that Super Bowl XL will garner the least interest of any of the previous Super Bowls. . ."

column1: Everybody had to bow down to Booker and Ike, this week. Somehow, they correctly picked the Giants over our quick-fading Chiefs. The Giants didn't win! The Chiefs, via poor tackling and an overall blue-funk, gave that game to New York. In the meantime, the Chiefs' playoff hopes are dimming fast. Nonetheless, like Jesse Jackson says, we must keep hope alive! And if we beat San Diego and Cincinnati at home. And if San Diego loses its next two games. And if Pittsburg loses just one of their last two games. . . then, and only then, will the Chiefs make the coveted play-offs.

Now that kind of scenario, leads most of the Monday Niters to give up on the Chiefs. And since it is San Diego who beat the then-unbeatened Colts, the haters believe the Chiefs season is virtually over, when the Chiefs meet those Chargers on Saturday, out at Arrowhead. And Marty will have his charged-up Chargers ready, with the goal of fully eliminating the Chiefs from the playoff picture. But, don't take me too far, too fast, with too little. Now, the Chiefs ""can"" win at Arrowhead. And remember, Kansas City has the most explosive offense in the NFL. And if the Defense just plays average. . . then, we will remain in the playoff hunt. And all the haters will have to shut up. . . once again.

With all that in mind, Dr. Charles Allen, Lloyd Greenfield, and I are staying the ever-weakening course. Surely, goodness and mercy shall follow the Chiefs, allowing them to dodge the ""lightning"". Chiefs over San Diego by at least six."

column1: During the month of November, we will pay special tribute our faithful readers, many who have trusted us for over 35 years. In fact, Carl Connor, Terry Strother, Jasper Hammock, and Cheryl Cooper read our column from faraway places. Avid local readers include J. J. Harris, Morris Henderson, Steven Young, Calline toe Coleman and many others who will be recognized in future editions. Thanks for believing and trusting in us.

Since everyone, except Ollie, thinks the Chiefs will win on Sunday, there was not much to argue about, this past Monday. In fact, we just sat around and reminisced. Bored and sleepy, we talked about Song's Buddha Inn (51st and Prospect) and Dixon's Chili (Truman Road and Prospect). Looking back is one of the favorite MNF activities, especially for those who were born and raised in Kansas City. That little activity serves to ""lock"" out those of us who are imports. But looking forward, we will be at Dr. Charles Allen's house, way out in South Kansas City. The Allens will team-host this Monday's event, employing the joint culinary talents of Dr. Allen, his wife Beverly, and their daughter, Terrill. And, of course, all of us will wel-come the chance to visit with Robert Lee Allen, the bright, 11-year-old from Mason City Elementary school. And even this child will know that Kennison and Larry Johnson will just mesmerize the Raiders.
Chiefs by 3 in a donnybrook of a game."

column1: Lloyd spent all day Monday, renovating his downstairs to accommodate us, Mon-
day Nite Footballers. The light blue walls houses his 62-inch, big screen television
and the ambience was fit for kings. However, little was said about the Chiefs loss to
Dallas, save for a couple of boastful, I-told-you-so comments from that Booker and
ol' Ike. The rest of us, mainly Dr. Allen, Ike and Ed West, just reminisced about
their college days (in ancient history). Then the conversation turned to the AFC
playoffs. Obviously dimmed by Sunday's downer, the Chiefs have to beat New
York, San Diego, and Cincinnati, in order to have any playoff hopes at all. Fortu-
nately for the Chiefs, they play San Diego and Cincinnati at Arrowhead.
But, let's not make our move, too soon. The Chiefs must keep Johnson running
and Kennison catching and we will leave the ""Big Apple"" in good shape. There is no
doubt that this duo will perform like All-Pros. After that, we can worry over the
next two AFC games. Just wait and see, the Chiefs by 3."

column1: Well, Monday night, the Chiefs against Tennessee did not fold. And that's a good thing. Those of us who are disillusioned optimists, might say* the Chiefs are unde- feated in December.

This statement begs the question, ... ""what does that mean"". Absolutely, nothing. But, it does say loudly that some bad coaching occurred, somewhere. For how can a running back be near-released one week and run two 40-yards-plus touchdowns on another. Where has Johnson been and why hasn't he been playing? Who held him back?

Was it all because he responded to a coach's inappropriate comments? We really do know what the deal is, don't we? And not to dwell on the past, Larry Johnson should let his legs do the talking at least from now on. And with that is a lesson. . . one cannot antagonize and persuade at the same time. You can't be at odds with the coach and expect to play. Which reminds me all is well with the two of them, now.
Especially, since Johnson is running as well as the venerable Priest Holmes. More important, he is really saving someone's job. Ain't it funny how things change?"

# Kansas City Chiefs vs. Houston Texans

column4: After last week's embarrassment, Chiefs have to save face!
Last week was a wakeup call!
I'm giving Chiefs one more chance!
A win, as long as they play 22 men!
A complete, thorough upset...ha, ha, ha!
Kicker can make at least one!

column1: The Houston Texans are way down on the list when it comes to rankings in the NFL. The Texans are 31st in total offense and 29th in total defense. On the other hand, the Chiefs are 8th in total offense and 26th in total defense. Thus, it appears that two of the league's worst defenses will pair up in what may be called the duel of the ""down and out"". Then, it follows that the difference will be in the offenses. Houston is notably weak at every offensive position. On the other hand, the Chiefs are experiencing vulnerability on the front line, something unheard-of just a year ago. So, if the Chiefs' weak offensive line outplays Houston's weak defensive line, then it is safe to predict a Chiefs' victory, advancing their record to respectable still-in-the-playoff-picture, 6-4. However, should the ""weak"" Houston defensive line overshadow the ""fail"" Chiefs offensive line, then the Chiefs will fall to a 5-5, and we can forget about playing past January 1.

Bottom line, except for Ollie Hubbard, all of us believe the Chiefs will prevail, despite its lackluster play last week at Buffalo. However, this is the week where several NFL-leading teams will lose. Fortunately, the Chiefs will not be among those experiencing unexpected defeat. The Chiefs by 14 and the ever-skillful Kennison and the ever-mouthy Jerry Johnson will shine."

AUTHOR: By Jim ""Grand Dad"" Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

ENTITIES: Jesse Jackson, Jim ""Grand Dad"" Nunnelly, Oakland, Kansas City, Trent, Ed West, Huard, Denver, San Diego, Fuzzy Thompson, Samuel Rodgers Health Center, Chillicothe, Dr. Rodgers, Lovie Smith (Chicago), Marvin Lewis (Cincinnati), Tony Dungy (Indianapolis), Dennis Green (Arizona), Romeo Cremmel (Cleveland), Herman Edwards, Art Shell
By Jim ""Grand Dad"" Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970
What does Jesse Jackson have to do with last Sunday's Oakland-Kansas City game? I'll answer that after I assess the Chiefs, now just over the half-way point in the season.

First, the Chiefs survived. Now, let's face it, y'all. A win is a win, no matter how ugly. And Sunday's win against Oakland was not pretty, except for the first and last Kansas City drives. The in-between was horrible, even though it was Trent's first day back.

However, I must say that we could not have done it without Trent. Ed West flat-out confirms that. Further, he feels that Trent's presence won the game, only because Huard could not have shepherded that last drive.

And we all agree that Sunday was the appropriate time to re-insert Trent, even though Huard had done a respectable job. Now, tomorrow will be Trent's real test. He and Kennison will have to be ""connected at the hip"", for the Chiefs to have half a chance. If we should happen to beat Denver, that will put us in good shape for a playoff spot. We know that Denver is vulnerable because San Diego beat Denver and we beat San Diego. In fact, last Sunday, LaDain-ian ran like crazy. . .just like Jesse did in 1988.

Speaking of Jesse Jackson, he really did have something to do with Sunday's game. But before I tell you, let me reminisce for a minute. Back in 1988, I received a call at the (Samuel) Rodgers Health Center. It was from Fuzzy Thompson, wanting a place to have a rally that featured Jesse Jackson and some farmers from Chillicothe. I agreed but Fuzzy was unsure if it would take place. . .so I kept it a secret from our staff. . .mainly because I did not want to face it, if Jesse failed to come.

Well, Jesse did come and so did all those farmers who were about to lose their family farms. Tractors, thru-sites, and a string of cars paraded right down in the middle of the Wayne Minor projects. And peo-ple came from everywhere, to hear and see of Jesse. And when he donned the back door, he immediately started planning who would talk, when, and at what point he would enter. Details. Details. Right there on the spot. I immediately saw how Jesse operated. . .great organizing skills, clear instructions, which included a tribute to Dr. Rodgers and a ""thank you"" to me for helping out. All within a ten-minute plan-ning session. Jesse stood tall in my books and one could see why he is important to all of us, especially us sports fans.

On the way out, I mentioned to Jesse that there were no black head coaches in the National Football League. Jesse, who had been a classy running back at Illinois, told me that he would work on that. A few years later, Jesse started meeting with the owners in the NFL, asking them to include one black coach in the final interviews, only when head coaching vacan-cies occurred. The owners agreed and today, some 10 years after, there are seven black coaches, proudly running things at various franchises. As of this date, the illustrious field of head coaches now include Lovie Smith (Chicago), Marvin Lewis (Cincinnati), Tony Dungy (Indianapolis); Dennis Green (Arizona); and Romeo Cremmel (Cleveland).

The remaining two were on the field Sunday, out at Arrowhead. . . Herman Edwards (Kansas City) and Art Shell (Oakland). And that is what Jesse had to do with the game, last Sunday, out at Arrowhead. Thanks, Jesse. You always know what to do and say.
Happy Thanksgiving To All Of Our Readers!"

column1: Herman Edwards now has to face the reality. No more presea- son crap. The much-celebrated, defensive-minded coach has foolishly talked about downsizing the Chiefs’ potent offense. Did he really mean that or was it just new-coach hype? How can he downplay the one promising unit of the Chiefs? Surely he knows our defense is untried and unreliable, no matter if they do have Tamba and the Rugrats. Everyone knows it will be Johnson's running and Trent's passing and Kennison's catch- ing that will start the 2006 season with a big win. More impor- tant, the special teams, made up of mostly second teamers from the offense, will show an unusual ability to get downfield and cover both punts and kickoffs. And the kicking game will be spectacular, with punters booming 50 and 60 yarders with ease. Don't ignore the new found consistency of the ""fired-up"" field goal unit. All said and done, it will not be the Chiefs defense that will dominate. Like anything else, it will take time for that shaky defense to jell. For right now, let's dance with the ones that ""brung"" us. Herman Edwards, you better encourage your offense or we will place you on the shelf with the rest of the perpetrators."

# Kansas City Chiefs vs. Miami Dolphins
When Paul Booker makes up his mind, nothing can change it. Most of the time, the “Bootheel” native is rather mild-mannered and soft-spoken.
But, on a few items, he is clear and unequivocal. And then I’ll tell you how all this relates to the Chiefs, after we peek in on of Booker’s journey to Kansas City.

At the urging of his wife, Booker attended and graduated from Lincoln University in Jefferson City, way back in the ‘50s. Working at the Capitol building, he actually put himself through school. In fact, it was at Lincoln that he met Dr. Charles Allen, one of his football cronies. Eventually, Booker and his stately wife landed in Kansas City, and started teaching for the Kansas City Mo., School District. Majoring in elementary education, he managed to stay in the classroom for about 10 years. At that point, he made up his mind to request a transfer to a Home-School Coordinator’s position. And, man did he enjoy helping students get glasses, clothing, healthcare and jobs. He loved it and according to school officials, Booker was “one of the best”. He did this job faithfully until he made up his mind to retire in 1996.

Everybody thought of Booker would waste away but he found time to visit around town. He now had time to visit small businesses, like the 6902. In fact, some say the Mayor appointed him to visit all evening establishments and report back to her on how many folks were there. Bottom line, he has made up his mind to enjoy life and that is exactly what he does... every day in every way. And that’s kind of where football comes in.

Usually, Booker is one of the “haters”. However, of late, he has been voting for the Chiefs. In the past, he felt the Chiefs were “losers” but under Herm Edwards, he believes the Chiefs may just make the playoffs, this year. Already the Chiefs are 5-3 and are expecting to be 6-3 by Sunday night. And, against Miami, of Booker is clear that Huard ought to start, even though Trent Green is ready to reclaim his quarterback position. You will remember that Trent was injured in the first game of the season and Huard has gone on to guide the Chiefs pretty doggone good. Booker has made up his mind about this and nothing is going to change it.
That’s just the way the “Apostle Paul” is. You can count on what he says. End of story.

column1: Christmas, Thanksgiving, New Year's Eve was all of the above at Dr. Charles Allen’s spatially modern house this last Monday night. He, along with his wife, Beverly, and their daughter Terry Strother (Jowan) and Rondalyn Graham of Los Angeles. And there were eight of us Monday Nite Footballers, reuniting for the 37th time to watch our favorite pastime. And, man, was it worth it all!

Fun and barbs and smart-ass remarks filled the air. As usually, Ike appointed himself Lord of Attention. And Booker, fresh from a recent hip surgery, actually stayed awake, amid all the hot air. And Greenfield and Carl Clark were so glad to see each other. They argued and cussed incessantly about nothing. And, there was the ever strategic Ed West, breathing a wise crack or two into the useless conversations. And Ollie Hubbard only came to see and taste what the Allen team had cooked.

As usual, the Allen team presented their victuals well. Something old and something new. There was fried chicken, a beautifully scorched Allen potato, decked with three different kinds of cheeses, and a marinated salad. But, the “new thing” this year was a colorful spinach casserole. Everybody tasted, enjoyed and returned to that dish again and again. Just like Charles Allen is a perfectionist at his dentistry, he is equally conscientious when planning, preparing, and presenting his food delicacies.

Like at all reunions, conversations galore. Some about football. Still more about Trent Green. But mostly about the Chiefs. The usual haters, Ike, Booker, and Ollie, predicted that the Chiefs would falter again against Denver. Wishy washy Carl and exploitative Ed jumped on that bandwagon. But the host, and always-loyal Lloyd stuck up for the quarterback-less Chiefs They quietly got what the others missed. It ain't about the quarterback. What's missing is big, old Willie Roaf or someone else of his class. No blocking, no running. No line, no winning. Even a child knows that.

Speaking of children, we had our usual conversation with the Allen's bright and personable grandson, Robert. Taking football pretty serious these days, the rather-muscular 12-year old schmoozed around and was able to re-unite with his elderly football uncles. Quietly, but outward he thinks the Chiefs will win Sunday, against Denver. Further, he thinks the team can make it without old Trent. In fact, some great, second-string quarterbacks have emerged, upon inquiry to the first-teamer. I think Robert Allen is right. So much for reunions.
Robert Allen KC by 14. KC still my favorite team
Terry Strother KC by 5. Just a die hard Chiefs fan
Paul Graham KC by 1. It's rally time"

column1: 1. Community policing is beginning to emerge in Kansas City and Jackson County, using drug crimes as a centerpiece. Neighborhoods have been asked to identify their trouble spots and drug houses. Through creative police assignments specifically to the neighborhoods, the concerns will be addressed in a cooperative joint venture. Leading this decentralization effort are Project NeighborhOOD, Jackson County's Community backed Anti-Drug Tax; Community Foundation; the Neighborhood Alliance, Kansas City Missouri Police Department; and the City of Kansas City. Start up assignments are to be made soon.

2. Mary Groves Bland, State Representative from the 43rd district, has introduced a bill in the State Legislature that will require all persons, seeking drivers licenses for the first time, to take an an alcohol and drug prevention course. More on this later.
PRAIRIE VIEW, Texas. -- Prairie View A & M's 92-86 victory over Mississippi Valley State Saturday, Jan. 15, broke a 31-game losing streak by the Panthers in SWAC regular-season contests.
Prior to Saturday's win, Prairie View's last conference victory was a 78-69 win over Alcorn State in Prairie View, Texas on Feb. 26, 1990.

Prairie View's previous last win over a SWAC school altogether was a 99-90 win over Grambling in 1991, but the Panthers were ineligible to compete for the SWAC title that year and none of their games counted in the SWAC standings."

column1: 1. For the third year in a row, the Chiefs and Broncos square-off in a December game with major playoff implications. This year's December battle spotlights two teams that have endured roller coaster seasons thus far.

2. The Chiefs and Broncos feature two of the NFL's top ranked offensives and passing attacks. Kansas City is averaging 342.2 yards per game, seventh in the NFL, while Denver is averaging 343.4 per outing, sixth in the league. The Broncos have kept four of their last six opponents under 17 points.
3. Denver's coach, 47-year-old Wade Phillips, is a former linebacker at the University of Houston. Phillips was an assistant coach with the Oilers from 76-80.

4. The Chiefs are 7-5 after 12 contests for the first time since 1991 when the club finished 10-6. The last time out with Denver, Oct. 17, the Chiefs experienced their first win at Mile High Stadium in 11 years."

# Kansas City Chiefs vs. The Houston Oilers

Dr. Smith was in town on Monday as part of COM-BAT Drug Awareness Month speaking to Jackson County treatment providers.
2. Sports Illustrated predicts that the Chiefs will meet Green Bay in the Super Bowl next January.
3. Jerry Rice has renegotiated his contract with the San Francisco Forty-Niners. Ike Gardner has been advocating this for an awful long time.

4. Tracy Simlen, Mark Collins, Brian Washington and James Hasty all had more tackles than Derrick Thomas in the 1995 regular season. Anthony Davis was the leading tackler in the 1996 pre-season with nine.

6. Todd McNair is back with the Chiefs again. McNair played the last two seasons in Houston. Also, Willie Davis, Chiefs' receiver for six years, is now a Houston Oiler. This year, Davis is being replaced by second year receiver, Tamarick Vanover."

1. The Chiefs have been triumphant in nine of the last 10 games and 12 of the last 15 against the Seahawks. Kansas City owns a 22-13 record against Seattle, including a pair of victories last season.

2. The Seattle Seahawks ended the 1995 season with a flourish, winning six of their last eight games. The club hasn't been able to carry that success into 1996, losing 29-7 at San Diego to start the year and then suffering a 30-20 set-back to Denver at home last Sunday.

3. The sack duo of LB Derrick Thomas (79) and DE Neil Smith (71.5) have combined for 150.5 sacks during the decade, 44 more than another twosome (San Diego's Leslie O'Neal and Chris Mims had 106.5). This decade, Thomas tops all NFL defenders with 79 sacks.
4. If you have or know someone who needs drug treatment, call **COMBAT** at 881-3814.

5. Derrick Thomas set an NFL record by registering seven sacks in an 11/11/90 game vs. Seattle at Arrowhead. In 14 career games against the Seahawks, Thomas has 53 tackles, 16.5 sacks (most vs. any NFL team) five forced fumbles, two fumble recoveries and a TD ( 86-yard fumble return in 1992).
6. The Orchid room was one of ""the"" hot spots in Kansas City after World War II.

7. About Clyde McQueen: Clyde came to the Full Employment Council in 1987 after serving as the State Director of Job Training and Employment in Austin, Texas. Instrumental in the hiring process were board members Charles Hazley and Don Maxwell. The Full Employment Council has 65 employees, $11 mil-lion budget and has been singled out by President Bill Clinton for its many national leadership programs, for example, youth employment and welfare to work.

Also, the Full Employment Council works with COMBAT to obtain employment for recovering addicts. McQueen is also the newest member of a local African American think tank that includes Dwayne Crompton, Dr. Stacey Daniels and Jim Nunnelly."

Seven Warning Signs: Dropping out of school, failing in school, fighting, interruptive parenting, coming from a substance abusing family, becoming pregnant and suffering from a physical or mental condition are the seven warn ing signs our children display when they are likely to use drugs and commit criminal acts. 1. Chiefs Tamarick Vanover set a K.C. career record with his third kick-off return for a touchdown against the Denver Broncos October 27. Vanover returned the ball 97 yards and was the only bright spot in the whole game. He broke the record previously held by himself and Paul Palmer.

2. Truancy and drug use: It is thought that when a child fights in school, they are seeking suspension. When suspension occurs, the commitment to school lessons decreases setting the scene for drug use and negative behavior. If your child, or a child you know, has been fighting or truant from school, contact COMBAT for information on where-to-turn at 881-3814.

3. The Vikings and the Vikings started the season with four straight victories. For the Vikings, this was the third time in team history since 1976 that record had been accomplished. The most memorable meeting between the two teams came in 1970 during Super Bowl IV with the Chiefs beating the Vikings 23-7.

4. Dennis Green was named the fifth head coach in Vikings history on January 10, 1992. The 47-year-old Green is one of only seven coaches in NFL history to lead his team to the playoffs in each of his first three seasons. Dennis Green is one of two African American coaches in NFL. Green also won two NFC Central titles in his first three seasons at Minnesota. As receivers coach with the 49ers from 1986-89, he developed Pro Bowl players Jerry Rice and John Taylor.

5. Chiefs cornerback Dale Carter is the younger brother of Vikings wide receiver Jake Reed. This will be the second time they have faced each other in a regular season game. The last time being in 1993.

6. Chiefs quarterback Rich Gannon played in 48 games, (39 starts), with Minnesota from 1987-92. Gannon, who was the Vikings primary starting quarter-back from 1990-92, completed 561 of 1,003 passes, (55.9 percent), for 6,497 yards with 40 touchdowns and 36 interceptions with the team. Greg Hill is expected to play in this week's game after having been made inactive for the Denver game. The Chiefs lost that game 34-7.

column1: ENTITIES: Pittsburgh Steelers, Kansas City Chiefs, New York Giants, Jim Nunnelly, Kordell Stewart, Elvis Grbac, Andre Rison, Brett Perriman, Greg Hill, Marcus Allen, Tony Gonzales, Kimble Anders, Donnell Bennett, Tony Richardson, Derrick Thomas, Tracy Simien, Anthony Davis, Terry Wooden, Donnie Edwards, Troy Dumas, Dale Carter, Willie Beamon, Arrowhead Stadium, Saturday, August 2, 1997
Pittsburgh Steelers vs. Kansas City Chiefs

On Saturday Kordell Stewart will remove the ""slash"" from his name and position. That is, he will no longer combine being a quarterback and a wide receiver. After a 50-50 regular season last year at quarterback, Stewart has won the starting job as the Steelers' quarterback. That should make NFL fans everywhere very happy.

For the Chiefs, there'll be many new faces. Starting with a new quarterback Elvis Grbac, the Chiefs also will start newly acquired wide receivers Andre Rison and Brett Perriman. Greg Hill, a four-year veteran and the Chiefs second leading rusher last year, will replace Marcus Allen as running back. And, just this week, first round draft pick, Tony Gonzales signed a contract to play tight end for six years.

Pro Bowler Kimble Anders will share fullback duties with Donnell Bennett and Tony Richardson. Look for somebody not to make the team here. Derrick Thomas, eight-time Pro Bowler, will play the ""falcon"" position, allowing him a better opportunity to rush opposing quarterbacks. However, he may not play Saturday because of an injury.

Linebackers face tough competition as Tracy Simien, Anthony Davis, Terry Wooden (acquired from Seattle), Donnie Edwards and Troy Dumas all battle to be starter in the 3-4 defense scheme. All-pro corner Dale Carter and Willie Beamon (New York Giants) will play a lot Saturday.
The Chiefs jumped off to a 4-0 start last year but ended the season losing the last three games, making for a mediocre 9-7 season and missing the playoffs."

column1: NOTE: The Kansas City Chiefs started this year losing to the league-leading, undefeated Denver Broncos. Since that time, they have field-ed almost a new team; beat the Raiders and the Bills at home; beat Car-olinas and Seattle in last second thrillers. Then, last Sunday, they lost to Miami. Certainly 4-2 is not bad. But it's not good. A couple of more seconds and the Chiefs could be 2-4. Thus, the guys in the group take a critical look at the woes, wishes and strengths of this 1997 edition of the Chiefs. Personally, I'm not sure if we're the ""best"" of the worst teams or the ""worst"" of the best teams. Here's what the fellas say:
* Dr. Charles Allen: ""Chiefs are not an excellent team but a good team.""
* The ""Apostle"" Paul Booker: ""The Chiefs lack consistency, especial-ly in the back of field.""
* Carl Clark: ""The offensive coordinator, Paul Hackett, suffers from amnesia.""
* Ike Gardner: ""The Chiefs have a mediocre defense and an inept offense.""
* Lloyd Greenfield: ""The Chiefs suffer from a lack of physical tackling and a lack of aggressiveness.""
* Uncle Walt Livingston: ""The Chiefs are playing well enough to make the play-offs.""
* Jim Nunnelly: ""Greg Hill will work it out and become worthy of his pre-season build-up.""
* Harry ""Hate the Chiefs"" Sypert: ""I can't say anything good, so I won't say anything at all.""
* Ed West: ""Both the pass rush and play calling are poor.""

Dr. Charles Allen, Carl Clark, Walt Livingston and Jim Nunnelly think the Chiefs will make the play-offs. Paul Booker, Ol' Ike Gardner, Lloyd Greenfield, Ollie Hubbard, Harry Sypert and Ed West think the Chiefs will have to wait until next year."

The New York Times and CBS's Eye On America visited Jackson County's anti-drug initiative this week. CBS was interested in COMBAT's Drug Court, where those arrest-ed for drug possession can opt to take an intensive treatment program under the super-vision of the court. As of now, the segment is scheduled to air 5:30 p.m. on Monday. The New York Times is looking at the total COMBAT program, with special emphasis on how community people respond to the drug problem. A date of publication has not been established for the Times article.

Since the Chiefs signed free agent Andre Rison this week, they would have an all new starting wide receive unit. Last week, the Chiefs signed Steve Perriman, to be one of the starters. What's going to happen to Tamarick Vanover and Sean LaChapelle?
**States' Fastest Females, A Freshman And A Sophomore**

A ninth and a tenth grader are the states fastest females. Yes, sophomore sensation Amanda Scullark (Center High School) won the 100-meter in 3A competition at the Mis-souri state track meet in Jefferson City recently. Muna Lee, a freshman at Central High School, captured the 4A titles in 100 meters and 200 meters. Amanda is the daugh-ter of Larry and Alfreda Scullark. Muna is the daughter of Doris and Keith Lee. Both yong ladies are good students and plan to work on their skills this summer.

Dr. Wade Nobles, noted psychologist and scholar on African American affairs, held several seminars with local substance abuse professionals and educators. Among those attending were Al Brooks; Dr. Ida Love, Dr. Phyllis Chase, Pat Glorioso, and Keith Brown. Dr. Nobles posed the question of how we can use our Africanity to teach our children. He also challenged those in the substance abuse field to develop character building, spirituality based treatment and prevention initiatives. COMBAT, through Pro-ject Neighborhood, sponsored the series.

Countywide alternative schools are gearing up to face the challenge educating our children in the 21st century. A two-day seminar for alternative school teachers is planned for August 18-19. The Mayor's Office and the Jackson County Prosecutor have been actively working with the alternative school to build capacity for the alternative school students.

St. Monica parents and students recently honored J.R. Robinson, popular teacher at the school, for his tireless commitment to developing character in children. His program, Alpha and Omega, stresses good grades and excellent behavior. The group has sponsored field trips to Chicago and Atlanta."

Kansas City Chiefs vs. Buffalo Bills
1. The Chiefs have always had trouble in Buffalo. They've only won one of six regular season games there and have failed altogether in playoff games.

2. Assuming a victory over Buffalo, the Chiefs still have a chance to play a playoff game at Arrowhead. That is possible if Indianapolis loses to Cincinnati and Jacksonville beats Atlanta. Then, they would play Jacksonville at Arrowhead either Saturday or Sunday of next week.
3. Also, the Chiefs could play at Arrowhead against Indianapolis if they beat Buffalo Sunday and both Indianapolis and Jacksonville loses.

4. Further, it is even possible to still make the playoffs even if Buffalo beats the Chiefs. Then, they could go to Pittsburgh, New England, Indianapolis, or not go anywhere. If so, this would be the only season that Marty's Chiefs would not be in post season competition.

5. One of the leading warning signs for drug abuse are teenagers who become pregnant. And, one of the warning signs for teenage pregnancy is childhood abuse. For more information on warning signs, call COMBAT at 881-4626. Thus, young ladies who are abused in childhood are much likely to become teenage mothers."

ENTITIES: Jim Nunnelly, Chiefs, Denver Broncos, Ike Gardner, Harry Sypert, Marty, Steve Bono, Danan Hughes, Elway, Grbac, Carl Peterson, Super Bowl, Pittsburgh, San Francisco, Green Bay, Kordell Stewart, Joe Montana, Steve Young, Gene Washington, Jerry Rice, Roger Craig, Bart Starr, Paul Hornung, Willie Davis, Reggie White, Brett Favre, Terrell Davis, McCaffrey, Rod Smith, Donna Stewart

No Chiefs game this week. No red and white Chiefs merchandise to buy. No more red Fridays. Yes, the fat lady has sung for the final time this 1997 season. But, before we look ahead to the Super Bowl possibilities, let's take one final ""look back"" at the Denver game and cry just one more time.

I guess Ol' Ike Gardner and hateable Harry Sypert had the last laugh. Both predicted the Chiefs would fold last Sunday and blight any hopes of playing any more this year. Sypert said it was Marty's coaching. And Ike reminded us of the 1995 Indianapolis game two years ago under Steve Bono. Why did we lose?

Some say it was the sticky stuff on Denver's new uniforms or Danan Hughes' penalty and two dropped passes. Others say it was the Elway factor. Or the poor clock management. And, most will blame it on Grbac and the horrible defense.

And my personal opinion is that the pressure of the play-offs and the myth of home field advantage both played a major part, and that's because we did not play our usual ""make it happen"" game. And, there is no such thing as a home field advantage in championship games. In short, where the game is played means absolutely nothing. And, don't let Carl Peterson fool you about the fans being a part of the game. Baloney! Every team has to play half of its game in hostile territory, and they win any way.
Really, it's all about who plays the best on game day. The team that tackles better; passes better; manages the clock better; runs the ball better; and scores more points will win the game.

But, four teams are still alive in the chase for the Super Bowl trophy on January 25 at San Diego. That's Pittsburgh, San Francisco, Green Bay and of course the Denver Broncos. Here's a little something about each:

**Pittsburgh** -- One of the NFL doormats that has four Super Bowl Championships behind (74,75,78,79), and, even made it again in 1995 to the now-defunct Dallas Cowboys. What difference two years make, especially with the maturing Kordell Stewart.

**San Francisco** -- Land of gold-plated quarterbacks. Like Joe Montana and Steve Young. And God-sent receivers like Gene Washington and Jerry Rice. And an all-time, all purpose back, like Roger Craig. All of these add up to an unmatched championship feeling and a respected West Coast offense. They too have been to the Big Dance and won five times (81,84,88,89,94). A dominant force in the NFL.

**Green Bay** -- Lombarde country where names like Bart Starr, Paul Hornung, Willie Davis, Reggie White, and Brett Favre; the frozen Tunda where cold, ice, snow, sleet and all bad weather are greeted as we greet warms sunny days. Green Bay has been in the play-offs 17 times. The Pack is always back. Look for the Pack to go back to the Super Bowl.

**Denver** -- In spite of Elway, the Broncos have never won the Super Bowl in four trips. They have a solid running game with Terrell Davis. Two pair of good hands from McCaffrey and Rod Smith. This is Elway's last chance at the big dance. Will he get there? Doubtful, but we will all know on Sunday.
The Monday Niters will make their final guesses for the year then. So long until then. Donna Stewart predicts Denver over Pittsburgh and Denver and Green Bay in the Super Bowl!"
