The trumpet has sounded the end of the NFL’s 2003 season. And the Chiefs actually survived pretty well, considering the number of Ike-led, Chiefs’ bashers out there. It is as if these Ike-like people expected the Chiefs to go undefeated, just because they got off to a good start. After beating the Chicago Bears on Sunday, the Chiefs finish out at 13-3, not bad for a team that has not been in a playoff game for seven years. But another season emerges, this week. It’s called the playoffs. However, all 32 teams are not a part. . . . only twelve get the chance to go to the next round and the other twenty leave with nothing. And no matter what you think of the much-maligned, Chiefs’ run offense, the Chiefs are the number two-seeded team in the AFC, just behind New England with a 14-2 record. So here’s the wrap-up:
*   Chiefs’ three losses were all on the road (Cincinnati, Denver, Minnesota)
*   The Chiefs will not have to play this week, being awarded a bye for their second best, 13-3 record.
*   The Chiefs will play on January 10 or 11 at Arrowhead against either Indianapolis, Tennessee or Baltimore.
*   Priest Holmes now holds the all-time NFL record for touchdowns in a single season with 27.

*   Chiefs will have eight representatives to the Pro Bowl, in February, in Honolulu. Except for Shields, Woods, and Gonzalez, the other five were not drafted by the Chiefs. Green, Hall, Holmes, Richardson, and Roaf were all acquired from other teams.
*   Chiefs offense ranked number one in scoring, in the entire NFL.
*   St. Louis is the number two-seeded team in the NFC and they will not play this week, either. An I-70 Super Bowl is highly unlikely (but could happen).

Bottom line, stop hating on the Chiefs. Considering where they have come from, they have done well. In retrospect, it was totally unrealistic to think that we would not lose at least three games. Did you ever think we’d see the day when we depended on the Chiefs’ offense, over the defense? Jason Whitlock had a party for Priest Holmes, making up for his faulty prediction that Holmes would be physically unable to perform this year. Ike’s grandson, Isaiah Gardner, was given a cameo shot on the regional television broadcast, during the Chiefs-Bears game. By the way, our newest member, Bubba Cleaver, had the most correct predictions, this year, at 14. He voted against the Chiefs once, against Denver. Maybe that Bubba is being unduly influenced by that Ike. Now let’s all unite and get behind the Chiefs, as they launch the second half of the NFL season. . . the playoffs. We’ll have Super Bowl-related predictions for you again, on January 9, 2004, right here in this column. . . . . .

ENTITIES: Oakland Raiders, Kansas City Chiefs, Jim Nunnelly, Ike Gardner, Ike Paul Booker, Ollie Hubbard, Charles Allen, Carl Clark, Lloyd Greenfield, Ed West, Seattle, Arrowhead Stadium, New York Jets, Gunther, Peterson, Grbac, Dishman, Woods

First off, we must all bow and congratulate Ike Gardner on his accurate prediction of last week’s Chiefs-Seattle game. He stood alone in forecasting the undesirable outcome, leaving the Chiefs still in pursuit of the play-offs. While Ike generally goes against the Chiefs, he is no less of a fan. Like all of us, he desperately wants the Chiefs to play like champions. As evidenced last week in Seattle, the Chiefs are an “up and down” team, even though they work hard to correct the apparent mistakes. So, Ike’s lack of blind faith is representative of so many of us. You can’t tell which team will show up.

Thankfully, the Chiefs play well at home. In fact, the only team to beat the Chiefs at Arrowhead was ironically Seattle. Six other teams, including play-off contenders like New England and Detroit, fell to the thunderous crowd at Arrowhead. This is all part of the adventurous odyssey the Chiefs have expe- rienced in this 1999 season. Playing well at times and then playing poorly at other times, leaving the fans with the reserved position when it comes to talking post-season play-offs. Ike, Paul Booker, and Ollie Hubbard would say that when it comes to the big game, the Chiefs always fold, especially down the stretch. They point to the 1995 season, when the Chiefs won the AFC West Division outright with a 13-3 record, only to fall to lowly Indianapolis in the second round game at Arrowhead. So, we are all understandably cautious when this part of the year approaches. Is this year going to be different or will it be like all the others? Will we win or will we fold?

Obviously, the regular Chiefs’ supporters say the Chiefs will win and claim a play-off spot, regardless of what Seattle does. Charles Allen, Carl Clark and Lloyd Greenfield unequivocally believe this scenario. Ike, Paul and turncoat Ed West think that Oakland will win and pour once again the Chiefs are a “once and done” team. Both sides have merit and appeal and evenly divided, 50 percent say yes and 50 percent say no. But the answer lies not in the stars. The answer lies in just how well the Chiefs respond to the hometown crowd and play-off pressure. So far, they have folded countless times. However, most people didn’t think the Chiefs would have a winning season, let alone make the play-offs. For certain, the Chiefs cannot advance to the play-offs unless they beat the Raiders. Ironically, all of us Monday Niters believe the New York Jets will beat Seattle.

Bottom line for me: look for the Chiefs to not only win, but become AFC west champions in the process. They’ll be 10-6 and all alone atop the AFC West and Gunther will be American Conference Coach of the Year.

column1: ENTITIES: Jim ""Grand Dad"" Nunnelly, Kansas City Chiefs, Arizona Cardinals, KCTV 5, Lloyd Greenfield, Charles Booker, Ed West, Carl Clark, Ike Gardner, Robert Lee Allen, Dallas, Washington, Redskins, Marty Schottenheimer, Tony Richardson, Priest Holmes, Ol' Ike Gardner, Ollie Hubbard

It was not the usual Monday Night Footballers meeting. The meal was extraordinary, marshmellowed sweet potatoes, red-topped meat loaf, and cabbage sprinkled heavily with polish sausage, and the handsome, yellow cornbread. The guys ate but there was no joy. No heavy conversation, no real differences of opinion. Even young Robert Lee Allen wasn't around to add youth and spice to this rapidly-aging group of football fans.

Charles Booker and Ed West nodded. Carl Clark talked. Ike, riding high on his ability to predict accurately, sat in a chair he thought was his throne. To top off the misery, there was, wo, dull teams playing, Dallas and Washington, neither had won a game. Hey, there's something.

That was the best thing we could say about our ""weakest link"" Chiefs. They are not WINLESS. After all, they beat still-winless Washington Redskins. And while we can talk about the many disappointments and shame the ""the Cheaps"" have brought us, there is still hope. And, just how do we propose to produce hope?

It's not in the unfulfilled passing game. It's not in the once-heralded defense. It's not in the Super Bowl-promised coaching. It's in what Marty Schottenheimer has been trying to tell us, the running game. Amid all the interceptions, dropped passes, missed tackles at Arrowhead last Sunday, Priest Holmes showed us, for the second time, he gen run, even without Tony Richardson's blocking. So, let's not listen to Ol' Ike Gardner, let's put our store in the running game. Let's slash left and right, five to 10 yards at a time.
It just might happen Sunday. Priest Holmes may become the first Chief in history to be named American Conference Player of the Week, twice in one year. Is it possible?
I'll ask Robert Lee Allen. He'll know.

You would think that these Monday Niters are just a bunch of Jayhawkers. Or maybe just a bunch of apostles for the Big 12. They really think the Big 12 is all that. Okay, but what if Roy Williams returns to North Carolina? The Tar Heels want to make Roy the highest paid college coach, replacing Duke's Coach K. That's why ol' Roy is dodging the media and deflecting by saying all he wants to do, this week, is concentrate on Kansas basketball. That means to me that when this week is over, I'll consider and maybe even announce his departure to North Carolina. Thus, our Monday Niters believe that Roy will win the national championship, dominate the sports' news for three weeks, and then, surprise everyone with his well-orchestrated plans. Anyway, who knows what Roy will do? But, contrary to my colleagues, I believe that the title will go East to Syracuse."

column1: Isn’t it funny that we are all worried about the Chiefs’ defense? It wasn’t that long ago when we were all equally concerned about the Chiefs offense. So it goes. The vicissitudes of professional football are what makes for the continued, record-breaking popularity of the game. And it will all start this Sunday, with the Chiefs visiting arch-rival, the Denver Broncos.

Our readers will remember that the Chiefs actually won 13 regular-season games last year (the best in the National Football League). While some of the games could have gone either way, the Chiefs generally rose to the last-minute occasion. That is a good thing.

With Boerigter out for the season and Johnnie Morton crippled, all of a sudden, everyone is talking about how the Chiefs are lacking in receivers. Do not sweat it! The Chiefs were overstocked in receivers last year, anyway. Remember, how much Tony Gonzalez used to complain about his few touches. And don’t forget about Eddie Kenniston, who I believe to be the “best” Chiefs receiver. And Dante Hall will be all right, since every special teams coach will be geared to limit his return yardage.

Denver will pose a tough test, especially for our defense. But, most of us think the Chiefs offense will prevail, especially with the stable of running backs. Look for the Chiefs to win, by a narrow margin. For more on the Kansas City Chiefs season opener against Denver, visit www.kccall.com and click on to ‘web sports’."

## Kansas City Chiefs versus Cleveland Browns, 12 noon, KCTV 5
They can and will do it, again.

Potpourri is a French word, meaning miscellaneous stuff. And, because the Kansas City Chiefs are “okay” at this point, we cannot say much about them, one way or the other. I'm never sure whether they are the “best of the worst” or “worst of the best”. Either way, let’s just look at several, unrelated things this week and just comment. Here goes:

*   **Chiefs are in the very position they were last year at this time, 7-4.** They went on to a 10-6 finish but missed the playoffs. General consensus is that the Chiefs will make the playoffs, this year.
*   Surprise, surprise. Priest Holmes won’t be back this year. Is that really news, y'all?

*   Booker and his stately wife, Carolyn, have been hosting Thanksgiving weekend for over 30 years. Coming mostly from Little Rock and bunking out where-ever they can, thirty Wilson family members attended this year. As usual, the guys visited local historical sites like the 6902.
*   Larry Johnson or LaDainian Thompson for NFL Most Valuable Player? It just depends on which team ends higher. San Diego wins, it will be LD. Chiefs win bigger, it will be LJ.
*   Look for Samie Parker to be the difference-maker at Cleveland. He and Kennison ought to dazzle the Cleveland Browns secondary. It's about time that Samie do something!

Only two of the Monday Nite Footballers seem to be suffering from delusions of grandeur; Charles Allen out of loyalty and “Granddad” Nunnelly, because he is a much better politician than a, football prognosticator.

While Dick Vermeil focuses on the receivers, inability to separate from the defenders, the Monday Niters insist that Trent Green invariably throws into coverage and is too inept to throw the ball away to avoid a loss of yardage or to avoid an interception. According to the group, several questions need to be answered. How long will Vermeil let his previous relationship with Green cloud his judgment? How long will Carl Peterson’s previous relationship with Vermeil cloud his judgment? How long will the fans of Kansas City continue to support a floundering team that sports such a dismal record? The answer to the last question may be found next door to our “so called” baseball team.

column1: ENTITIES: Kansas City Chiefs, Chicago Bears, Jim ""Grand Dad"" Nunnelly, Dale Carter, ""Mighty Mouse"" McMillian, Derrick Thomas, Marvcus Patton, Donnie Edwards, Grbac, Moon, Shehee, Cloud, Anders, Morris, Sammie Sosa, Mark McGwire

Sporting a new secondary and a new head coach, the Kansas City Chiefs will take the field Sunday, fresh from a disappointing 7-9 season. Dale Carter has moved on to Denver and “Mighty Mouse” McMillian is no longer a target for opposing passers. Derrick Thomas promises to correct his erratic play and behavior and may be headed to another Pro Bowl. Newcomer Marvcus Patton and veteran Donnie Edwards sure up the linebacking crew. It’s all about defense...because there is not much offense. The jury and fans everywhere are down on Grbac and “Medicare” Moon. One is inadequate and the other needs to give it up. Thus, without a quarterback, the locals have to depend on the committee of running backs, composed of Shehee, Cloud, Anders, and Morris. This law-firm sounding quartet may offer some hope to an otherwise, failed offense.

BREAKING NEWS: Sammie Sosa, Chicago Cubs outfielder, hit his 59th homerun of the season Thursday. He is five ahead of the pace Mark McGwire set last year. With one more homerun, Sosa will be the only player in Major League history to hit 60 homeruns in two separate seasons.
# To Beat Chicago, The Chiefs Must:
*   Let these running backs run ‘til the defense tires out.
*   Win the special teams battle by getting good field position.
*   Avoid before the snap and interference penalties altogether.
*   Either record three sacks or take the ball away three times.

column1: Now that we are partly recovered from last week's surprise win, we can honestly say the Chiefs are 100 percent unpredictable. We thought the offense was strong. It wasn't. We thought the defense was weak. It wasn't. We always longed for the big plays. We got them, last Sunday. And, whether we understand it or not, we really have won three games and are in the chase for the division lead. Enough said.

Now the Chiefs travel to Miami, to face Rickey Williams and the Dolphins. All of us unanimously believe that the Chiefs will prevail. But they will be unpredictable. The sad thing about the Chiefs is you will never know what part of their team will show up. We count on the offense and the defense wins the game. We believed in Derrick Johnson and Jared Allen makes us think of Derrick Thomas. And we thought Priest Holmes was a runner, only to find out he has major receiving skills. In a phrase, the Chiefs have become apostles of volatility and we just don't know what they will do, next. But, there is one thing that we always count on.

That's the Arkansas hospitality that Carol Booker will display, this coming Monday. She'll have that corn bread just steaming hot. And she'll prepare her own secret recipe of ""greens a la Carol"". And what's more, she will delight her table with the best of garnishments. It's like she is preparing for and honoring dignitaries of the highest order. And her proud husband, Paul, will smile and receive us, as if he had a hand in preparing this meal. And this good food will spur good, healthy football conversation. But, for once, we will all be on the same page. However, we will have to find ""something"" to argue about. For, this time, we all know the Chiefs will win big, making us all look good on our predictions. Sadly, these Chiefs have become unpredictably predictable. And it's a nice thing to know that Carol Booker is still around, serving her spread, in a predictable fashion.
Look for the Chiefs to devour the Dolphins by 20. You can count on it!"

I'm still faithful that the Chiefs (will) win. Someday. Sometime..."" - Jim Nunnelly

When COMBAT programs talk about identifying children who are likely to commit crimes and do drugs, they utilize risk factors. These 'red flags' indicate a high likelihood that trouble lies ahead, especially in doing drugs and committing crimes. Things like failing in school; fighting; dropping out of school; getting pregnant; losing a parent to jail; being a child of a drug using parent and even, being sick, are not to be overlooked. Each of these are bad enough alone but when they are all linked together, it surely leads to drugs and crime. In big words, this is called “cumulative adversity.” And what does this have to do with the Chiefs?

Things just keep happening to the Chiefs that prevent them from winning. Poor clock management. Just reflect on the fact we have no time outs when we need them. Then, there's the dropped passes. Alexander, Thomas, and even Snoop Minnis, have missed some passes right in their hands. Not just a few times but 10 that I can think of in their last three games. And what about our once-heralded defense. Missed tackles and there is no more pass rush in Kansas City. And last but not least, there are those interceptions, most of them coming in the critical final quarter or final minutes. What's the point?

Or shall we say, what is the answer? Well, our once-loved Chiefs are not only suffering from these things individually, they are now facing “cumulative adversity.” And that usually leads to a depression. Which leads to poor play. Which leads to more losses. Which leads to less fan support.
Talk about ""cumulative adversity.” We have found the enemy and the enemy is us.

column1: Cleaver hosted the Monday Niters this week. And while he predicts that the Chiefs' defense will help them win against Arizona, all he talked about was the people and plans of the St. James United Methodist church. Of course, it helps that Ike, Carl, and Ed are members of his church and it is little wonder that he only talks to them when we meet. However, it is pretty clear that Bubba Cleaver is an avid Chiefs' fan.

Of course, Cleaver is no stranger to the Chiefs. One of his 520 marriage ceremonies was for Carl Peterson, general manager and president of the Chiefs. Also, Cleaver and his wife Dianne flew to Tokyo to watch the Chiefs in pre-season. And it isn't unusual to see a Chiefs' player or two in the ever-increasing St. James congregation. And while our purpose for being at his house was the Chiefs, he chose to frequently edify the people at St. James. Even as Congressman, he keeps St. James uppermost in his everydayness. In fact, he is taking his 100-voice choir to Washington to celebrate National Day of Prayer in February.
Throughout the entire evening, he alternated his conversations. First, St. James. Then, the Chiefs. But, mostly about St. James and all the fine people there. And, then back to the Chiefs.

And thank God the Chiefs gave him something to talk about. Elated with last week's showing against lowly San Francisco, he has proudly predicted that T'amba and the Rugrats will terrorize the Cardinals. And boldly, he is already looking ahead, thinking that Pittsburg is next in line for the Chiefs to beat. Wait up a minute, Rev! Let's not go too far, too fast, with too little. Cleaver and his exemplary congregation may need to lead a prayer service for the Chiefs before they take on Pittsburg. But for right now, let's just beat Arizona and then, we can talk more about that, after Sunday.
K C 33, Arizona 16 St. James, priceless.
**Interesting note:** Of the six African American head coaches in the NFL, not one has a starting black quarterback. Makes you go hmmmm-mm!"

# Monday Nite Footballers View Super Bowl For Ninth Time; Take Issue With Experts

SUPER BOWL XIX will be remembered by sports fans as the day when Miami and quarterback Dan Marino left their hearts in San Francisco. Out-classed, out-tackled, out-defensed; everything belonged to the National Football Conference Forty-Niners.

Other members present included: Carl and Marylynn Clark; Vee Maxey and Big Ike Gardner; Paul and Carolyn Booker; Edna Brannock and Tom Brown; James Nunnelly; Ollie Hubbard; Ed West and Charles Allen DDS. Jerry Price and C. Ashley were not present.
“Montana was chosen the most valuable player. Wendell Tyler, Roger Craig, and Eric Wright, should have been (chosen),” Jim Nunnelly.

For the ninth time, Monday Nite Footballers gathered to monitor the annual event. As usual, the Monday Nite Footballers feasted and at times took issue with the events and comments of the day. The ten-member group has been watching football together for over 15 years.

Five of eight Monday Nite Footballers predicted a San Francisco victory. They were Charles Allen, DDS, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, and Ollie Hubbard. Ed West, Big Ike Gardner and Jim Nunnelly favored Miami.
Here are some of the comments recorded at the Paula and Carolyn Booker residence about Super Bowl XIX:

1. Roger Craig and Wendell Tyler were chosen “Most Valuable Players,” opposing the network's selection of Joe Montana. Craig, who recorded three touchdowns, and Tyler played consistently throughout the game;
2. The group disagreed vehemently with Don Shula's post-game comments, when he singled out punter, Reggie Roby, as the key reason for the Miami loss. Roby had a 40 plus punting average for the day;

3. The group felt that Dan Marino, 23-year-old Miami quarterback was “welcomed” to the NFL. San Francisco emphasized “rushing Marino” rather than emphasizing coverage on the San Francisco super receivers;
4. The group said “hello” to Buck and Georgia Emma Johnson Buchanan, who were at Palo Alto to view the Super Bowl in person.
Monday Nite Football’s post game comments included:
“Terse, concise and to the point,” Charles Allen, DDS.
“The best team (San Francisco) won,” Tom Brown.
“Dan Marino grew up today,” “Big Ike” Gardner, historian and co-founder.
“It went the way I wanted,” Carl Clark.
“I went with the winner (it was) a well-fought gridiron battle,” Ollie Hubbard.
Other key events of the occasion were:

1. The menu for the day which included: hoghead cheese, greens, blackeyed peas, baked chicken and rice, barbequed ribs, fried chicken, macaroni and cheese, jello salad, green bean casserole, potato salad, beef brisket, Allen’s ambrosia, and spaghetti with meat;
2. The group praised Paul and Carolyn Booker for their hospitality on a very cold “Super” January day;
3. The group’s special guest was Walter Livingston, an original member of the 15-year-old Monday Nite Football Group."

With Super Bowl XX just two weeks away, there is no real indication of just who is going to be the final two. The only thing certain is that the Monday Niters are beginning to agree more, now that the field of possible teams has been narrowed to four. Miami and New England in the AFC and Chicago and Los Angeles in the NFC.
1. Most Monday Niters feel that Chicago and Miami will be in the ""final game.""
2. Only C. Ashley, contract electrician, predicted before the playoffs began that Miami and Chicago would be the ""final pair"".
3. Two believe that the LA Rams will be NFL representative on January 26.
4. One Monday Niter thinks New England will be the AFC representative prior to the playoffs, no one voted that the Patriots would be.
5. Tom Brown, Paul Booker, Jerry Price and Ed West did not cast votes for the final pair.
**Picks For The Final Pair For Super Bowl XX**
The 17th annual Monday Niters New Year's Eve Party went off this past year with a bundle of contrasts; mixing the traditional with the non-traditional.
First the traditional. As always, the party included food for the guests; beef brisket, ham, three bean salad, potato salad, cole slaw and avocados, all nicely planned.
Then there were the beverages. The finest in the land.

And as before, Ed and Celeste West offered their spacious third floor penthouse for the event. The Monday Niters decorated with the traditional ""Happy New Year"" sign. The Monday Niters and ladies sported the traditional party-costume hats.

Now the non-traditional. The 100 or so guests were entertained with fine music. Carefully sprinkled alongside the latest sounds were old ""45"" favorites; ""Midnight Train to Georgia"" and ""The Bus Stop"" and ""Have You Seen Her"" and several others. The music was orchestrated by KCDJ Nate Littlejohn.
At the midnight hour, instead of Auld Lang Syne, the Monday Niters chose to play ""Friends"" by Dionne Warwick, Stevie Wonder, Elton John and Gladys Knight.
The song was a special tribute to those ""special ones"" and to those guests who had shared many wonderful hours with the Monday Niters.

Another non-traditional aspect was the age shift. Usually the Monday Niters attract the 30-40 age group. This time there were at least a dozen people who were in their early twenties. They kept the party going until around 4 a.m. Some golden agers ""hung on"" but the majority went to rest right after they played ""Friends.""

But, all in all, another fine social event. Others paid big money to celebrate New Year's Eve but there was not another place in town where they shared more fun, food and foolishness. Another ""winner"" and congrats to all those who made it possible."

TITLE: Monday Nite Footballers Commentary: The Texas 'No Pass-No Play' Law
ENTITIES: Texas, Jim Nunnelly, Monday Nite Footballers, Supreme Court, Isaac Gardner, Kansas City, No Pass-No Play Law

The state of Texas has a law concerning where athletics stand in the high school. The law is called the ""no pass, no play"" law. Simply stated, if a student wants to participate in extracurricular activities, like football, basketball or band, he or she must receive a passing grade in every course taken.

Monday Night Footballers debated the question about the fairness of such a law. Right now, the law is awaiting Supreme Court review. Is it right to deny a child participation in sports because he or she does not pass a course? Passed in January of this year, this law has become the most controversial piece of legislation ever passed in regards to educational/athletic circles.

First of all, there are those who say that the first objective of school should be learning. Those who like the law openly assert that a child should know more than the rules of an athletic contest. He or she ought to know math, English, and the sciences. They see a college star signing a million dollar contract and, at the same time, murder the English language.

Moreover, the proponents of this law agree that there is a 3,000 to one chance that a high school athlete will play professional sports. There is ""life after football"". We all know what happens to all those high school stars who fade into nowhere because they could not read or write or complete a job application. One Monday Niter, Isaac Gardner holds steadfast to this position.

Now, the other side does not differ that much. But, they do believe that it is unfair to stop an athlete or a gymnast from participating because that student fails one course. They believe that it is ridiculous to think everyone will do well in every subject.

Further, they say that the law discriminates against blacks and minorities. Such a rule could threaten the stability of an inner city school league, anywhere in the United States. Maybe, even here in Kansas City. For we all know that many high school athletes are ""marginal, at best"" when it comes to the classroom.

In short, the opponents shoot for a tougher requirement on the total grade point average. That approach would allow a student to fail in one class, but would require him or her to do well in the remaining classes.

Which is right, no pass, no play or a tougher grade point requirement? Seven Monday Niters favor the tougher grade point. Though, no such rule exists now, we do believe the time has come when the student athlete will be required to perform much better in the classroom before every being allowed to participate in athletics.
,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,2826089639_2,Monday Nite Football,,"Charles A. Allen, C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jerry Price, Jim Nunnelly, Ed West","---
ENTITIES: Charles A. Allen, C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jerry Price, Jim Nunnelly, Ed West

The day has come and gone when the athlete can ""get by"" on his or her athletic prowess alone. We firmly believe that educators, families, the community and the student athlete, all will be better off when we require the student to perform better in the classroom. Though it is punitive in some ways, it does remind us that there are people who are not willing to see society as a whole crumble because our athletes cannot compete in the open job market.
Monday Niters, welcome the refreshing trend, doing well on the field and in the classroom.
It is something to think about!
Charles A. Allen, C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jerry Price, Jim Nunnelly, Ed West."

ENTITIES: Monday Nite Footballers, Jim Nunnelly, Lincoln Academy, Northeast, Charles Harris, Sam Cofield, Kansas City, Lafayette, St. Joseph, New York Times, USA Today
The above score was the only game in town last week.
Never before in the history of Interscholastic League football has there been so much talk about one game. Everyone is asking, ""Did Coach Price run up the score on winless Northeast?""

The whole issue has cast a dubious cloud on Kansas City high school sports. Many, including national newspapers, have considered the game newsworthy and as a tribute to Lincoln Academy. Others, closer to home, say the score will have a horrible effect on the entire football program.
Before we comment either way, let's look at the details.
1. Lincoln Academy scored 18 times against Northeast.
2. Northeast played 13 freshmen, mainly because veteran players were facing discipline problems.
3. Lincoln Academy played 18 players, mainly because it had to reduce its squad because several players failed to make the required grade point average.
4. Coach Charles Harris, Northeast coach did not feel that Lincoln ran up the score.
5. Partly because of the score and mainly because of the lack of upperclass players, Northeast has decided not to play its last game this week.
6. As a result of the victory, Lincoln Academy is the only team from the League to have a guaranteed spot in the state playoffs. They play Lafayette in St. Joseph on Wednesday.
7. The score attracted the New York Times and USA Today and all the local media. Some calls to the District office voiced grave concern for such a game.
It is really unfortunate that we have to talk so much about this game. Sam Cofield, League District Coordinator, has articulated the concerns well.

Basically, he believes that the score could have been held down. Over the years, we have always held that Cofield is one of the most knowledgeable and influential people in any sports program anywhere. And obviously, anything that threatens the existence of high school sports should be addressed (as Cofield has done.)

But, Monday Night Footballers, look at another side. Lincoln Academy has accomplished a great deed. With only 18 players, they have gained a spot in the state playoffs. More importantly, they did it, representing a school district that requires high and tough academic requirements at the school.
Really, the young men can be proud of their success. We, as longtime football fans, have to congratulate a team that has blended athletic prowess with academic achievement.
While the score can be debated, the fact is we have some real winners at Lincoln Academy.
It is something to think about."

# Necessary Progress: College Football Super Bowl?
Progress begins with the belief that what is necessary is possible.""
That phrase was the guiding force that led the NCAA Postseason Committee to approve a national championship game. Before we comment, let's take a quick look back to last January 2.

That was the day when Miami took on Penn State in the ratings-minded Fiesta Bowl. Though mythical, the game was one of the few times ""an actual game"" had been played to decide the number one team. And of course, college football fans loved it.

Individually and collectively, we in Monday Nite Football, support the subcommittee's position. It is a long, overdue concept. Yes, let there be a game to decide which team is the best. While Ed West cautions us about the season being longer, he still wholly supports the notion.

Moreover, Carl Clark specifies his position by saying that a playoff system be employed to decide who’s even to play for the ""final two."" And because there was no system in place this last year, Ollie Hubbard thinks the present system (voting) is highly inappropriate. He believes that Oklahoma was really the best team in 1986 and because they lost an early, season game to Miami, they were literally removed from the running.

Just why did the postseason committee approve a national championship game this year? Especially since fans like us have hun-gered so long for such a ""mini super bowl""? Was it the television revenue, and Lord knows there would be plenty?
Was it to save face after the Fiesta Bowl showed the way? Was it a deep-seated desire to copy after the NFL’s Super Bowl? Or was it just plain progress?

Whatever the reason, the time has come for sportswriters and their ""unmitigated arrogance"" to step aside and let the best team win, on the field and not with the stroke of a pen. That’s what Ike Gardner says and we all agree.

Obviously, we have always favored progress in sports, especially those changes that are fan-inspired. And it is our unanimous opinion that sport fans everywhere will welcome this progressive step. What took the NCAA so long?
Our hats are off to the postseason subcommittee for believing it is now ""necessary"" to employ an actual game to decide. More importantly we’re glad they now believe it’s possible."

TITLE: Monday Nite Footballers Commentary: Calling...Buck Buchanan
ENTITIES: Buck Buchanan, Jim Nunnelly, Kansas City Chiefs, Jackson County Sports Authority, Miller Lite, All Pro Advertising, NFL Alumni, Eddie Robinson, Grambling, Governor Ashcroft, Black Chamber
Working in your calling is half praying. That saying applies to Buck Buchanan, recently nominated by the Legislature to the Jackson County Sports Authority.
Before we explain that proverb, let's examine why we have to write about Buck.

First, Buck is a major part of Kansas City Sports history. He was an all-pro defensive lineman for the Kansas City Chiefs—from the first day they came to Jackson County. He is quiet, unassuming and articulate. And, he's one of the few Chiefs around who relates well to the ""fan on the corner"" or the fan in the stands.

Secondly, Buck is well known today, a decade or so after his playing fame. He can be heard after each Chief's games, explaining inside information to hungry sports fan. There his articulation comes alive.
And, as part of his duties as NFL Alumni youth director, he also speaks to youngsters all over the country about the complex world of sports.

And, as a special favor to us in Monday Nite Foot-ball, he spoke to a St. Louis group about his days at Grambling and his special relationship with the winningest coach in college football, Eddie Robinson. His funny seriousness kept the audience spellbound for an hour.
So, you can see, he's deep, deep into sports.

Last, Buck is a true businessman, mainly self-employed. And you guessed it, he makes most of his living in sports. Besides being one of the well-known faces in the famous Miller Lite commercials, he owns his own business. One that will imprint anything on everything, especially sports items such as T-shirts, jackets, golf club, etc. Principally called ""All Pro Advertising,"" his business supplements his promotional contract with Miller brewery.

So, you see, Buck knows, lives and loves sports. More importantly, he has a child-like excitement about the whole, complicated world of sports. Anywhere there's a sports event, you can find Buck. Further, he has risen to a viable role in the black business community, as president of the Black Chamber. He's clear headed, clean and cautious in his approach to his life in sports.

We, as an organized sports fan organization and speaking for the ""fan-on-the-street,"" salute the Jackson County Legislature for nominating Buck to the Sports Authority. In addition, we urge Governor Ashcroft to appoint a man, who is deeply involved in the profession he represents.
Buck truly works in his calling. And he's good at it, too."

# Instant Replay System: Fans Win Again

Right up there with the three-point shot, the designated hitter and the photo finish, the approval for an instant replay system in pro-football is a major victory for the fans. The NFL will now allow an eighth official to overrule a questionable play from the press box—something that the Monday Niters have long waited for. Particularly, Ike Gardner is tickled pink.

While the whole world welcomes another electronic addition to pro football, many say that it's just too soon to tell what problems this new system will cause. Others say the game will be delayed unnecessarily. Still others assert that ""the man"" in the press box is not mistake-free. But, the Monday Niters as lifetime sports fans, have only good things to say about it.
Let's examine the rule and see how it applies. And then we'll comment both individually and collectively.

First off, the ""high priest"" official will have two televisions to watch—one that homeviewers see and the other that provides different angles of the play. They can only rule on touchdown, out-of-bound and incomplete pass plays.

This eighth official will signal his objection on the play and then overrule the ground official. Also, officials may ask the ""high priest"" for an opinion on ""any"" play. However, he must respond to both situations in twenty seconds. Simple enough. Let's comment.

Of course, everyone knows Ike Gardner and the Monday Niters have waited a long time for this system. And like Ed West, we know that the ground officials are human and certainly need help from time to time. Paul Booker quickly points out that the referees are ""not God"" and they can and do make mistakes. All in all, he believes that the system is a ""very positive"" move for everyone—the fans, the owners, the coaches and even the ground officials themselves.

Another Monday Niter suggests that the system also is a move for ""justice"". Ollie Hubbard says it will give the fans in the stadium a chance to see questionable plays—like the home viewer. Openly, Charles Allen and C. Ashley state that it will eliminate the controversy and arguments that always follow a questionable call; it may even reduce the number of ""unsportsmanlike conduct"" calls.

And there you have it. As you can see, we Monday Niters believe in the instant replay system. And while we are grateful, Carl Clarke reminds us that this replay thing can go too far. We really don't want a ""Big Eye"" approach that can overrule everything.

The three chosen areas are ""just enough"" to be effective. We don't want to see another layer of decision making on every decision. It must be ""viewed"" as just another tool, another aid to right wrongs and keep the game alive and responsive to the fans.

Like all other fan-inspired rule changes, it will be both perilous and painful at times. Starting with the pre season, the rule should prove ""very positive"" and it shouldn't alter the game that much. And pretty soon, like the three-point play, the designated hitter and the photo finish, we'll accept it as just another rule, to keep us interested in the game we love.

1972. That year makes Johnny Miller speak up. And, when he talks about that year, he smiles. He gives you a I-will-correct-you look, if he thinks you do not have the facts about 1972. Usually, Johnny Miller is a quiet, unassuming man. But, when it's time, he will speak up.
Before we explain about 1972, let's look in on the ""usual"" Johnny Miller. His retirement. His religious and helpful ways. And his memories about past NAIA basketball tournaments.

You see, Johnny and his wife, Ovara, have witnessed twenty five consecutive NAIA tournaments. They were there Monday night for the finals in their usual front-row seats at Kemper. Quiet, faithful, loyal.

**Super Bowl Predictions – How Far Will They Go?**
# Odd Combination Of Super Bowl Possibilities
At our last meeting, Monday NITE FOOTBALLERS sat around and put together all kinds of playoff combinations. While debating what teams would go to Super Bowl, Sunday, January 30.
It is possible but not likely that:
1. Two teams from the NFC Central could play for the NFC Championship. (Minnesota, Detroit, Green Bay)
2. Two teams from the AFC West could play for the AFC Championship. (Kansas City, LA Raiders, Denver)
3. Two teams from the Texas could play in the Super Bowl. (Houston and Dallas)
4. Two teams from California could play in the Super Bowl. (LA Raiders & San Francisco)
5. Two teams from New York could play in the Super Bowl. (Buffalo Bills and the New York Giants)
6. Buffalo Bills (AFC) and Dallas (NFC) could repeat as Super Bowl entries.
7. The Kansas City Chiefs and Dallas Cowboys, two teams originated in Dallas, Texas, could play in the Super Bowl.
8. Two Montana - led teams could play in the Super Bowl. (San Francisco and K.C.)
9. Two Dan Reeves - coached teams could play in the Super Bowl. (Denver and New York Giants)
10. Two second-place finished teams could play in the Super Bowl (Denver AFC West and NY Giants NFC East)"

2. Nick Lowery, Derrick Thomas, Albert Lewis, Neil Smith are expected to make All Pro this year.

3. Former Chief Otis Taylor, perhaps the best wide receiver in NFL history and Frank White, perennial Golden Glove second baseman from the Royals, are scheduled to be inducted in the Missouri Hall of Fame.

# Doug Williams: Testing His Limits And Succeeding

To dream anything that you want to dream. That is the beauty of the human mind. To do anything that you want to do. That is the strength of the human will. To trust in yourself, to test your limits. That is the courage to succeed.

Those last two lines... ""To trust in yourself, to test your limits. That is the courage to succeed."" Could very well become the theme for Doug Williams, the Washington Red skin quarterback, as the starting Redskin field gen eral, Doug could become the first black quarterback to lead a pro team to the all-important Super Bowl. While we will see how the theme applies, let's also see how Doug has tested his limits—three times in his life.

First off, his days at Grambling State univer sity. There, Doug had a fruitful and rewarding col lege football career. His ""test"" rested in the fact that his older brother had played baseball there be fore him and ""little brother Doug"" was tested and un afraid, but not knowing quite why. Under the steady, guiding hand of Coach Eddie Robinson, starting in 1974, Doug went on to toss 93 touchdown passes and become a viable candidate for the Heisman Trophy four years later. Not bad for a tall, lanky kid from Zachary, La, (near Baton Rouge). Now, watch Doug go on to take a bigger test.

On college draft day that next April, Doug was se lected by the last place, lowly Tampa Bay Bucca neers. Two battles faced him—being a black NFL quarterback and playing for a cellar-doomed team. To the contrary, Tampa Bay went on to participate in three post season playoffs, all under the rifle like arm of Doug Williams.
After a contract dispute with Tampa Bay, Doug left in 1983 to join the now defunct United States Foot ball League.

When Doug had to look for someplace to play, the Washington Redskins signed him away from the ""needy and greedy"" Los Angeles Raiders. To date, he is a seven-year veteran in the National Football League and has passed for 84 touchdowns (almost equalling his Grambling output). In addition, he has had eleven 300 yard games and single-handedly resurrected the 1987 Red skins by taking over and pushing them into the playoffs. Testing his lim its—all the way! Go ahead, Doug! Now for the third test.

The final time Doug had to test his limits was also in 1983. This time it was not professional—it was personal. Unexpected and unplanned for his college sweetheart and wife, Janice, passed away from a shocking and surprising brain tumor. That sad event left Doug a widower, with a 3-month old daughter, Ashley. Of all the tests faced by Doug, this was the hardest and the most painful test to experience. With every pass of the football and every passing day he re-members her and contin- ually forces himself to an other and different test— another and different limit.

Obviously, we Monday Niters far too well know the Doug Williams story and we have known it for a long time. But, all this past week, after the Wash ington victory over Chi cago, sportscast and sport caster's have offered Doug's name—some in awe, but all in respect. They are finally realizing his contributions to and greatness in the NFL.

We, on the other hand, look at it a bit differently. In fact, Ike Gardner wond ers what Doug would have been like, if he had good receivers—to the likes of Miami or San Francisco. All of us knew that Doug was great, given half a chance. And. Joe Gibbs, the Washington Redskin coach, was an important play in Doug's hard-earned ""game of chance.""

Once again, this Sunday, Doug will trust in himself to test his limits. And, if his history continues to repeat itself, he will master the courage to succeed and lead his team to and through the Super Bowl, come January 31.

TITLE: Monday Nite Footballers Commentary: A Testimony of Courage
ENTITIES: Monday Nite Footballers, Monday Night Ladies, Isaac Gardner Jr., Janice Nunnelly Grun, Grun, Prospect

Usually this column discloses the views of ten men who rarely agree on anything concerning sports. The ladies of the Monday Night Footballers infrequently get the credit and recognition they fully deserve. The following episode is a testimony of the unquestioned courage of one of the Monday Night Ladies.

Late last Thursday afternoon, Mrs. Janice Nunnelly, known to the group as Grun, received a call from her bank. The caller indicated there was a lady at the bank trying to cash a check, claiming that she was Janice Nunnelly. Grun rushed to the bank, ""faster than a speeding bullet."" The imposter was being detained by bank officials, still maintaining that she was Janice Nunnelly after Grun brought forth a veritable plethora of identification, clearly establishing her identity. Meanwhile, the imposter feigned thirst, pretended to go to the water fountain, but then fled through the door. Grun, observing the get away, followed the imposter in hot pursuit.

The imposter made it to her car, but Grun, the Swift, was upon her as she started the car. As the car was pulling off, Grun reached through the door and grabbed the imposter. While the car was in motion, Grun, with the grace of a ballet dancer, wrapped her legs around the mirror of the car. The car sped down Prospect with Grun persistently holding on to the neck of the imposter. After a sojourn of seven blocks, Grun finally decided that discretion was the better part of valor and managed to disengage herself from the car and the imposter's neck. Grun escaped with minor scratches.
Grun, now more persistent than ever, searched the neighborhood on foot. With Grun's information, the police apprehended the imposter, who is presently incarcerated.

We constantly hear that members of our community do not want to get involved. The incident described in this article clearly belies that notion as a generality. There may be some who would question Grun's judgement in pursuing the imposter. However, no one would question her courage. The Monday Night Footballers are proud to have her as a member, and I feel honored to be in a privileged few who have her as a personal friend."

TITLE: Monday Nite Footballers Commentary: Lynette Woodard, The First Woman Globetrotter

ENTITIES: Lynette Woodard, Jim Nunnelly, Monday Nite Footballers, Globetrotters, Super Bowl Playoffs, Kansas City, Sweet Georgia Brown, Wood, Little Sister, Baby Girl, Sweet Lou Dunbar, Academic All American, Kansas University, Wilt Chamberlain, Jo Jo White, 1984 American Olympic team, Gold Medal, NAACP, Woman of the Year, Wichita, Kas., American Cancer Society, Big Brothers-Big Sister program, Charlotte, N.C., Kemper Arena
# Lynette Woodard, The First Woman Globetrotter

While it may seem strange that the Monday Nite Footballers would be writing about a basketball player right in the middle of the Super Bowl Playoffs, our interests in athletics and academics is well documented and Lynette Woodard exemplifies one of our key ideals, to assure that athletes also do well in school.

On January 11, the Globetrotters brought their 60-year-old act to Kansas City. Having performed in 10 countries before Kings, Presidents, Shahs and just everyday folks, there was something different this year.
There was Lynette Woodard, the first woman Globetrotter. But there is something real special about Lynette that interests Monday Nite Footballers.
But first, let's look at her career, her performance at Kemper and then we will look at what's ""special"" about her.

Everyone knows that Lynette Woodard was an All-American at the University of Kansas. There, she scored an amazing 3649 points, surpassing both Wilt Chamberlain and Jo Jo White. She was the captain of the 1984 American Olympic team that won the Gold Medal.

Apparently a diligent community worker, she was honored as NAACP **""Woman of the Year""** by her native city, Wichita, Kas. She has actively been involved in the American Cancer Society and the Big Brothers-Big Sister program. It was 1984 at the Charlotte, N.C. Globetrotter camp, that she achieved history. She was chosen to be the Globetrotters ""Sweet Georgia Brown."" Affectionately referred to as ""Wood,"" ""Little Sister"" and ""Baby Girl,"" what court contribution does Ms. Woodard make to the Globetrotters? In Kansas City before a near sellout crowd, she made four layups, blocked one shot, and performed a 6-minute, magic pass routine with teammate—Sweet Lou Dunbar. Not bad for the lightest, shortest, and prettiest Globetrotter. All good but there is a lot more to Lynette.

You see, Ms. Woodard is also a star athlete who has done well in the classroom. Of all her many honors and media attention, seldom is it mentioned that Lynette Woodard was an Academic All American at Kansas University. That means she maintained a B-plus average in college while being a starter in a major sport.

Every young lady and young man in the world should know this fact about Lynette. Unfortunately, most of us will only know that she played at Kemper Arena and she is the attractive star of the Globetrotter television commercial.

We Monday Nite Footballers, honor Lynette Woodard for she has demonstrated that you can do well both on the court and in the classroom. In an exclusive interview with us, she simply gave this advice to young, aspiring athletes. **""Get your education first, and everything else will fall into place.""**
It is something to think about."

3. The Chiefs defense, which has allowed just seven offensive touchdowns, has gotten outstanding performances so far this year from DT Dan Saleaumua, LB Derrick Thomas and CB James Hasty. Saleaumua has 28 tackles, 22 QB pressures, three sacks, two forced fumbles and one fumble recovery in five games. Thomas, now playing ""stack linebacker,"" is tied for third in the NFL with five sacks and is second on the team with 32 tackles. Hasty, who was named the AFC Defensive Player of the month for September, has had a hand in four of the Chiefs 11 takeaways.

4. COMBAT funds 31 drug prevention and 28 drug treatment agencies across Jackson County. If you or someone you know needs these services, please call the information line at 84-COMBAT or Jim Nunnelly at 881-3814.

5. The Chargers have allowed just 256.4 yards per game, second-fewest in the League, and have the NFL's top-rated pass defense ( 147.6 yards per game). That unit is led by LB Junior Seau, who has ventured to four consecutive Pro Bowls.
6. For the Chargers, punishing RBNatrone Means is the AFC's leading rusher with 504 yards and three touchdowns on 109 carries.

7. Martin Bayless, now with the Chiefs, was the Chargers' starting strong safety from '87-'91. During that time, Bayless collected 355 tackles, three interceptions, 7 1/2 sacks and five fumble recoveries in 73 games in 63 starts). He had a great game against Arizona.

# Kansas City Chiefs Vs. Pittsburgh Steelers

column5: Chiefs unprepared for the big time.

column1: 1. The head groundskeeper at Arrowhead Stadium is Kansas City native Andrew Bruce. Bruce, a Central High school graduate, was elevated to that position in March, 1990. The NFL Players Association voted Arrowhead Stadium's grass playing surface as one of the best in the League after its installation in 1994.

2. The last time the Chiefs played the Steelers was January 8, 1994, at Arrowhead Stadium, (AFC first round playoff game). It was the second playoff game in Arrowhead Stadium history. The Chiefs upended the Steelers 27-24 to set up a Divisional playoff battle against the Houston Oilers.

3. The defending AFC Champion Pittsburgh Steelers opened the season with a 24-9 loss at Jacksonville but have since rebounded. They have won three straight games over Baltimore, Buffalo and Houston. The core to the success of the Steelers is their defense which is allowing just 281.8 yards per game, fourth fewest in the NFL.
4. Sixteen different Chiefs have caught passes, including 10 with at least 5 catches. Wide Receiver Chris Penn leads the group with 13 receptions for 139 yards and two touchdowns.
5. Running back Marcus Allen is 13th in the AFC with 194 rushing yards and three touchdowns on 56 carries. Linebacker Derrick Thomas is second in the AFC and tied for third in the NFL with six sacks.

6. The Chiefs, up until the 1993 season, had Pittsburgh Steelers Head Coach Bill Cowher as the defensive coordinator. Coach Cowher was in Kansas City for two years (1989-91), under Coach Marty Schottenheimer. Both Derrick Thomas and Neil Smith played under Cowher.

7. COMBAT drug treatment providers are required to take classes on cultural diversity as part of their contractual responsibilities. These classes aid the counselors in meeting the treatment needs of Jackson County’s diverse population.

8. Kordell Stewart is listed as the number three quarterback behind starter Mike Tomczak (18) and backup Jim Miller (16). Stewart is a second-year player from Colorado. So far this season, he has 40 yards rushing and 135 yards receiving with one touchdown. His nickname is “SLASH” because he splits his time as a quarterback/wide receiver (QB/WR). He will be the star on Monday night.

column1: We'll all be home for New Year's. That's the cry for our Kansas City Chiefs, once thought to be the AFC's entry to the Super Bowl next month. Well, it didn't happen and the once 13-3 Chiefs are now 6-9, and may even go to 6-10, i.e. if Oakland overtakes the Chiefs Saturday.

What happened, you may ask, especially in the last half of the year. The only thing that did not happen negative to the Chiefs was there was no drug-related incidents. And, we who are associated with combating illegal substances are eternally grateful for that. But, there were many other football aspects to ponder and talk about. Here are a few:
*   First, the Chiefs wavered every week about who the starting quarterback would be. That took away from the stability every winning team must have.

*   Cornerback Mark McMillian, size notwithstanding, became every passers' target. His 5'7"", 145-pound stature invited opposing quarterbacks to pick on him. They did again and again. We never fixed this glaring gap.

*   Until Bam Morris, we never established a running back. Donnell Bennett was chosen and that was it. The last time you heard Kansas City mentioned as the home of a first-class running back was way back in 1967. Remember Mike Garrett. No running, no playoffs.

*   Kansas City literally set Derrick Thomas up for failure, by making him a ""falcon"" linebacker (a down lineman). Because of his small size, big 300-pound linemen just tossed him around. Speed is good but size is still a major factor in sports. Derrick made All-Pro as a linebacker, utilizing the quarterback. We ruined his effectiveness.

Then, too, there were other things off the field that distracted the Chiefs as well. I'm not sure how much all of this affected the play. I just know since the October 4 rainy win over Seattle, the Chiefs have spiraled downward and outward. Whatever the reasons, we all will be watching and pulling for Minnesota, Denver, Miami, San Francisco and Atlanta. The Chiefs will take their place as one of the 18 NFL teams whose season will end this week.
For the rest, the second season starts in January."

# Los Angeles Raiders vs. Kansas City Chiefs

1. The Chiefs have gained more than 300 total yards in just two of the last nine games against the Raiders, while Los Angeles has not registered more than 280 yards in the past five games vs. Kansas City.

2. Art Shell was named the ninth head coach in Raiders history on Oct. 3, 1989. The 47-year-old Shell had been the Raiders offensive line coach for seven years. He first joined the staff in '83 after 15 seasons as one of the finest offensive tackles in NFL history. An eight time Pro Bowl selection, he was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame on Aug. 5 of '89. His regular career record stands at 49-38 (.883).
3. On Oct. 3, 1993 at Arrowhead (Game 4), the Chiefs opened up a 21-3 half-time lead on the Raiders, and never looked back in a 24-9 victory.

4. The midway point of the season finds the Chiefs with a 5-3 record for the third time in five years ('90 and '91). The Chiefs opened the season with three straight victories, but have lost three of their last five games.

5. The Los Angeles Raiders have been victorious in three of their last four outings, beating New England (21-17); Atlanta (30-17) and Houston (17-14) while losing in overtime to Miami (20-17). The team's offensive leader is WR Tim Brown who has caught 44 passes for 658 yards with six TDs.

column1: Obviously, this game is it.! The Chiefs have come to the fork in the road...either they win or we will all turn our attention and support to Tony Dungy (Indianapolis) and Marvin Lewis (Cincinnati). But if Detroit should happen to beat Pittsburg and Denver wipes out San Diego and the Chiefs beat up on Cincinnati.... then the Chiefs will make the playoffs. Folks, it is that simple. And complicating matters is the hovering speculation that Vermeil will retire ... one way or the other. Clearly, Vermeil is at the fork in the road, too. Win and stay in! Lose and sign up for Medicare! Folks, it is that simple.

And we, Monday Niters, are facing another kind fork in the road. Monday Night Football, as we know it, is over. ABC will no longer broadcast the perennial ratings-winner. In fact, we all said our goodbyes to Al Michaels and John Madden and remi-nisced about all the history-making plays witnessed on Monday night television, everywhere, In 1970, we were all younger, quicker, and immature. Now we are older, slower and fall asleep with out warning. It takes us longer to do EVERY-THING; including selecting our teams. Some of us even have to ask ""who's play-ing"". The fork in the road for us is that we are just getting old and things are chang-ing, with or without our support. But some things will never change....Ike, Ollie and that Booker will always be haters of the Chiefs. Cleaver, Carl, and Ed West will go with the wind. And faithful, fine people like Lloyd Greenfield and imminent Dr. Allen, will always support the Chiefs, just like on New Year's Day against North Division champions, Cincinnati. We know the Chiefs will win by at least ten and Vermeil stays in. We never have been at that fork in the road!

Like each of you, we will have to switch the channel over to ESPN and carry on as usual. Booker, Ollie and Ike will continue to hate the Chiefs. And Ed, Carl and Cleaver will switch up, every now and then. And Dr. Charles Allen, Lloyd Green-field and I will always support the Chiefs. Wisely, we don't have to face a fork in the road. We know the way, just like we have always done. And just for the record, we will be looking for the Chiefs to win on New Year's Day, out at Arrowhead. Chiefs by 10 and Vermeil will be back, again!"

Let's take a glimpse of what's been happening over the past two weeks in sports. Everybody knows that Michael Jordan is back in the NBA. Wonder how much they are really paying ""Medicare Michael"" to put his 38-year-old body back out there on the hardwood. And we all have been crying good tears for the Mighty Yankees were shut down. And by the unexpected, youthful Arizona Diamondbacks. And Arizona did it to the Yankees just like the Yankees did it to them . . . in the bottom of the ninth inning. And what about those University of Missouri Tigers? Kareem Rush is on the front cover of Street and Smith magazine. And the national coaches' poll ranks the Tigers eighth, behind fifth-ranked Kansas. And there is only one senior on this year's squad . . . that's Clarence Gilbert. He and Rush will team up with Paulding, Johnson, Gage, and some new freshmen. Perhaps this just might be the year that Mizzou goes forth.

Speaking of going forth, how about those Chiefs, last Sunday, against the San Diego Chargers! Ike and his football Taliban predicted that San Diego would just beat our Chiefs to a pulp. Didn't happen. And, while the Chiefs almost gave the game away, Kansas City even held on in the waning moments, to preserve the victory. It was not like in weeks past, when ol' Spent Trent would throw an interception. Or there wouldn't be enough time outs. Or the special teams would allow a big touchdown to turn the game around. It was as if the Chiefs were managing the passing and running game, effectively.

That word, running. You will remember that Priest Holmes has been showing out lately. In fact, against San Diego's high-ranked run defense, he skipped, dipped, and ripped through Junior Seau and his friends as if he had called ""psychic hotline"" on them. Scampering 181 rushing yards, Priestly Priest has helped us restore faith in the running game. ""Trent Bono"" can't pass. But Priest Holmes can run. That is the same thing Marty Schottenheimer has been telling us. I can see ol' Marty standing over there on the sidelines, calling repeatedly for the run. Maybe his ""ghost"" was still there.

And that's just what apparition means. It means a ghost or an unexpected appear-ance. With Marty now winning three straight at Washington, we should be glad to have ol' Marty around . . . even if it's just his apparition. He apparently know what's best. A running game in hand is worth a St. Louis-style passing game in the bush.

column1: **John Elway: Take A Bow On Super Bowl Sunday**

John Elway is after all used to being on center stage and at the center of attention. That's understandable. He was the first athlete picked in the 1983 draft. He passed up a chance to play baseball for the Yankees. For starters he's brought Denver back to 40 some victories after being behind in the fourth quarter. All that and he's never won a Super Bowl and time is running out on the 15-year All-Pro quarterback.
Before we review Elway's chances for victory in Sunday's Super Bowl game against Green Bay, let's look back and see how ""other"" quarterbacks have upstaged Elway in past Super Bowl games.

First off, there was Phil Simms at Super Bowl XXI... when the New York Giants quarterback completed 22 of 25 passes that afternoon in Pasadena. Then in Super Bowl XXIV in 1990, Joe Montana passed five touchdowns, helping the 49ers to a 55-10 trouncing of the Broncos. There's more.

But, it was the 1988 Super Bowl that ""Big John"" will vividly remember. That was ""Doug Day afternoon."" Quarterback Doug Williams will put on a record setting passing clinic for Elway and African Americans everywhere. Being the first black quarterback to start a Super Bowl, Williams threw four touchdown passes in the second quarter, one for 80 yards to Ricky Sanders. And when it was all over, the Washington Redskins had dwarfed Elway and the Broncos 42-10. Grambling's favorite son and now coach was selected the game's Most Valuable Player, again upstaging one-way Elway. One more time.

But, like many of his comeback victories, Elway has another chance this Sunday. In fact, Ollie Hubbard, Paul Booker, and hate nable Harry Sypert believe it's Denver's time this year. Some others are just loyal to the American Football Conference, who hasn't produced a winning Super Bowl since Marcus Allen starred for the Los Angeles Raiders, way back in Super Bowl XVIII.

Will John Elway once again be the ""other"" quarterback, taking a back seat to Green Bay's Brett Favre? Carl Clark puts it best when he says Elway has a ""cast of supporting characters"" and that makes Elway a star and a winner this time.

In years past, he didn't have a Shannon Sharpe, or a running back like Terrell Davis, who on any given day can equal Barry Sanders. And, most of all, he didn't have a young, big-play, wide receiver like Missouri Southerner's Rod Smith. Mix that in with defensive giant Neil Smith and Keith Traylor and you will see clearly why John Elway will get the last bow on Sunday. San Diego will not be able to hold all the hoopla when the Broncos package the Packers. And Dear John will be on the shoulders of his team mates. The TV cameras will be all in his face. He would have done it, won the ""big one."" And he'll take a bow, right out in the middle of field, where 134 million people will clap and stand up.
Take a bow, John. This Bud's for you.

* Doug Williams, record-setting Most Valuable Player of Super Bowl XXII, will be part of the opening, coin-toss ceremonies. Williams was the first African American quarterback to start a Super Bowl. Now head coach at Grambling where he played college football, Williams will share pre-game honors with his old coach of the Washington Redskins, Joe Gibbs.
* 134 million people will watch Sunday's Denver-Green Bay Super Bowl. The game will start at 5:12 p.m. and can be seen on Channel 41 (NBC network).
* Lots of creative commercials will be launched on Sunday. Advertisers are paying 1.3 million dollars for each 30 second spot.
* Ralph Brown, owner and operator of the Sportsmen's House of Coiffures, will attend the Super Bowl in San Diego this Sunday. He has been to every Super Bowl but one since the Chiefs in 1970."

TITLE: Monday Nite Footballers Predictions: Super Bowl XXXIV

ENTITIES: Super Bowl XXXIV, Tennessee Titans, St. Louis Rams, Georgia Dome, KMBC-TV, Derrick Thomas, Tony Dungy, Tampa Bay Bucs, Kurt Warner, Isaac Bruce, Marshall Faulk, Houston Oilers, Steve McNair, Paul Booker, Doug Williams, John Elway, Charles Allen, Carl Clark, Ike Gardner, Lloyd Greenfield, Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly, Ed West
Georgia Dome In Atlanta, 5:18 P.M., Sunday

Being Super Bowl week, these last few days surely have been eventful. Not only did we get some sad news about Derrick Thomas, we have not fully recovered from the last minute, last quarter incomplete pass call against our beloved Tampa Bay Bucs. That was in the championship game at St. Louis, and we are left wondering why there was even an instant replay review in the first place. That one call erased Tony Dungy's chances of taking his team to the Super Bowl this Sunday in Atlanta. Really, it should be Tampa Bay vs. Tennessee rather than St. Louis vs. Tennessee.

Any way, let's look at the lucky St. Louis Rams. For us in Kansas City, this will be as close to a hometown as we will get. However, because of reasons we'll explain later, most of us will not be pulling for the Rams. By now, we all know just how talent-loaded this ""arch-city"" team really is. Even if one forgets about Kurt Warner and his 40-plus touchdown passes this season, you still have to contend with wide receiver Isaac Bruce. As if that's not enough, one has to also consider the venerable Marshall Faulk, formerly of the Indianapolis Colts. Now, there's a difference maker...solid runner, great pass receiver and steady four quarters. While the Rams are generally known for their overpowering offense, they are just as respectable on defense. Thus, they will be task-ready for the Tennessee Titans. Let's now turn to the team, formerly known as the Houston Oilers.

Certainly not as powerful on offense as St. Louis, the Titans do have a franchise player named Steve “Air” McNair. A rifle-armed passer, he was good at Alcorn A.M.&N, and he's been great in the National Football League. Paul Booker is betting on him to win the game, just like ol' Doug Williams did back in the eighties Super Bowl. On that day, MVP Doug Williams did everything for Washington. He completed so many passes that the records still stand. That includes John Elway's last stand two years ago. Thus, Doug and Steve McNair are the reasons why most African Americans nationwide will be rooting for Tennessee on Sunday. It will be about ethnic pride that comes with playing in a championship game, with the eyes of the entire world watching you perform.

For us, Tennessee is the best team that has worked the hardest and overcome the most adversity to make it to the top spot. Not just the politics of playing an African American quarterback, but in moving a struggling franchise in a big city (Houston) to a thriving powerhouse in a small city (Nashville). And taking a young team and wielding it into a contender in a very short time. You see, all of this mirrors the migratory struggles that so many of us have faced and thus, we can identify largely with Tennessee.
So, at 5 p.m. Sunday in Atlanta, look for the Titans to be on top all the way.

column1: ENTITIES: Pittsburgh Steelers, Kansas City Chiefs, Jim Nunnelly, Ike Gardner, Dick Vermeil, Robert Lee Allen, Washington Redskins, Denver, Trent Green, Priest Holmes, Tony Richardson, Jerome Bettis, Charles Allen, Paul Booker, Carl Clark, Lloyd Greenfield, Ollie Hubbard, Ed West, Robert Allen
*""I've read in-depth a good book where man walked on water. But, his name was not Dick Vermeil.""*

You all don't know Robert Lee Allen. He's 7 years old and he attends elementary school out in Martin City. Young, gifted and black, young Allen sums it up quite adequately when he says, ""The Chiefs are good. . . sometimes."" From the mouth of babes flows the truth.

Fresh from a surprising victory over the lowly, winless Washington Redskins the week before, our Chiefs fell hard to Denver in their new stadium. Denver ran through our run defense as if they had stolen the Chiefs' playbook. And Trent Green threw well, except, he threw it four times to Denver's record-setting cornerback. And, Priest Holmes, was lost without the blocking of Tony Richardson. All in all, the Chiefs made young Allen sound awfully good because their overall performance against Denver, was at best, ""sometimey."" What adjustments need to be made against the Pittsburgh Steelers?

Well, first off, the run defense needs to do double time. That's because Jerome Bettis, fresh off of attaining 10,000 yards, will test them to the max. Secondly, Trent and his receivers must spend time, just throwing and catching the ball. There is a severe loss of fundamentals in the passing game. And, it has to be fixed before the Chiefs can win another game. Thirdly, the special teams smell. Like the offense, there is something missing. . . blocking, running, just plain aggressiveness. And all of this must be in place before Sunday. That is, if we are to become 2-3.

A record of 2-3 or 1-4, there is not much difference, especially if we don't fix the aforementioned problems. They won't go away. And young Allen will be watching and wondering if Sunday will be the day when the Chiefs get on the win side of ""sometimey.""
We ought to pay attention to this 7-year-old. So should Dick Vermeil.

This Monday night, we, the Monday Nite Football group, will gather at Ed West's house to be in our 32nd year as the city's only official Chiefs' Fan Club. Same guys watching football and conversing for more than three decades. As always, there will be food galore, prepared for members of the larges repository of football history, anywhere in Kansas City. Ike, Carl, Ed and Charles, have had season tickets ever since the early Chiefs were over at the 22nd and Brooklyn site. All will be happier because of the reunion, and as has been the group's policy, no one will leave mad. . . just a bit more enlightened and a lot more prepared. . . to talk anytime, anywhere about their favorite subject. . . the Kansas City Chiefs. Welcome back to our world.

column1: ENTITIES: Seattle Seahawks, Kansas City Chiefs, Robert Lee Allen, Tony Gonzales, Priest Holmes, Trent Vermeil, Charles Allen, Beverly Allen, Martin City, Paul Booker, Carl Clark, Ike Gardner, Lloyd Greenfield, Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly, Ed West

I’ve been talking to Robert Lee Allen. You will remember him because he is the very bright, 7-year-old grandson of Charles and Beverly Allen. And, occasionally, he attends our Monday Nite football meetings. Robert seems to think the Chiefs are really a good team, even though they have not been winning. At first, this seems like a paradox. . . good, but not winning. Let’s explore this wisdom, coming from this talented, Martin City student.

First, it is true that Chiefs have some talented players. They don’t come any better than Tony Gonzales. Robert agrees. And Priest Holmes is showing signs of becoming the next Barry Sanders. Robert agrees with that, too. And, no one is quite sure why Vermeil keeps talking about the mediocre play of the receivers. For the most part, they are ok . . . at least most of the time. So, what’s wrong? Why aren’t these Chiefs winning?

It’s really very simple. It’s not the defense, even though they are not playing optimal-ly. Not the special teams, even though they played only one good game (Washington). Well, what is it? It is simply Vermeil’s refusal to admit that “Bent-Trent” IS the prob-lem. The man leads the NFL in interceptions; has lost four games in the final minutes; and will invariably hold on to the football as if he is trying to get sacked. God only knows why Vermeil holds on to what Ike calls the team’s “weakest link.” But, that’s why Robert Lee Allen is saying that the Chiefs are a good team . . . without Trent. Will we win?

Definitely. Those Chiefs are going to win Sunday. Not because Trent will play bet-ter. And, like another youngster told me . . . you can’t sell tickets to a circus that’s got sick elephants. Number 10 is one of ‘em. From the mouth of babes!!!

TITLE: Monday Nite Footballers Predictions: Super Bowl XXXVII

ENTITIES: Jim Nunnelly, Oakland Raiders, Tampa Bay Buccaneers, Ike Gardner, Paul Booker, Charles Allen, Carl Clark, Lloyd Greenfield, Ollie Hubbard, Ed West, Bubba Cleaver, Jon Gray, Donna Stewart, Tracy Allen, Rich Gannon, Jon Gruden, Warren Sapp, Keyshawn Johnson, Jerry Rice, Woodson, Romanowski
Good defense overrides good offense; But my ‘gut’ level pushes me toward Oakland
Raiders will serve up plenty of Brown/Rice
Tampa will disintegrate; Oakland will prevail
Respect your elders (Gannon, Rice, Brown, Woodson, Romanowski)

There are two important similarities to the two teams in the 2003 Super Bowl. First, they both proudly wave Pirates' flags, one calling themselves the Buccaneers and the other known respectfully as the Raiders. Then, too, each share the influence of a common head coach, Jon Gruden. There, the similarities cease. On the other hand, the Raiders sport the NFL’s Most Valuable Player in quarterback, Rich Gannon. On the other hand, the Bucs have Warren Sapp, arguably the best defensive lineman to ever play the game in recent times. The Raiders will place their receiving hopes in their Medicare acquisition, Jerry Rice. And the Bucs will put their trust in Deion-like, Keyshawn Johnson. All of this makes for a competitive and entertaining Super Bowl, assuring that each team has a reasonable chance to win. Of course, most Monday Niters believe in Oakland. That's because they are 'Raiders worshipers,' never mind go, of the fact that the Chiefs let this rugged individualist star get away. Plus, they feel some faulty pride in that the AFC West produced the most competitive division in football. Nevertheless, the Bucs have a coaching genius in Jon Gruden, and it is my belief that this ""coaching factor"" is a real key. While some may rightfully argue that the coach will not wear a uniform, leaving player performance as the most important outcome factor on Sunday. But, any respectable football fan, knows that in close games, the coaching effect reigns supreme. Things like clock management, blitz-timing, and playcalling are all decisions directly influenced by the head coaches. So, given that this will be one of the closest Super Bowl games, Jon Gruden becomes the ""difference maker"" out in San Diego, giving the Bucs the vital edge and their first championship ever."

column1: Stewart Watson (guest) San Diego by 14 Chiefs can't stop a snail

In order to obtain relief from pressure, individuals and organizations sometimes do real badly at first. Then, when they improve slightly, everyone thinks of the improvement and forgets the horrible start. Sound familiar? I never thought of Herm Edwards would dip that low.

Whatever the rationale, Of Herm finds himself trying to explain why the Chiefs are off to another bad start. I can tell you that Chiefs fans are not going to take another ""dumbing-down"" ruse (trickery).

Face it, y'all. The Chiefs looked like a ""rag-tag"" team out there against Pittsburgh. From start to finish and all points in between, the Chiefs just played to the lowest level ever. The corners didn't cover. The defensive line didn't tackle. The running backs didn't run. And the offensive line didn't block. The only positive thing that emerged last Sunday was the punter. That boy, ""Croquette"" boomed that ball 70-plus yards. The only bright spot on an otherwise horrible display of Kansas City football. But let's not get sentimental.

Without regard to Herm’s motivational explanation, it still boils down to the Chiefs not playing. Period. Now, he can play that ""dumbing-down"" card, if he wants. But we old fans know that game. The message is simple: Win against San Diego Sunday or get out of the way!""
Personally, we loyalists are pulling for them. Don't tell the haters."
