## Perspective On The Hall Of Fame
For almost a decade I have been trying to mo tivate the Monday Night Footballers to take a trip to Canton, Ohio and par ticipate in the Hall of Fame activities.

This past week, a few of us decided to make the sojourn and found it to be 'quite enlightening.' We were particularly proud that one of our own Kansas City Chiefs, Len Dawson, was inducted into the Football Hall of Fame. It seems that the local television media shared our pride. However, I am equally, if not more, proud to see two other Hall of Famers sharing the stage with Len Dawson. They were two of the premier linebackers of all time, Willie Lanier and Bobby Bell. It should be pointed out that both Willie Lanier and Bobby Bell had shorter playing careers than Len Dawson, but both made the Hall of Fame before 'Cool Len ny.'

This does not detract from the achievements of Dawson, but it clearly sig nifies the stellar perfor mances of two super ath letes. I say all of the above to pose the following query. Why didn't the local tele vision media give the two super black athletes the same coverage they gave a good white athlete?
Since the answer is ob vious, I'll waste no more time dealing with the mo tives of our 'fair-minded' television executives.
The football game itself was at best lackluster.
If the quality of play against the Forty-Niners was an omen of things to come, we are in for a very long season.

The most disappointing aspect of the game was that most of the points were given up by the specialty teams. Last year, our spe cialty team frequently made the difference be tween victory and defeat. In that, our head coach got his recognition on the performance of our spe cialty teams, one would think that our specialty teams would be ready even if the offense and defense were not ready.
People are already ask ing if the appointment of Ganz is a classical example of the ""Peter Principle.""
If there was a bright spot in the game, it has to be Christian Okoye. Most of his yardage was gained against rookies, so 1 still maintain my position of wait and see on the young man from Nigeria.
The trip was informative, entertaining, and very worthwhile.
Chiefs may have a decent team this year.

The greatest thing in this world is not where he is but in what direction he is heading. The ""he"" is Christian Okoye and he's heading for the Arrowhead Stadium. On Tuesday, the Chiefs selected him as their second-round draft choice. Let's look this newcomer over—his career, his life, and his strengths and weaknesses, and his direction.

First, his college career. By now, everyone knows that he was an All-American from a little-known college called Azusa Pacific. And unless one is completely blind, one can tell he's big, weighing a giant-size 253 pounds and standing 6'1"". On top of all that, he's fast, clocked at 4.4 in the 40 yard dash. Go on. Go on.

And just think he didn't even play football, prior to three years ago. That's because he came to the United States on a track and field scholarship. His specialty—a worldclass discus thrower. From Nigeria. The remainder of his family, a father and three sisters, are still in the homeland.

Now, to the football field. Even though he has never played a down on artificial turf, he scored 21 touchdowns on natural grass. But, he doesn't consider that a big problem—at least, not an unsolvable problem. And in his final two years, he gained over 3,000 yards rushing.

And he's really serious about doing well, owing to his older-than-usual twenty five years. So what makes Ike Gardner (fresh from the marriage altar) say, ""let's wait and see"" when asked about Christian Okoye?
Is it what the critics say?

They say he fumbles a lot. And they go on to caution fans that he runs ""straight up,"" thereby making Okoye unreliable and injury-prone. Okoye answers his critics by simply chalking it up to his first years of football. However, he quickly points out that that was a ""yesterday"" problem and all is well, now.
And from the way Kansas City fans have been singing his praises and raising their 1987-season hopes, they believe that he has corrected those rookie jitters, too.

Jerry Price is one of those. He's convinced that Okoye is the ""sleeper"" of the entire NFL draft. Jerry expects Okoye to rise to the status of a Gale Sayers or a Mike Garrett, both former Chiefs' draft choices. In fact, most are thinking that he will be better than first-round pick, Paul Palmer of Temple. *So what's the bottom line.*
The bottom line is this. Okoye is okay.

He has size, speed, a proven career, intelligence, and modesty. And we, Monday Nites, like the fact that he doesn't murder the English language, when interviewed. After all, he's one of the few athletes that will have earned a college degree—before entering pro football. What else can we say, except that we wish him the best that Kansas City can offer. And, like Avis, he'll try harder. And that trait alone makes him all right with us. As we said earlier, he's heading in the right direction. Or again, Okoye is okay."

# Tracy Cherry: Moving Up and Giving Back...Uniquely

Moving up and giving back."" That was the theme for last week's Women's Day program at St. James Methodist church. More personally, the theme applies neatly to a brilliant, unusual, athletic, and unique lady, Tracy Cherry. Let's see how.

First of all, Tracy was a scholarly athlete at Ursinus college, a small private Jesuit school, just outside Philadelphia. There she earned a 3.5 grade point average, majoring in Physical Education, with a minor in Business and Psychology. As if that was not enough, she was awarded All-America honors in field hockey. Now, how's that for being unique, a black female All America on the Dean's List. Moving up. Let's go on.

After graduation from this well respected institution, she was flown to Kansas City—to interview for and accept a teaching position at a local school, Barstow. There she became the first (and only) black faculty member in the entire history of the well-known school. Besides teaching Physical Education, she was appointed Coach of the Girls' field hockey team. Under her tutelage, the team overtook archrival Pembroke, in September 1982. Moving up, farther. What about this field hockey?

Simply put, field hockey is her ""pride and joy."" But her touchstone and strength is centered around her family. Being the only girl (again unique) with a cast of four brothers has entitled Tracy to a special position with her father. It was with his encouragement and guidance that she was able to select her college early, in the seventh grade. He wanted her in the best school, academically and athletically. Ursinus offered both. And her love for field hockey was sustained. Thanks, Dad for helping me move up.

But, when she talks about her mother, a heavy and deep-seated, emotional high settles on her face. That's because she's really close to her mother. Not only because of her ""only girl"" status, but because she respects her mother's undying devotion to her—and her four brothers.

Her mother made sure that all of them went to college, in spite of the fact that her parents were divorced when Tracy was twelve. Her mother helped Tracy ""move up."" Let's get back to Tracy's real love.

And, again she smiles widely and sincerely when she resumes talking about Field Hockey. She emphasizes that the sport is unique and requires a set of unique characteristics...strong legs, rangy wrist work, and the finesse and grace of an Olympic figure skater. And Tracy possesses and wears these attributes—in a pleasant and charming way. Enough of moving up, let's see what and how she gives back.

Not only does she sing in the choir at Mount Vernon Baptist church, she also teaches aerobics at the Linwood YMCA. And besides attending all her brother's football games (Deron Cherry of the Kansas City Chiefs), she finds enough time to talk to her mother every day, long distance from Palmyra, New Jersey. Talk about giving back. And the list could go on.

Finally, anyone can see that here is somebody who knows how to be somebody. Of all her honors, on and off the field, she believes deeply, in moving up and giving back—at the same time. Only a few people are ever treated to a set of unique experiences, academically and athletically. But, Tracy is a unique person, and she wears it well."

# Ed West: A Lesson In Little Extras

A native of Osawatomie, Kas. Graduate from Pittsburg State. Married, three children. Taught basic electricity, Lincoln high school in the early '60s. Likes to fish in the Kansas reservoirs and the Canadian clear lakes. Enjoys tinkering with all kinds of electronic devises. An avid sports enthusiast. All of these phrases describe Ed; but none better than the last one—an avid sports enthusiast. Let's see how.

First, Ed clearly remembers two championship games in his lifetime — one in high school and the other in college. One in which his team lost, the other in which his team won. And, he put together a valuable lesson and philosophy from both.
Like many athletes, Ed remembers vividly the day his Osawatomie high school team lost to Rosedale of Kansas City, Kas. That was

way back in 1957. And his memory is still impeccable. And a sound of disappointed excitement still permeates his voice. That day Ed, his four other black teammates, and the rest of his team went that “little extra.”

And Ed still talks about it with a yesterday's freshness. Rosedale, a big school, beat Osawatomie, a little school. By one point. One lousy point, but, a big point. And Osawatomie had beat other big schools. But, that day David could not slay Goliath. From that, Ed learned that even you can learn from a loss. Especially, when you give your all and all; and even with that little extra, you can still lose. But, a real champion never gives up.
And he didn't give up. Two years later, as a sophomore corner back at Pittsburg State, he found himself in the first NAIA
championship game—Pittsburg State of Kansas verses Hilldale of Michigan. That day he was a star, especially on defense.

He intercepted two passes and returned a punt 60 yards, setting up a key touchdown. Pittsburg was proud that day, winning 26-21. And Ed was too. He and his team gave a little extra. And you can hear him smile about that game any time he talks about it. It was like a lesson. For extra credit in life.

Speaking of life, Ed holds a creative and steady interest in his work—as production supervisor at AT&T. Besides being athletic, he’s alert, ambitious, attentive and articulate. He has to be right; because his department produces 30,000 electronic circuit breakers daily. And every single day, he experiences the agonies of defeat and the thrills of victory, just

But, he gives that “little extra.” For he knows that his company and his superiors are concerned that foreign competitors will force them out of the marketplace. And the American companies must ban together. He’s proud to be a part of this happening. You can detect that challenge, deeply rooted in his smile. Just like in those championship games.

And, that little extra emerges, again. It’s evident that he’s learned well from his early athletic experiences. And, maybe that’s what sports teaches us, that the difference between ordinary and extraordinary is that “little extra.” And, that you must apply that “little extra” to everything you do, especially your chosen field.

ENTITIES: Jim Nunnelly, David Robinson, Tyrone Bogues, Reggie Williams, Paul Booker, Ike Gardner, Spud Webb, Ed West, Magic Johnson, San Antonio, Philadelphia, New York, Portland, L.A. Clippers, Atlanta, Washington Bullets, NBA, World Championship

San Antonio, Philadel- phia, New York, Portland, L.A. Clippers, and Atlanta had something in common on draft day—Monday June 22. They all wisely drafted seven footers in the 1987 talent-rich, NBA draft. Even the first team to pick, San Antonio, drafted David Robinson, a seven-foot, three incher out of the Na- vy.

But, one team used their first round draft pick to take a 5 foot 3 inch guard from Wake Forest. Yes — not seven foot, not six foot, by a five foot, three inch midget. What would compel the Washington Bullets to waste a precious, first round choice to pick a very short basketball player? Before we comment, let’s look in on the player and see if we can find some reason for this madness.

First of all, the honoree is a “little ant” named “Bugsy” (real name Ty- rone Bogues). Besides be- ing the shortest ever to be drafted into the giant- dominated NBA, he was once the teammate of Reg- gie Williams, 6'7"" first round choice for the Los Angeles Clippers.
Okay, that was at the Dunbar high school in Bal- timore.

And recently, his claim to fame was his outstanding showmanship at the World Championship games in Spain. There he dazzled the international fans with his fancy footwork and pre- cision-like, bullet passing.
Okay, so what do the Mon- day Niters think?

Well, the guys all have expressed “guarded” res- ervation about this “gutsy” move on the part of the Bullets. Both Paul Booker and Ike Gardner think that it's just a publicity stunt and no more. Already, Washington has the tallest player at seven foot eight. And, with “Bugsy” Bogues, they would have the tallest and the shortest players.

Ike further quips, that to be successful, Washing- ton will have to play Atlanta every week, suggestng that the only player that Tyrone can adequately guard is the Hawks Spud Webb (he’s 5'7""). Ed West says that he'll have to be able to jump through the moon. And, who knows, maybe he can. However, publicity or jumping ability do not win games.

To win an appropriate share of one hundred and sixty two games, a team should choose the best play- ers possible. Thus, every game and everybody counts. Can you imagine little Bugsy trying to guard 6'9"" Magic Johnson? How silly, point guard or not, it's just plain foolish. And, it tends to make a mockery out of this serious game of professional basketball.

But, even more seriously, we advise Washington to use choice, not chances, to determine their destiny. Put the best, most-likely- to-succeed players on the floor. Competition among the pro teams is fierce."

column2: Everything depends on what we are capable of wanting. Whoever said that must have been thinking of the young general manager of the Indianapolis Colts. Before we demon- strate how that statement applies, let's take a look at this year's edition of the Colts.
column3: $10,000, for the weight room. SMU never had any- thing like that.

column2: Last year, the Colts won just three games and lost 13. The team is now 4-6 and in first place in the NFC East. We have to ex- plain just what motivates a team to become the NFL's turnaround dream team? The easiest answer is coach- ing.
column3: Speaking of SMU, Eric Dickerson, the Colts's la- test acquisition, is also an All-American from that school. Eric is considered by many to be the game's best running back. And, we all know the contribu- tion he has made to football, and more recently to the playoff-bound Colts. Ev- erytime he runs for a first down with that tornado- like glide, the Indiana fans are reminded that the state is now famous for more than its college basketball team, the Indiana Hoosiers.

column2: That's because it's no secret that the Colts were right down there with Jim. on Bay. Surely, they needed something like a coach, for they lost their first thir- teen games last year. And then Ron Meyer was ap- pointed head coach and the 1986 Colts won their last three games. Meyer is a product of the Texas- based school, Southern Methodist University.

For the record, SMU is the alma mater of Jim Ir- say, the Colt's general man- ager. Young Irsay was a weightlifter there. Though there is no obvious reason, the SMU name keeps popping up. Is it that a school like SMU has some- thing else that sparks this upward lift?
column3: Though it may be easy to conclude that there is something magic about SMU. No. No. But, the mag- ic does lie in the courage, competence, and confi- dence of the Colts's 28-year- old general manager. He hired Meyer from a school that had been under in- vestigation. And he went on to acquire Eric Dick- erson, who was full of prob- lems and demanding more pay. The young general manager went for it. He

column2: Paul Booker says it's money. Paul believes the owner, Robert Irsay, will spend millions to produce a winner at Indianapolis. And Paul, in his unassum- ing way, may be right. For the elder Irsay is willing to give an player $50,000 for a year-round weight- lifting program. And, he permitted his weightlifting son to purchase a stereo sound system costing a cool
column3: doubled Eric's pay and the payoff is in the making. Big Jim Irsay continues to press on—not only in lifting weights but in lifting the horizons of a team and a city. He wanted ""an

column1: edge."" And the two SMU thoroughbreds, Meyer and Dickerson, gave him that edge. And the record shows that he's still getting it.
Again, everything de- pends on what we are ca- pable of wanting. Hats off to Jim Irsay."

2. Since 1989, the federal government has mandated that any agency receiving federal money must certify that the agency will provide a drug free workplace. Not only must a written policy be in place, but the agency must inform employees about where to go for any drug or alcohol treatment. Drinking and drugging on the job is a major problem in the workforce. For a copy of the law or for information about avallable treatment services, contact Jim Nunnelly at 474-4920.

Why is it that so few women enter treatment for substance abuse? Statistics show that women enter treatment less than men. Mainly because there are very few treatment facilities available for children that will house a mother and her children while treatment is underway. The other problems is that usually these women are single heads of households with no outside assistance, and to go into treatment would probably cause a financial hardship to the family."

TITLE: Monday Nite Footballers Commentary: Who Is Better: Bird or Magic?
ENTITIES: Larry Bird, Magic Johnson, Jerry Price, Ed West, Carl Clark, Boston Celtics, Los Angeles Lakers, NBA, Indiana State, Michigan State
# Who Is Better: Bird or Magic?

Basketball fans every- where have compared “the Bird” with “the Magic.” And, if you listen to the daily Superfan show on radio, you’ll hear it repeatedly. Bird is better. Magic Johnson is better. And on and on. And the fact that their respective teams are again competing for the championship, just fuels the “war” even further.

Well, we Monday Niters usually don’t talk about basketball—it’s off season for us. But, because everyone else is rendering opinions so freely, it does deserve our commentary. We’d like to talk mainly about salary and floor ability. Then, after that we’ll add some insight to this controversy.

L a r r y Bird makes $1,800,000 annually, on a seven year contract, from the Boston Celtics. His counterpart, Magic Johnson enjoys a lucrative pay- check from Los Angeles, making a cool million each year, for twenty-five years, regardless of how long he can play. So, Bird makes more sooner and Magic makes less longer. Let’s move onto the court.

On the radio show, whites generally pick Bird and the blacks pick Magic. And, it’s been that way since they met in the now-resurrected Indiana State-Michigan State game of some years ago. Now, let’s ask the fellows what they think.

Ed West quickly points out that it’s a “tough comparison.” He says Magic is better, mainly because of his all around ability. He adds that Magic can win tough games. And fair-minded Carl Clark agrees. But, Carl thinks that the Bird is just as important, and plays just as well. Further, both players take control of the game—Bird from forward position and Magic from the guard. Even, Carl says. And what about scoring?

Jerry Price is kind of like Carl Clark, when he addresses the shooting issue. “Bird is a better perimeter (outside) shooter and Magic is the best inside shooter.” Obviously the two have demonstrated countless times how true this statement is. And all of us know that Bird won the Three-Point Competition outright. Decided yet? Let’s wrap up.

Tough comparison. It certainly is. But, it appears Magic gets the nod. Not only for his personal contributions, but for his “knack” for making his fellow Lakers rise to greater heights of excellence. He’s smart, courtwise, and he knows where everybody is, all the time. That is rare talent, anywhere in the NBA. And year after year, he is among the leaders in assists. Face it, he’s a bonafide floor general, without peer. A true leader. And on top of all that, he is a “picture of excellence”—unknown to and unbelieved by many. He is truly magic."

column1: Ever watch ""House"" on television? It's a reality show, where the title character is slightly impaired and occasionally is seen as human i.e. he may not be the know-it-all that doctors seem exude.

But, it is an excellent show because through it all, humanity triumphs. Well, that also explains the plight of our Chiefs. Through it all, they manage to somehow triumph. Last year, they actually made the back-door-playoffs.

They stumbled and fumbled but somehow they made it to the first round. . . but were immediately eliminated. This year, this same reality is going to set in. Just one glance at the schedule confirms this assertion. The Chiefs have to play both Super Bowl teams. . . Indianapolis and Chicago. Then, the Chiefs will have to play San Diego twice. That's four losses, already. Add in a split with Denver and an improved Oakland. Figure in five blown games, where the Chiefs fail to tackle; then throw a series of unexplainable interceptions; and then, cop penalties that no one can fathom. To top it off, Cincinnati and Tennessee will just flat out kick the Chiefs' butts. That means the Chiefs will only win five games. But, Houston is one of them. Dr. House, can you heal thyself?"

column1: Mary Lynn Clark said it best. This has been a relatively quiet Super Bowl. Not much said about “all” of it, because these two black coaches have broken through the “glass ceiling”.

Or is it because there just isn’t much to talk about? Well, let’s see if we can raise the “talk” bar. As you have noted, we predicted the “coaches” this week rather than the teams. That’s because this is moment in history where everyone wins. Either way, a black coach wins Super Bowl XLI. OK? And America wins. Because we can proudly say that a black coach has won and coached in a Super Bowl. And, last, Caucasian announcers will find it a bit awkward for either over-emphasizing or under-stating it. It will be interesting to see how they handle it.

But, back to Mary Lynn’s comment about the “quietness”. Now, from where I sit, every African American is talking about it, proudly. But the television sponsors are not. They ought to be talking about this like they talked about Elway, finally making it to his first Super Bowl. The way it appears, is that Manning will replace Big Bad John as the hype center piece. And that is an affront to all of us black people. This two-team black coaching thing is right up there with Jackie Robinson, breaking into base ball way back in the ’40s. And it should be on every media outlet in the country. Foreign countries ought to know that America has made good on some of its promises (not all of them, just some).

But, reality tells me that the giant media outlets will treat this rather “low-key”. Thus, I predict that very little media will be said about all of this on Sunday and the days leading up to it. And quite frankly, I mad about it. Whatever you do, don’t say that it doesn’t matter. Sunday may be the last time, for a long time, when we all can sing “We Shall Overcome” with meaning and purpose. After all, to me and to you, Super Bowl Sunday will be a sacred part of Black History Month (lest we forget).
Let all rejoice and be glad in it. Thanks, Mary Lynn for nudging us to speak up."

column1: Once again K.C. is at a fever pitch with visions of the Super Bowl “dancing in its head.”
Before you go off on the deep end, note well, in 1995 a team far less formidable than Denver came to town and left us crying with our 13-3 record.
My objective sense tells me that we will be crying again.
Emotionally, I will be in my seat behind the Chiefs bench cheering for the home team.
For those of you who will be crying that river, I strongly recommend that you go to any store other than Dillard’s to purchase your towels.
The Monday Nite Niters wish the entire community a felicitous New Year."

Yes, it's that time of the year. It's time to focus on the Chiefs and their upcoming search. Will the Chiefs go all the way to the Super Bowl this year, after promising for the last eight years? Don't forget the Chiefs won the AFC West Championship in 1997 and it was Denver who was the Wild Card entry. However, everyone knows that Denver beat the Chiefs 14-10 in the playoffs and went on to become World Champions, beating Green Bay in the Super Bowl.

But this is a new day and a new season. Nationwide, everyone says it will be the Chiefs and Packers in the Super Bowl. However, skeptical fans here in Kansas City still believe the Chiefs are ""right"" in the regular season...but real poor in the playoffs. The saying mostly is, ""Always the bridesmaid and never the bride.""

MONDAY NIGHT FOOTBALL takes the New England Patriots to Denver's Mile High Stadium. All the Monday Niters, except Ike and Walt, think that the Broncos will walk all over the Patriots. It's all on Channel 9 at 8 p.m. on October 6.

THE MIAMI DOLPHINS have beaten the Chiefs the last four times in regular season and all three times in postseason play. Last year, Miami beat the Chiefs, 10-9, at home. The deciding field goal was kicked by Pete Stoyanovich, now with the locals.

The Chargers have “one-up” on the Chiefs. They actually went to the Super Bowl in 1995 but lost to the Forty Niners. column1: **ABOUT THE SEAHAWKS:** Seattle is 3-1 so far in this young season. They beat Arizona, Washington and Philadelphia before losing last week to Pittsburgh 13-10. Ageless 41-year-old Warren Moon has thrown for 674 yards and seven touchdowns. His rifle-like arm is still amazing fans everywhere. Joey Galloway and James McKinght are his favorite receivers. Ricky Watters, onetime San Francisco 49er, is now helping round out the potent offense for the Seahawks. Tackle Cortez Kennedy and linebacker Chad Brown heads up a strong defensive corps. Bottom line -- look for Seattle to come close or even beat the Chiefs.

**REMEMBER?:** Back in 1990, Warren Moon passed for 527 yards at Arrowhead. That day, he set an NFL passing record. Back in 1995, Tamarick Vanover returned two kick-offs against Seattle -- one for 99 yards on September 3 and another for 89 yards on Christmas Eve.

**GRBAC TO START:** Well, Elvis is back. Backup Gannon has completed 34 of 61 passes for 352 yards. People in the barber shops are saying Gannon should be the starter. Derrick Parker at Seven Oaks thinks so. So do I. But, Marty doesn't and so Sunday, we'll see Elvis again.

**WORD ON GENERATION RAP:** Carl Boyd will host another talk session on community and educational issues this Saturday at 8 a.m. on the HOT 103 JAMZ. As usual, a vocabulary segment of the program will feature the word **synergy**."

# Chiefs Offense Not Good Enough To Think Super Bowl

Two weeks ago, the Monday Niters gave you an early indication of how the Kansas City Chiefs will do. And, we also reminded you that the Chiefs won the AFC West Championship, even though the Denver Broncos won the Super Bowl over Green Bay. As promised, this week we'll look at the offense and evaluate all the unspent potential as they began the pre-season in Tokyo last Saturday. The Chiefs lost to Green Bay 27-24.

**QUARTERBACK:** Elvis is back for the second full season and he should feel secure as the number one signal caller. That's because Rich Gannon is the backup again, in spite of his 5-1 record last year (Grbac was healing from a broken collarbone). While Grbac is no John Elway, he will guide the Chiefs ""Midwest Coast"" offense. Rating: Average

**RECEIVERS:** Andre Rison, the Spiderman, is back after a 1,000 yard receiving season in 1997. And, everyone knows he made Grbac look awfully good, especially with his third-down, over-the-shoulder catches. What's more, he has plenty of help from tight end Tony Gonzalez and newly acquired Derrick Alexander. If Elvis Grbac can throw it, these guys can catch it. Rating: Excellent.

**OFFENSIVE LINEMEN:** 1998 first-round draft choice Victor Riley will be on the bench. That's because Center Tim Grunhard, guards Will Shields and Dave Szott are still in place here, still stabilizing the most improved part of the 1998 Chiefs. Tackles may be weak on the pass rush out but overall the Chiefs line is as good as any. Rating: Above average.

**RUNNING BACKS:** Medicare Marcus Allen is gone, leaving behind several NFL endurance records. He won't be missed because Kimble Anders is the best of the Committee backs. He catches, he runs, and is rarely injured. Besides Kimble, no one has a for sure starting position. MIZZOU fans would like to see James Blackwell make the team. Rashaan Shehee, this year's draft choice, is still untested and unproven. We will all have to wait to see what happens. Don't look for much. Rating: Average at best.

**FIELD GOAL KICKERS:** Pete Stoyanovich, the Miami transplant, is making us all forget Nick Lowery. He was the NFL's most accurate and no one will forget that long field goal that beat Denver. Rating: Very Good.

**KICK-OFF AND PUNTER RETURNERS:** Even though someone said Tamarick Vanover will be used as a running back, don't believe it. He'll be running back kickoffs and giving the Chiefs' offense good field position. Most important, he's an exciting runner who needs more space than a regular backfield can give him. He's the real prime time. Rating: Good.

**OVERALL OFFENSE:** Because of the quarterback situation and a cast of unproven running backs, the Chiefs cannot even think about a Super Bowl appearance down in Miami. The truth is they must get better as the season progresses. Even though it's the same offense that won 13 games, they still need some help. The Monday Niters believe the Chiefs' chances all lie with the Chief's great defense. And, next week we'll look at that defense.

**ON SUNDAY'S GAME** -- First off, the Las Vegas oddsmakers have made San Francisco five-point favorites over our luck-filled Kansas City Chiefs. Even with a lowkey offense, a case full of untimely penalties and a committee of ineffective running backs, the Chiefs have surprised everyone with its 9-3 record and second place AFC West standing.

Kansas City has only lost to Denver, Miami, and Jacksonville, three playoff-bound teams. But, it is in the wins that make us all shudder with guarded praise. When they win, they win with dubious excitement, in overtime, in the last seconds, or with an interception and a special team's miracle. Point is there is no offense. And, we can't win much more by defense alone. Everyone knows that the Thomas-led defense holds all opponents to scoreless second halves. Has the luck really run out? Donna Stewart thinks not.

We'll see this Sunday out at Arrowhead, when our local Chiefs will match the league's third best defense against the San Francisco's Young and Rice-less offense. The 49ers have won eleven straight games after losing the game's best wide receiver, Jerry Rice. Simply put, the Chiefs can't win on Sunday because they are not stable enough to consistently beat good teams.

**MONDAY NIGHT FOOTBALL, DECEMBER 1, KMBC-CHANNEL 9** -- Everyone in the group thinks that the Green Bay Packers will defeat its NFC Central rival, the Minnesota Vikings. Fresh from beating up on the Dallas Cowboys, the Packers are riding high and are making a bid for a repeat opportunity in San Diego's Superbowl in January. Harry Sypert, longtime Chiefs' hater, thinks that Minnesota will surprise the green and gold. Ike Gardner keeps talking about the book just published by Dennis Green, head coach at Minnesota. Green takes a shot at the management in the book and ol' Ike thinks that this will be the last season for Green in the snowlands. I believe Harry Sypert is right."

As we have done faithfully for over 30 years, the Monday Nite Footballers sat down at Carl Clark's house for a regal ""Marylyn meal."" While there were many positive comments about the royally-presented meal, everything said about the Chiefs was negative and critical. Over and over, the word was about how the Chiefs were embarrassingly defeated by an inferior team, the San Diego Chargers. Miraculously, the Chiefs actually beat the Chargers...first down, passing yardage, rushing yardage, etc. But somehow the Chiefs became penalty prone, and with Grbac throwing four interceptions, the game slipped slowly into the hands of the Chargers. What's the problem? Simply put, it's the woefully poor performance of our quarterback, Elvis Grbac. While we can forgive Grbac for his interceptions, calling a time-out with only a minute or so into the third quarter is just plain unforgivable. The boy's lack of clock management skills ultimately was key to the Chief's loss.

So, what do we do? Clark and Dr. Charles Allen say ""dump Grbac."" More over, his lack of consistency overshadows the emerging fine play of Rashann Shehee, replacement for the injured Kimble ""the no longer nimble"" Anders. All of these reasons are significant enough to demote the king. However so, Grbac will remain number one.

And with that in mind, it is difficult for some of us to even consider the Chiefs as winners on Sunday. The New England Patriots will be ready to take advantage of our dwindling self esteem and failing offense. And Ol' Ike Gardner has Ed West and Paula Booker predicting against the Chiefs. Only Allen, Clark and Lloyd Greenfield think the Chiefs have a chance. Specifically, Clark alertly believes the 78,000 fans in the stadium will make a difference in the outcome. He's right because the Chiefs have managed to win both home games over Denver and Detroit. Whatever the prediction, it is getting tougher and tougher to support the Chiefs and their uncertain and unpredictable displays. But, I think, the Chiefs will slide another one in the win column and become 3-2 and hold a candle to Gannon's Raiders. Here's what we think:

**SUPER BOWL SUNDAY**, an undeclared national holiday: More friends will gather on this day than on any other Sunday of the year. There’ll be big screens and big fun. Some will decorate, some will just watch the game. But most will watch all the new commercials being released for the first time. A 30-second spot will cost $1.6 million. Mastercard, Pepsi and Oldsmobile will be among the 25 major companies showing off their new creations. Look for Anheuser-Bush updates on the lizards and the famous, field-goal kicking Clydesdale horses.

**ABOUT ATLANTA**: This is the first Super Bowl appearance for the ""dirty birds"" of Atlanta. The Falcons won the NFC West after a 14-2 record for the 1998 season. But, it's the fourth trip for Head Coach Ed Reeves to the Super Bowl. As you know, the Atlanta Falcons were a 3-13 in 1996 and won the next 22 out of 26 in 1997 and 1998. The main ""dirty bird"" is running back Jamal Anderson, who set a Falcon's rushing. Look for the Falcons to ""fly right"" over the Broncos in a close one.

**ABOUT DENVER**: Will it be Elway’s last game? Yes. Will it be Denver’s second consecutive Super Bowl victory? Probably not. But, Denver’s got it all…Terrell Davis at running...Shannon Sharpe at tight end...John ""Medicare"" Elway at quarterback...Smith and McCaffrey as the receivers...Smith and Atwater on defense. And, remember they beat the Green Bay Packers last year in Super Bowl XXXII. Denver will have their hands full on Sunday.

**SCHOLARSHIP MONEY**: Middle and high school students, listen to Carl Boy’d's ""Generation Rap"" each Saturday morning in February for a ""Word From Grand Dad."" If you write a poem about your ""Grand Dad,"" you could win schol-arships and prize money. Listen to Hot 103 JAMZ at 8 a.m. this Saturday for more details.
**MONIKER FOR CHIEFS NEW HEAD COACH**: ""Gunning Gunther,"" says Mon-day Niter Lloyd Greenfield."

# Monday Night Footballers Entertained in St. Louis

From check-in to check-out, the Kansas City Monday Nite football group and guests were entertained in sheer elegance by their St. Louis counterparts during their Governor’s Cup weekend. Each year the two groups exchange hospitality when the St. Louis Cardinal-Kansas City Chiefs Football game is played.

The “More in 84” theme was evident at every turn from Friday, August 10, thru Sunday, August 12. Except for the game itself, all events took place at the Executive Inn International Complex on I-70 at Lindebergh, near Lambert airport.
The week-end events, courtesies and gifts included:
1. A hospitality room in the seventh floor maxi-suite.
2. A Watson-smoked turkey, ham and crabmeat salad.
3. A mixed-fruit basket and a bottle of wine with a note saying “Welcome KC - More in 84”. (These were in the room).
5. A traveling trophy presented to the winning group. This year it will reside in St. Louis because of the Cardinal victory.
6. A swimming party, featuring intermittent “Lifestyle and Anatomy seminars.
7. A Polynesian feast in the 11th floor Polynesian World restaurant. Entrees included Teriyaki or New York steak and Waikiki dinner (shrimp, rice, sweet-sour pork, moo goo gai pan).
8. A personalized monogrammed bar plaque bearing the Kansas City Chiefs Arrowhead emblem.
9. A “St. Louis Victory” party in the maxi-suite, complete with food, drink and deejay-selected music - all until 5:15 a.m. Sunday.

Making the trip from Kansas City were Dr. Charles and Beverly Allen, Paul and Carolyn Booker, Carl and Marylynn Clark, “Big Ike” Gardner and Violetta Maxey, Ollie and Sandra Hubbard, James T. and Janice Nunnelly, Jerry Price and Ed West. Tom Brown and C. Ashley and guests were unable to attend."

It’s That time of year again when we sports fans start talking about our sometimes Kansas City Chiefs. What will they do this year? How many games will they win? Are they going to the stardom of the sixties and seventies?

We Monday Nitears, take the challenge to predict the “shaky” destiny of our last year’s last place Chiefs. Is this the year? Let’s rate the 1986 edition of the offense and defense. Afterwards, we’ll predict where the Chiefs will end up in December. Here goes.
Kicking game is very solid. Nick Lowery is the best. The Chiefs need a lot of work on punt and kickoff coverage, however.
Defensive Line: **Art Still** is strong and is one of the best in the entire NFL. **Ike Gardner** has always considered this area number one.
Linebackers: No comment at this time.
Secondary: Potentially the defensive backfield is one of the most explosive. **Kevin Ross, Deron Cherry, Albert Lewis** may be the “crown princes” of the NFL secondaries. Just plain awesome at times.

Offensive Line: **Ed West, Ollie Hubbard** and **Carl Clark** all agree that there is “hope” for the front line. For once, they did protect the quarterback. At various points, last Saturday, they did open holes. **Jozwiak, Lutz, Budde, Adickes** and newcomer **Eatman** will be tested; too new to say.

Receivers: There’s none better in the NFL—**Carson, Paige, Hancock** and **Marshall** represent the “alpha and the omega” in pro football. We rate this crew as ""unstoppable”; there’s none better. If **Todd Blackledge** can get them the ball, they can do the rest.

Running Backs: Here we had mixed feelings. **Ollie Hubbard** says **Ethan Hor-** ton is not the power threat we need. **Ed West** feels that the “Purdue Pair” of **Pruitt and Green** are refreshing and promising. We’re for ever looking the shaky and the coaches shaking. We’re forever looking for running backs and it never stops. Too shaky to say.

Quarterbacks: All of us are still a bit shocked at replacing **Bill Kenney** with **Blackledge**. Most of us fans believe that with stronger line protection, either could do well. **Blackledge** will make a difference, depending on his ability to connect with the best receivers in football, whereas this area is still questionable.

Here’s where **Ollie Hubbard, Ed West, Carl Clark** and **Tom Brown** all agree: No Super Bowl for the Chiefs, winning 500 or less (at best). All feel the Chiefs will place third in the Western Division, behind L.A. Raiders (Oakland) and the Denver Broncos. It will be the defense that will get them that far.
Except for the receivers, the rest of the offense is either “too new” or “too questionable” to predict.

As always, hot or cold, we’ll be there in Arrowhead (especially Ike and Carl) on a someone’s house each Sunday afternoon from September to December. Hollering, screaming, argu- ing—just like we’ve been doing for over 20 years. Yes, it’s that time of year. And, we’re glad about it.
Members of the Monday Nitears are: **Charles Allen, Paul D.S., C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner, Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnally, Jerry Price** and **Ed West**.

# Larry Holmes: He Knocked Em Out When He Said...

While Monday Niters don't claim to know about everything in sports; however, we do represent the over-30, long-time, innercity black sports fans. What those fans think, coupled with a little history, always makes for solid commentary. But, last Saturday night, all Kansas City was shocked to see the SpinksHolmes rematch reduced to a joke. And that's our feeling too. It was a joke.
These same fans commented in a disbelieving sort of way; ""Spinks didn't do anything."" ""They (the judges) took the fight from Holmes."" ""Ain't no way."" ""Holmes made folks mad and that's all...""
They were shocked that Spinks was declared the winner. Let's look backward for a moment before commenting further.

Going back to an earlier moment, Holmes said he wanted to fight Spinks again to regain ""his respect"". Certainly it was not for money, for Larry Holmes can buy Libya and a few other countries. He admits that everything he says is turned around and he ends up having to apologize. Well, that respect was returned to Larry Holmes Saturday night. But, not as the winner. His respect came in another way.

Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly, Ed West, Ollie Hubbard's father, Sandra Inez Hubbard and Janice Nunnelly all thought Holmes had won the fight. It was not surprising that it was a split decision—but not for Spinks.

The way we see it Holmes won two thirds of the fight—the earlier and middle five rounds and Spinks won the last five. Even Sugar Ray Leonard couldn't even agree with the unexpected decision. Only Judge Joe Cortez had the guts to render a reasonable decision in favor of Holmes. Well! Well! What now?

Well, no doubt Holmes is through in the ring. He even states ""I can't win no more,"" not only in the ring but in the role as a champ. For some odd, unknown reason Larry Holmes was never accepted as a champ. He possessed neither the humility of a Floyd Patterson nor the charisma of a Muhammed Ali.

They just didn't let Larry Holmes be. He fought winners and he won. Now he leaves the profession winning 48 and losing only two—both times to Spinks. And maybe really only losing once. We clearly believe Holmes won Saturday night. But the question is—did he get his respect back and just how?

In the usual post fight interview, Larry Holmes calmly admitted, ""I got a big mouth"" referring to his many comments about the judges, Rocky Marciano and others for which he eventually had to apologize. But, the more he talked in that interview, the more he stopped apologizing. Finally, he got his respect back.

He knocked them out when he told the judges, the promoters, the press and all his critics, ""You Can Kiss Where The Sun Don't Shine"". Larry Holmes knew he had won—Holmes gained the respect of sports fans everywhere.
Members of the Monday Nite Footballers are: Charles Allen, D.D.S., C. Artis Ahsley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly, Jerry Price and Ed West."

ENTITIES: Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly, Charles Allen, C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Ike Gardner Jr., Jerry Price, Ed West, Kansas City, Lincoln university, Bingham Junior high, Frances Willard, Hough-Mifflin method, AAA
# The Outside And Inside Of Ollie Hubbard
Ever been around 18th Street? Or 28th and Wabash? Lincoln university? Bingham Junior high? Or Frances Willard elementary school? If you have, then you have to know Ollie Hubbard.
Friendly, vivacious and always wearing a Santa Claus smile. His countenance suggest he's running for county office. Yes, that's ""Big Ollie""—all 270 pounds of him.

Except for a few very close friends, like Monday Nite Footballers, few would know that Big Ollie carries inside his head, deep-seated views on the issues surrounding his profession. For 20 years, Ollie has been an educator in the Kansas City, Mo., school district. And, he has cleverly blended his love for his profession.

First, Ollie is quite concerned about all grade point requirements being placed on athletes. He strongly feels this approach is sound and just. He's constantly telling his young, sixth graders that ""you must learn the curriculum to the fullest"".

Further, he is always telling those same elementary school athletes that sports is secondary to a good education. And, with today's classroom requirements for athletes, Ollie accepts his role as elementary teacher and strives to prepare the young athlete to work hard in the classroom. And, he does it, feeling inside that he could influence all sports-minded, elementary students at an age where it would be easier to emphasize the classroom.
Is this the Ollie we know? Let's look further inside and see how Ollie feels about other things. What about reading and test scores.

A little nervous, but emphatic, he says the District is improving the students' reading skills. He likes the Hough-Mifflin method, for he knows that reading is to the mind what exercise is to the body. And he smiles proudly when talking about how the test scores have steadily improved over the years. But, he changes that smile and says bluntly, ""we can't let up"".
From there, he goes on and on about the school levy and tells how it can affect the teaching of young, innercity children.

One, he really wants the District to regain its AAA, top rating. He knows that the teachers and the students are working harder than ever ""to keep improving"" and that the levy must pass, for the children need it now more than ever.

Now after 30 minutes of his inside, Big Ollie returns to his usual jovial self. His athletic frame sighs back to normal. His friendly, vivacious, Santa-Claus smile reappears. And he starts again, talking about sports and the upcoming football season. His outside regains itself.

Yes, he's more normal now. But, we all know that this little trip inside Ollie's world helps us to know there are dedicated teachers, who truly believe that all children must learn, regardless of their athletic ability.
Ollie happens to be one that knows the ""inside"" of both worlds and that we must start young if we expect athletes to perform well in the classroom—in high school and in college.
We truly do salute your efforts, Ollie. For we know, with your inside input, the young athlete will know that their performance inside the classroom is just as important as outside on the field.
Members of the Monday Niters are Charles Allen, D.D.S., C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Ike Gardner Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly, Jerry Price and Ed West."

# A Pleasant Illusion Is Better Than A Harsh Reality
**Editor's Note: The fol lowing column takes a look back in Chiefs' history with quarterback Len Dawson when the team won a wild card berth in the NFL playoffs and went on to win the Super Bowl.**
Sure glad we did it. We came back. And we showed the world that we're as good as any in the National Football League. Number One.
That's a guy named Len ny talking. It's about 6:00 p.m. on January 11. in New Orleans. After the Big Game.
And just think they paid fifteen bucks to see us. And at Tulane Stadium. How much will the share be? Doesn't matter, we got a big ring coming.

And, boy did the offense click. We went right over number eighty eight, Mr. Eller. And Mr. Page, we took him, too. So now! What do you think, you Minnesota Vikings? We beat you. We beat you. We beat you.
Sure glad that gambling thing didn't get in my way. And a week before the big gest game in my life. Super Bowl four. Just think, I was selected Most Valuable Player.

And the best part about it, I'm now up there with other Super Bowl MVP's. There was Bart Starr in Super Bowl I and II. And Joe Namath in number III. Ain't that something. Till now, all MVP's have been quarterbacks. And I'm up there with them.
And we beat Oakland to get here 17-7. Holmes, Mar sails, Buck Buchanan, Cur ley Culp.
23-7. Can you believe it! Can you believe it!
And wow! Did that game plan work? That Stram. Ain't he something? Short, play-action passes. And run right at 'em. You Vikings, you were just out classed today.
Yes, me - the MVP. Su per Bowl IV.

Probably should have been Otis or Mike Garrett. That was the smoothest five yard touchdown I've ever seen. And, that catch and run by Otis Taylor. 46 yards. That broke it open. Yes, that one broke the Vikings' back. Yes. Yes. That one was the one. I can still see that little kick as he crossed the goal line. And, that guy named Ralph Brown from Kansas City knows it. He's here today.
Hey, wait a minute, I almost said it. Otis should have been the MVP. Wo wie!
I'll think about that, tomorrow, January 12, 1970 or maybe, I'll think about it years from now. Maybe in 1986. It'll sure be a while before we'll be back here. Or anywhere near the playoffs.
Let's enjoy it. Enjoy, en joy........
And the harsh reality is right now. Will the Chiefs make it to the playoffs again as a wild card team? And then go on to win the Super Bowl twenty one? Maybe? Probably, not.

## *Who Will Still Be Playing On December 22?*
After celebrating with Sandra Hubbard on her birthday the previous weekend, the Monday Niters began to talk about the playoffs. Who will be playing for the Super Bowl? That was the question.

Well, the discussion bounced around amid the between quarter naps. First, they talked about Miami Hurricanes and their poor schedule. But, all agreed that Vinny Testaverde was a shoo-in for the Heisman Trophy. Then they talked about Nebraska's big win over Kansas. But, everybody but Ollie Hubbard agreed that Oklahoma would beat the Cornhuskers this weekend.

And, then they talked about the Chiefs playoff chances. But, only Carl Clark and Jerry Price felt the Chiefs would win the AFC West. And Charles Allen believed the Chiefs could take one of the two wild card spots. Who will it be on Dec. 22? Which team will try for the coveted Super Bowl rings?

ENTITIES: Deron Cherry, Chiefs, Jim Nunnelly, Bob Grupp, Gary Barbaro, Todd Blackledge, LA Raiders, Steelers, Walt Livingston, Tom Brown, Ed West, Rutgers

Certainly the Chiefs have taken their ups and downs this year. With a coach in his ""year of challenge"" and a seemingly easy sched-ule, the Chiefs have won the big ones and lost to the little ones. And, way back in 1981, Deron Leigh Cherry was kind of an up and down too. But before we elaborate, let's look at Deron's football career.
Born and raised in Pal-myra, N.J., Cherry was signed by the Chiefs as a punter in 1981. Beaten out by Bob Grupp, Deron switched to the secondary and played behind All Pro Gary Barbaro.
1982 didn't bring much. He didn't make one inter-ception. Not one. But, he stayed on as the fifth back, especially on third down passing situations. Key phrase—no interceptions. Wait until next year.

Yes, the 1983 season start-ed the upswing for Deron. Amid all the hype about first round pick Todd Black-ledge, Deron made All Pro—at safety. He inter-cepted seven passes for 100 yards. And, the All Pro status seemed to fit. It sounded good. Deron—All Pro. Deron—All Pro.

For the next three years, Deron would wear that re-vered title, even in spite of the Chiefs 8-8 and 6-10 seasons. 1984 and 1985 were still banner years for Deron. Further, he became one of the Chiefs most con-sistent tacklers. Now, to-day.

After fifteen games this season, Deron has im-proved over his previous All Pro years. Monday Ni-ter Ed West says Deron's record speaks for itself. Ike Gardner adds that in a large measure, Deron is responsible for the Chiefs' playoff hopes and Carl just says ""he does his job"". Let's look.

A league leading nine interceptions, two in the key game against the LA Raiders. That's 30 in a four-year period, not counting what he is likely to do in Sunday's game against the Steelers. And his durability is unquestionable, having started every game during the past four seasons. And like Walt Livingston, Carl Clark, and Tom Brown pre-dicted, the players, on Wednesday, voted Deron All Pro again, for the fourth consecutive year. Not bad. Not bad.

No, it's really pretty good. For you see, Deron was signed on as a free agent punter in 1981, from an eastern school called Rutgers. And, as the Chiefs have often made many mis-takes this year, they made a more dramatic one in 1981. They actually re-leased Deron during the final cutdown, just before the beginning of that sea-son. Can you imagine they actually cut an All Pro ""in the rough"". True, they did.
But, after a few key in-juries, Deron was reac-tivated a few games later. But, you really do have to wonder what went on in the Chiefs' minds—to release a potential four-year All Pro.

Thanks, Deron—for your good season. More impor-tant, thanks for staying around Kansas City, es-pecially when it appeared you were not needed. But, a really big thanks for being relentless and hanging in there and waiting for your ""down"" to come ""up"". We salute you."

ENTITIES: Ike Gardner, Lloyd Greenfield, Ollie Hubbard, Denver Broncos, Kansas City Chiefs, SWAC, MEAC, Alabama State, Bethune-Cookman, Tennessee State

The Denver Broncos’ 24-20 upset victory over the Kansas City Chiefs this past Sunday took its toll on the Monday Niters with nine out of ten expecting the local guys to win. Only Ike Gardner was out on a limb with the right pick.

Earlier in the season, Lloyd Greenfield did not submit predictions a couple of times, but he's still in first place with a 5-2 mark. Gardner’s pick in a very tight field lifted him from eighth to second, and his accuracy on his six correct picks is phenomenal! Gardner is off by only a season total of 12 points. Closest to Gardner is Ollie Hubbard’s 6-4 record with a point difference of 66 points. Five more weeks of the regular season remain.

# Monday Nite Footballers And Their Predictions

The Sunday is a bye for the Kansas City Chiefs and the Monday Nite Footballers have the week off from their predictions. This is an opportunity to update their individual success with the first Predictor's Scoreboard of the 1993 season. Ike Gardner is off to a 2-0 start in hopes of defending his 1992 season title. Last year Gardner finished with a 10-5 record and 89 points edging out Ollie Hubbard who finished 9-6 with 73 points.

Though the Monday Niters have been featured in **THE CALL** for years, the scoreboard premiered with the 1991 season. Lloyd Greenfield took the inaugural honors compiling an 8-5 mark and 72 points. Gardner was second that season with 9-7 and a very impressive low point total of 29 points.

The record is based on picking the winning team and the point total is the sum of the difference in points between the predictor's ""win by"" and the actual margin of victory. The point differences are calculated only on correct selections.
Seven of the group are 1-1 with only one prediction by Harry Sypert and none so far by Walt Livingston. The Monday Niters did not participate in the Tampa Bay season opener.

""Hold the Raiders to ten points or less""

column1: 1. Marcus Allen played eleven years and 144 games with the Los Angeles Raiders, being selected in the first round draft of 1981 out of University of Southern California. During that period, he scored 98 touchdowns — 79 rushing, 18 receiving and 1 by a fumble recovery. In 1984, in his second season, he was selected most valuable player in the NFL, after gaining 2314 combined rushing and receiving yards. That record still stands today. Marcus was also MVP in Super Bowl eighteen.
2. Joe Montana, as a San Francisco starter, has only one victory out of three tries against the Raiders. About 78,000 fans are expected on Sunday to see him even his record.
3. At lane college, Jefferson Joe Gilliam, former Pittsburg Steeler quaterback, was a classmate of Monday Nite's Dr. Charles Allen.

4. Carl Clark thinks Houston quaterback Warren Moon will quit after this year. Carl is a newly-installed board member of Newhouse, a shelter for battered women funded by Jackson County's Anti-Drug Sales Tax.
5. Charles Ward, Florida State quaterback is also the starting guard on the basketball team.
6. The group saluted the class of 1958 (Lincoln, Central, Westport, Manual, Hogan, and De La Salle) who celebrated a class reunion at the Hilton Plaza. About 75 classmates attended this past weekend.

7. Urban League president Bill Clark and Dr. Stacey Daniels Director of Evaluation at the Kauffman Foundation are part of the task force to evaluate the goals and objectives of the Anti-Drug Sales Tax programs. A public hearing on the tax will be held on Oct. 5 at 5:30 p.m. at the Jackson County Courthouse in Independence. The group, which has been working all summer, is chaired by William Session, private attorney.

ENTITIES: Kansas City Chiefs, Green Bay Packers, Jim Nunnelly, Greg Hill, Marcus Allen, Super Bowl, Lamar Hunt
**Kansas City Chiefs vs. Green Bay Packers**

1. Greg Hill struts his stuff! During Sunday's game against the Vikings, running back Greg Hill produced his best day in his NFL career. Hill carried the ball 15 times for 100 yards. More importantly, out of that yardage came two rushing touch downs, his first and second of the season.

2. Marvelous Marcus strikes again! Marcus Allen flipped his way into the NFL history books last Sunday against the Vikings. At the end of the second quarter, Allen made his 110th rushing touch down to tie Walter Payton for the all time rushing touch down record. One more rushing TD and Allen is all alone on top. K.C. fans say, “Just one more baby, just one more!”
3. Drug testing at the Family Court reveals that youths are likely to use marijuana. While crack may be the most dangerous drug around, “pot” is used more.

4. Remember Super Bowl I? Representing themselves and the American Football League in the first championship match with the National Football League, the Chiefs played well for one half before bowing to the supremacy of the Packers at the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum on January 15, 1967. The contest was billed as the AFL-NFL Championship game. It would be a couple of years before Chiefs founder Lamar Hunt would actually put the name “Super Bowl” on pro football’s premier game: the name came from his children’s toy “Super Ball.” The game would feature 13 Pro Football Hall of Famers: Hunt, LB Bobby Bell, DT Buck Buchanan and QB Len Dawson for the Chiefs, and Packer head coach Vince Lombardi, RB Jim Taylor, T Forrest Greg, QB Bart Starr, LB Ray Nitschke, DB Herb Adderley, DE Willie Davis, S Willie Wood and DT Henry Jordan for the Packers.

5. Only one of Green Bay Packers Reggie White’s NFL record 162 career sacks have come against the Chiefs, partly because he has played against the team just twice before. Sunday’s game is only the seventh time during the regular season these two teams have met.

6. Neil Smith has faced the Packers three times and has had 13 tackles, three sacks, two forced fumbles and a fumble recovery. Meanwhile, Derrick Thomas has had 10 stops, three sacks and two forced fumbles in his three encounters with the Pack."

column4:
Chiefs play Santa Claus
Better than football

column1: The Monday Nite Footballers were thoroughly disgusted with still another humiliating performance on national television by the Chiefs. Even though Marty is the winningest coach in the last decade many said he needs to “get out of Dodge.”

Jason Whitlock ( new minority sports columnist for The Star) tends to be candid and accurate. Some of the Monday Niteers suggested that many of the Chiefs problems would be resolved if several of the Chiefs stayed away from a popular night spot on Highway 350, especially on Tuesday nights ( fish night as I understand it) and the week-ends. Many of the Chiefs’ fans enjoyed viewing all home games in their homes this year. This writer predicts that the same luxury ( or pain) will not be afforded next year due to the fact that our glorious Chiefs are nothing but a group of mediocre players and a couple of stellar “has beens.”"

Kansas City Chiefs vs. Indianapolis Colts

column5: With a replacement QB, no offense!
K.C. has to be high style, agile and mobile!

column1: 1. The Chiefs lead the series with the Indianapolis Colts 6-4, but the teams have not met in a regular season game since 1990. The two clubs did meet last January in a play-off game at Arrowhead stadium in which the Colts won 10-7.

2. Children from homes, where the parents do not supervise or have expectations of the child finishing high school, are more likely NOT to finish the 10th grade. Students who expect to finish no more than high school are seven times more likely NOT to make it past the 10th grade. (American Research Journal, 1995). Message: Expect children to finish college and maybe finishing high school can be achieved.

3. The Chiefs need one victory to clinch a wild card play-off spot. Two victories might secure the Chiefs hosting a play-off game at Arrowhead. A play-off spot this year would be the seventh consecutive year that the Chiefs have made it to the play-offs, the longest streak in the NFL.

4. Last Sunday, Bethel Missionary Baptist church had 182 persons who attended Sunday School and saw two skits, entitled ""Soldiers of the Cross"" and ""Cheerleader for Christ."" Compliments to Rev. Woods and his church family for demonstrating innovative ways to attract youth to church.

5. Colts running back Marshall Faulk has had seven rushing touchdowns this season, the most for the club. Quarterback Jim Harbaugh and running back Lamont Warren have also had one rushing touchdown each. So far this season, the Colts have had nine rush touchdowns and 12 passing touchdowns. The opponents combined totals are 11 rushing touchdowns and 20 passing touchdowns.

1. Andre Risen, Falcon wide receiver, is the leading receiver in the NFL with 26 catches, 316 yards and four touchdowns.
2. Safety Kevin Ross and tackle Irv Eatman are former Chiefs players, now with the Atlanta Falcons.
3. Joe Montana has completed 43 of 64 passes for 518 yards and four touchdowns so far this season. He has thrown no interceptions, which helps him rate as the fourth leading passer in the AFC.

4. Students who are chronically truant report over 20% higher incidence of using alcohol, tobacco and marijuana each month. More statistical information is available at the Jackson County Prosecutor’s Office, call COMBAT at 881-3555.
5. Willie Davis, Chiefs wide receiver, has caught at least one pass in 34 straight games. He ranks eighth in the AFC with 11 catches and 168 yards this season.

# Post Season Advice To The Chiefs:

It seems only yesterday when everyone in Kansas City was talking about the Chiefs. Coming off a 13-3 record in 1995, and feeling like we were just a step away from the New Orleans Bowl, we bought up all the red sweat shirts, missed church on Sundays and bragged about our Chiefs, as if we had become the mecca for the National Football League.

And, then it was here...the 1996 season. And, bam...off to a 4-0 start and even after eleven games, we had managed to win eight...some in the last minute and a couple in overtime. But, we were winning, and New Orleans was still in our radar.

And after that, things went downhill. Even the ever-venerable defensive led by Neil and Derrick and Tracy, began to crumble. Players began to talk openly. Some even accused the marquee players of being more interested in lucrative commercials, business interests, and good times rather than playing football. What used to be Super Bowl pursuit became a barely audible cry for the playoffs. You know, we didn't even make that, losing to Buffalo.

What is the answer and what advice should we tell the Chiefs as they prepare for San Francisco, Carolina, Jacksonville, St. Louis, and Miami in 1997? Not to speak about Denver, San Diego and maybe a new improved Seattle.
**QUARTERBACK:** Jeff George is available. Take him. It's better than nothing (Bono). We now know we can't make the playoffs without an effective quarterback.
**DEFENSIVE END:** Neil Smith is about to go on Medicare. The 1996 season was his worst and it signals a coming decline. For once, the Chiefs should get ahead of the parade rather than behind it.

**RUNNING BACK:** Congratulations to management for announcing that Greg Hill will replace Medicare Marcus. Ike Gardner questions the wisdom of doing this so early mainly because it may dampen the preseason competition. Maybe you're right Ike. But, at least, we made a move.
**OFFENSIVE TACKLE:** John Alt should join the NASA space team. The Chiefs offensive line is falling down.

**RECEIVERS:** We let Willie Davis go to Houston and we've been struggling ever since. The early season injury to Lake Dawson didn't help matters. Chris Peen tried. LaChapelle is overrated. So, our advice, get two top receivers from the draft or the free agency pool.

**DEFENSIVE SECONDARY:** Hold the line here. Help Mark Collins get over his mad spell. Continue to work with Hasty, send Stargell home. And, start looking real seriously at our linebackers. May need some new faces here.

**COACHING AND MANAGEMENT:** Well, Carl Peterson has been here eight years and he has put the hope back into Kansas City. He hired Marty, prayed for Joe Montana, and sold out Arrowhead week after week. But for some reason, there has been no announcement that he has been rehired. Obviously, it's Carl's call. He can go anywhere he wants. After all, he did turn the franchise around. Owners, fans, and the front office staff are happy. We'll see. Come on Lamar, tell us...is he in or out?

Well, that brings us to hard-headed Marty. While Marty cannot be blamed for the sub-mediocre performance of his players. He can, however, get them out of the game before they hurt the chances of winning. He can revise the whole play-calling process. And he can stop telling us we're ""Super Bowl ready"" when obviously we're not. Clark, Booker, Ed West and Walt Livingston have doubts about Ol' Marty. But, consensus says Marty will stay and realistically address some of the concerns the fans have raised.

**FINAL COMMENTARY:** Most of us in the Monday Nite Footballers have watched a dozen or so coaches promise the rain and deliver raindrops. We've seen the defense carry the offense. We've seen a packed and empty Arrowhead. All of this leads us to why we are fans. We love these Chiefs like parents love their children. Very unconditional. Good or bad. Under or over. Behind or in front. We are going to be there for these Kansas City Chiefs. Are we going to stop talking about them? NO. Are we going to remind them of the failed Super Bowl return promise?
**YES.** Are we going to sit in subzero weather next year to see a possible promise turn sour. YES! YES! YES!

And now, one final piece of advice. Since we're still trying to get to the Super Bowl, it is possible we need to take a look at the two-year expansion teams, Jacksonville and Carolina. They both seem to have the ingredients to expedite and implement a sound plan...maybe Kansas City ought to become an expansion team.
Then everybody, including the players, would be happy!"

ENTITIES: Jim Nunnelly, Todd Blackledge, Bill Kenney, John Mackovic, Kansas City Chiefs, Ed West, Ollie Hubbard, Tim Brown, Paul Booker, Mike Gardner, Lord Chesterfield, Charles Allen, D.D.S. C. Ashley, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner, Jerry Price

The Monday Niters just can't understand it. Why Todd Blackledge is the number one quarterback over ""seasoned and proven"" Bill Kenney. While we are known ""never to agree"" totally on anything, we got some good advice for John Mackovic, head coach of the Chiefs. Here goes.

After four pre-season and three regular season games, the Chiefs offense has not produced to the fans' expectations. But, before we get ""too deep"" into commentary, let's look back at Todd's past and then analyze the present problem (as we see it).

Now, Todd has a fine history. Born and reared in Canton, Ohio (home of Pro Football's Hall of Fame); son of a college football coach (his father is now offensive line coach for Pittsburg Steelers). High school and college All-American. First-round draft choice. 1983 Kansas City Chiefs (the worst according to Charles Allen). Though ""armed"" with a strong resume' and solid pro potential, Todd right now, should not be the start-ing quarterback. Why?
Let's listen in on the Mon-day Niters.
Ed West, former pro pros-pect, says Todd just ""can't read defenses"". And, he and Ollie Hubbard point out that the problem is not for ""lack of receivers"".
It's just that Todd is not having the season Bill Kenney had at the same time last year. That's what Tom Brown says. Just that sim-ple. But let's keep on.

Mostly, the real problem lies in the way things were handled. Before pre-season, Big Mac (Chiefs Head Coach, John Mackovic), announced that Todd Black-ledge was his number one quarterback before the first 1986 football was thrown. No competition, just BIRTHRIGHT. He didn't have to prove him-self. He just appointed Todd ""number one"". Let's go on.

In a heated discussion, Paul Booker asserts that Chiefs front office may have dictated that Black-ledge become number one. However so, or whatever the reasons, the Chiefs can't win with Blackledge at the helm, not right now, in the ""year of the chal-lenge"".

If one watched the Hous-ton win, one would know it was all defense. The of-fense, under Blackledge, just ""failed to thrive"". Six completions out of twenty three passes. Very unim-pressive. Ike Gardner put it best ""man can't live by bread alone and the Chiefs can't win by defense alone"".

Now, there it is. As long time fans, we deserve more than we're getting. And, choosing a quarterback is one example of a decision made earlier, that has to be corrected. And, we Mon-day Niters, with over 170 years of combined and or-ganized fan experience, just can't buy the whole Blackledge affair. Our ad-vice to John Mackovic is simple: give the Chiefs' offense a boost; restore Kenney to number one, put Blackledge on the bench.
But, in the words of Lord Chesterfield, ""advice is seldom welcome, and those who need it the most, like it the least"".
Members of the Monday Niters are: Charles Allen, D.D.S., C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner, Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nun-nelly, Jerry Price and Ed West."

column5: If Gannon plays. It's all up (to) Hill.

column1: **SPECIAL NOTE: These predictions were made Monday, Nov. 25. The ""records"" do not reflect the results of yesterday's game. An updated tally will be printed in next week's edition.**

EDITOR'S NOTE: The Monday Niters made their comments on Bono prior to the announcement of the quarterback change. The Monday Niters strictly want to see quarterback Steve Bono benched and replaced with Gannon. He's not the charter to prove that he can rise up from a backup at San Francisco to a starter here with the Chiefs. So far, he has yet to prove it.

It's not because he arose on the heels of the now-departed Joe Montana. It's not because he hasn't had sparks of brilliance along the way. In fact, sportscasters often remark of the Chiefs' winning percentage when Bono is quarterbacking. The reason we advocate the big change to Gannon is that Bono has yet to show that he has the ""right stuff"" to lead us to a Super Bowl somewhere in the future.

Marty Schottenheimer and Carl Peterson promised us a Super Bowl visit in five years. Well, we can see put our losses and readjust our goal by first replacing Bono. Some will say, that Bono is not the only reason we are in a weak second place in the AFC. We agree, but not the most glaring spot needing front office attention is quarterback Steve Bono.

Dr. Allen, Ollie Hubbard, Ike Gardner, Ed West, Lloyd Greenfield and certainly Harry Sypert--all have called for Bono to resign. They constantly are on the move that five column appearance especially now that each game is so critical to our doused Super Bowl hopes. Walt Livingston is now predicting a loss for the Chiefs whenever Bono is the starting quarterback. Don't they have us?

Well, that's our column for this week. As you look at our predictions remember they were made before yesterday's game against Detroit. Of course, we all like and support the Chiefs but we have been so disappointed with ""Hands-Off Bono"" policy. Carl Clark put it best when he said, ""Once a backup quarterback, always a backup quarterback.""
And this time, Carl is right.

“Get the babies off the offensive line”

column1: 1. Paul Booker feels the game on Monday will be a national contest between the Chiefs’ Joe Montana and Denver’s John Elway. In three starts, Montana has not beaten Denver in a regular season game. However, in Super Bowl January, 1990, San Francisco beat Denver 55-10.

2. The Chiefs have won their last three Monday night games at Arrowhead - twice over the LA Raiders and once over the Buffalo Bills. the next Monday night game at Arrowhead will be on November 8 against the Green Bay Packers.
3. The group recognized the running talents of Barry Words, former Chiefs running back, now at Minnesota.
4. The group heard about the fabulous Gates Barbecue in Las Vegas, Nev.

5. The Kansas City Police Department uses a German-trained, Labrador retriever to help them find illegal drugs. The animal, named Cole, was purchased with Jackson County Anti-Drug Tax dollars and has been considered effective in the fight against drugs.
6. The Monday Nite Footballers will be featured Monday night on KMBC-TV channel 9 on the tailgate show just before the 8:00 p.m. game at Arrowhead.

7. Hostess Patsy Brown prepared turkey wings in a bed of dressing, brisket, cheese and macaroni and collard greens for the group. Her dining room was sprinkled with a red, white, and gold streamer, marking the Kansas City Chiefs' colors."

While the project has concentrated on middle schools, Bass says discussion is underway to move the program to elementary schools as well. When children skip school, they are likely to engage in crime and drugs."

column1: (Image of football players on the bench)

**HEADING BACK TO THE BENCH** . . . Chiefs receiver Andre Rison ( No. 89) heads back to the bench with teammates after scoring a touchdown on a 2-yard pass from quarterback Elvis Grbac late in the third quarter in the Chiefs game against the Pittsburgh Steelers Monday night at Arrowhead. Rison's score tied the game at 13-13. However, the Steelers came back in the fourth quarter with a touchdown score on a 5-yard pass from quarterback Kordell Stewart to receiver Charles Johnson. The Steelers didn't look back, defeating the Chiefs 20-13. The Chiefs, with a 4-3 record in the AFC West will face the New York Jets this Sunday afternoon at Arrowhead. – CALL photo by Jerry Lockett

**ABOUT THE JETS** -- The New Yorkers have won the last four of five games. Former Tampa Bay quarterback Vinny Testaverde has guided the Jets to two consecutive victories, and his crisp passing to Keyshawn Johnson and Wayne Chrebet has made him the top-rated passer in the NFL. Vinny and the Jets will make a long afternoon for the Chiefs' defense. Classy running back, Curtis Martin, rounds out this up and coming Jets' offense. Linebacker Mo Lewis and cornerbacks Aaron Glenn and Otis Smith will shake-up the Chiefs shaky offense. While both teams are 4-3, the Jets have momentum on their side, given that the Chiefs have lost two consecutive games. This will be a close, close one and the Chiefs and the Chiefs could lose again at Arrowhead.

**OPERATION BYPASS FOR PARENTS - NOVEMBER 7:** On Saturday, Nov. 7, a workshop on how to obtain, maintain, and improve the quality of our schools will be held. Program planners say we must bypass all the negative and go right to the most important element in a child's education...the parents. Parents are encouraged to attend and learn about all the resources available to help you help your child. Tutoring, Substance abuse, and health insurance resources will be available. That's November 7, from 1 to 4 p.m., at Paseo Academy, 4747 Flora. A must event for all parents of public school children. Call Carl Boyd for more information at 363-5720.

**ABOUT THE CHIEFS:** The lackluster offense has always been a major concern for the Chiefs. But, now the once topnotch defensive and special team units, are beginning to fold a bit. Defensive End Chester McGlockton showed up well last Monday night. And receiver Derrick Alexander streaked past receivers on a perfect strike from his college teammate, Elvis Grbac. Grbac is struggling and, in our opinion, has become a quarterback full of unkept promises. Kimble Anders is definitely not going to the Pro Bowl this year. And, Derrick Thomas might not go either. Well then, who can rise up to beat the Jets? Joe Horn can and should return all kickoffs. Tamarrick Vanover can return punts only. Further, let's keep throwing the ball to Rison and Alexander. Give Bam Morris and rookie Rashad Shehee a chance. Obviously, the Chiefs are at a make or break point-in-the season. Let's all pray."
